scenario:
Basic science: fluid + electrolyte management

คำถาม: postoperative IV fluid + electrolyte management

choices.label: A
choices.text: Liberal IV fluid for all postop

choices.label: B
choices.text: Postoperative fluid management — goal-directed restrictive trend

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Postoperative fluid management — goal-directed restrictive trend:

(1) Fluid types:
  • Crystalloid: balanced solutions (Lactated Ringer's, Plasma-Lyte) preferred over 0.9% NSS for resuscitation (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis); 5% dextrose for hypoglycemia adjunct;
  • Colloid: albumin (5% for resuscitation in selected — septic shock per Surviving Sepsis 2021); HES (hydroxyethyl starch) no longer recommended (renal injury + mortality); gelatins;
  • Blood products for specific indications;

(2) Goal-directed fluid therapy (GDT):
  • Avoid both over-resuscitation (third spacing, edema, ileus, anastomotic, ARDS) AND under-resuscitation (organ hypoperfusion);
  • Dynamic measures of fluid responsiveness: passive leg raise (PLR), pulse pressure variation (PPV), stroke volume variation (SVV), POCUS;
  • CVP no longer recommended as fluid responsiveness predictor;
  • Targets: MAP > 65, lactate clearance, UO 0.5 mL/kg/hr (caution in over-resuscitating just to UO);

(3) ERAS principles for fluid:
  • Pre-op: no prolonged NPO (clear fluid up to 2h), carb loading 2h pre-op (improves insulin sensitivity);
  • Intra-op: balanced solutions, restrictive but adequate; avoid 'over-the-top' fluid;
  • Post-op: oral intake as soon as tolerated; minimize maintenance IV; goal euvolemia;

(4) RELIEF trial (Myles NEJM 2018) — restrictive (low) vs liberal (higher) fluid in major abdominal surgery: restrictive associated with more AKI; modern recommendation: moderate (not extreme either way) goal-directed;

(5) Electrolyte management postop:
  • Sodium: monitor; both hyponatremia (overhydration with hypotonic) + hypernatremia possible
  • Potassium: monitor; K losses from NG, diarrhea, diuretic, alkalosis; replete carefully (max 10 mEq/h peripheral, 20 mEq/h central with monitoring)
  • Magnesium: often low postop; replete (Mg essential for K + Ca regulation, neuromuscular, cardiac)
  • Calcium: hypocalcemia post-thyroid/parathyroid surgery; transfusion (citrate); replete
  • Phosphate: refeeding syndrome, depletion;

(6) Glycemic control: target 140-180 (avoid both extreme hyper + hypoglycemia); insulin sliding scale + correction; long-acting basal + bolus for outpatient regimens;

(7) Acid-base disorders postop:
  • Metabolic acidosis: lactate (perfusion), hyperchloremia (NSS over-use), ketone (NPO, DKA)
  • Metabolic alkalosis: NG suction, vomiting, diuretic
  • Respiratory: hypoventilation (opioid, pain, sedation), hyperventilation (anxiety, pain, sepsis);

(8) Special situations:
  • Cardiac surgery — pulmonary edema risk
  • Major liver resection — coagulopathy, hypoalbumin, ascites
  • Bariatric — dumping, dehydration
  • Sepsis — initial resuscitation 30 mL/kg crystalloid then goal-directed;

(9) Monitoring: weight daily (most accurate volume change), I/O, electrolytes daily or more, hemodynamics, end-organ perfusion (UO, lactate, mental status)
————————————————
Perioperative fluid + electrolyte management — fundamental + has evolved significantly. Major shift from 'liberal' to 'restrictive moderate' (goal-directed). Crystalloid selection:
  • Balanced (LR, Plasma-Lyte) preferred over 0.9% NaCl (normal saline) for resuscitation
  • NS large volumes → hyperchloremic acidosis, AKI risk (SMART, SALT-ED trials)
  • LR contains some K (4 mEq/L) — caution in hyperkalemia; safe in most
  • D5W for hypoglycemia/hypernatremia adjunct Colloid use:
  • Albumin — selected (e.g., large volume paracentesis, hepatorenal syndrome, septic shock per Surviving Sepsis; no general benefit over crystalloid per SAFE trial)
  • HES (hydroxyethyl starch) — NOT recommended (CHEST, 6S trials — increased renal injury + mortality)
  • Gelatins — limited use, expensive, no superior outcomes Goal-directed therapy: avoid both extremes; dynamic measures preferred over static (CVP, urine output alone). ERAS fluid principles:
  • No prolonged NPO (clear fluid up to 2h)
  • Carb loading 2h pre-op
  • Moderate intraoperative (balanced solutions)
  • Early oral intake postop
  • Minimize maintenance IV when oral OK Restrictive vs liberal — RELIEF trial (Myles NEJM 2018): restrictive fluid in major abdominal surgery associated with more AKI vs slightly liberal; modern consensus: moderate goal-directed (not extreme either way). Electrolyte considerations postop:
  • Na: monitor; hyponatremia from hypotonic fluid + SIADH common postop; hypernatremia from dehydration
  • K: NG losses, diuretic; replete carefully
  • Mg: low common; impacts K + Ca + cardiac; replete IV
  • Ca: post-thyroid/parathyroid; transfusion (citrate)
  • PO4: refeeding syndrome Refeeding syndrome: severely malnourished resuming feeds → PO4 + Mg + K drop into cells; cardiac + neuromuscular dysfunction; thiamine deficiency manifests. Prevent: gradual feed advancement + thiamine pre-feed + electrolyte monitoring + replacement. Acid-base: assess Anion Gap; common postop disorders: metabolic acidosis (lactic, hyperchloremic), metabolic alkalosis (NG, vomiting), respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation). Glycemic: target 140-180; hyperglycemia + hypoglycemia both bad; use insulin sliding scale + correction. Special: cardiac surgery (pulmonary edema risk; restrictive); liver resection (coagulopathy, ascites, hypoalbumin); bariatric (dumping, dehydration risk).
reference_source: ERAS Society Fluid Therapy; Myles PS et al. NEJM 2018 (RELIEF); Semler MW et al. NEJM 2018 (SMART); Surviving Sepsis 2021

  • Crystalloid: balanced solutions (Lactated Ringer's, Plasma-Lyte) preferred over 0.9% NSS for resuscitation (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis)

scenario:
Basic science: cardiac arrest + cardiopulmonary resuscitation in surgical patient

คำถาม: post-cardiac surgery cardiac arrest — special protocol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac Arrest after Cardiac Surgery (CALS) Protocol — modified ACLS

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Cardiac Arrest after Cardiac Surgery (CALS) Protocol — modified ACLS:

(1) Critical differences from standard ACLS:
  • Recent thoracotomy + sternotomy + intracardiac/great vessel anatomy
  • Resternotomy with internal cardiac massage if external CPR fails
  • Specific arrest etiologies — VF, tamponade, bleeding, ischemia, hypovolemia, pacemaker;

(2) Immediate response within 5 minutes (modified ACLS):
  • Confirm arrest
  • Call for help + arrest team + perfusionist (CPB on standby)
  • Begin chest compressions (caution with sternotomy site / wires)
  • Defibrillate VF/VT (3 shocks before drug if witnessed in monitored ICU)
  • Pacing if asystole / bradycardia + pacemaker available (post-op patients often have epicardial wires);

(3) Specific etiologic management:
  • Tamponade — open chest within 5 min if PEA / ineffective CPR (life-saving)
  • Hemorrhage — open chest, identify + control source, transfuse
  • Tension pneumothorax — decompress
  • Hypovolemia — fluid + blood
  • Hyperkalemia — Ca + insulin + glucose
  • MI / graft thrombosis — emergent intervention;

(4) Resternotomy:
  • Indications: VF/VT unresponsive 3 shocks, PEA without reversible cause, asystole, suspected tamponade
  • Within 5 min of arrest to maximize survival
  • Internal cardiac massage 100/min, defibrillation internal, identify cause
  • All ICUs caring for post-cardiac surgery patients should have resternotomy capability;

(5) Drugs in CALS:
  • Avoid epinephrine bolus initially (can cause severe HTN + intracardiac complications)
  • Use vasopressin / norepi infusion
  • Amiodarone, lidocaine for VF/VT;

(6) Extracorporeal life support (ECMO / ECLS):
  • VA-ECMO bridge for refractory
  • Cannulation femoral + peripheral or central
  • Improved outcomes with rapid deployment;

(7) Outcomes with CALS protocol — survival higher (50%+) than standard ACLS in cardiac surgery patients due to addressing surgical etiology + resternotomy;

(8) Training + drills — STS CALS course + simulation; ICU + OR teams should be familiar;

(9) Documentation + debrief: M&M, learnings, prevention; family communication
————————————————
Cardiac Arrest after Cardiac Surgery (CALS) — distinct protocol per Society of Thoracic Surgeons (STS) due to unique pathophysiology + therapeutic opportunities. Why different from standard ACLS:
  • Specific etiologies (tamponade 35%, hemorrhage 25%, VF/VT 25%, MI/graft, tension pneumothorax, hyperkalemia, pacemaker failure)
  • Recent thoracotomy + sternotomy enables internal cardiac massage + visualization
  • Standard chest compressions may injure surgical site
  • Epicardial pacing wires often available
  • Goal: identify + treat specific cause within 5 min to optimize outcome Key steps (STS CALS Protocol):

1. Within 1 min: confirm arrest, call for help (arrest team + perfusion)

2. VF/VT (witnessed monitored ICU): 3 shocks immediately, then chest compressions

3. Asystole / severe bradycardia: pace with epicardial wires if available

4. PEA: think of reversible causes (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, hypovolemia, hyperkalemia)

5. Resternotomy within 5 min if:
  • VF/VT after 3 shocks
  • PEA without reversible cause identified
  • Persistent arrest > 5 min
  • Suspected tamponade or active bleeding

6. Internal cardiac massage during open exploration

7. Identify + treat cause: control bleeding, evacuate tamponade, repair injury, ECMO if cardiogenic shock Drug differences:
  • Avoid bolus epinephrine (severe HTN, intracardiac complications)
  • Use vasopressin or norepi infusion
  • Amiodarone for VF/VT
  • Calcium for hyperkalemia / Ca channel blocker toxicity ECMO / ECLS: bridge to recovery / transplant; rapid deployment improves survival in refractory. Outcomes: CALS protocol implementation improves survival from cardiac arrest after cardiac surgery to ~ 50%+ (vs 17% standard ACLS). Training: STS publishes CALS course + simulation curriculum; ICU + OR teams should be familiar + drilled.
reference_source: STS Cardiac Surgical Resuscitation (CALS) Protocol; Dunning J et al. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg; Society of Thoracic Surgeons

scenario:
ระบบ EMS / quality / business of medicine: medical malpractice + surgical complications

คำถาม: how to handle adverse outcome + communication

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adverse event management + communication — open disclosure + patient safety

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Adverse event management + communication — open disclosure + patient safety:

(1) Immediate response:
  • Address ongoing medical care first (treat patient, stabilize, escalate)
  • Document accurately + factually;

(2) Open disclosure / 'Sorry Works':
  • Communicate transparently with patient + family
  • Express empathy + concern (apology for distress experienced, not necessarily admission of fault)
  • Explain what happened in understandable language
  • Discuss what investigation will be undertaken
  • Discuss plan for ongoing care + how to prevent recurrence
  • Avoid speculation + blame;
  • Evidence: open disclosure REDUCES litigation + improves trust (Michigan Open Disclosure, COPIC, Kaiser studies);

(3) Apology laws / 'I'm sorry' laws — many jurisdictions (~ 39 US states, multiple international) protect expressions of empathy from being used as admission of liability; check local;

(4) Root Cause Analysis (RCA) — systematic event analysis:
  • Multidisciplinary team
  • Timeline reconstruction
  • Identify contributing factors (people, process, equipment, environment, communication) - 'Just culture' — system vs individual
  • Develop action plan
  • Implement + monitor;

(5) M&M Conferences — surgical tradition:
  • Case-based learning
  • Educational + safety improvement
  • Confidential (peer review protection) + non-punitive in best practice;

(6) Quality improvement processes:
  • Failure Mode + Effects Analysis (FMEA) — proactive risk assessment
  • PDSA cycles (Plan-Do-Study-Act)
  • Lean / Six Sigma methodology;

(7) Documentation principles:
  • Contemporaneous + factual
  • Avoid speculation, blame, emotion
  • Late entries marked + dated
  • Original records preserved;

(8) Legal considerations when adverse event:
  • Notify risk management / hospital administration
  • Consult legal counsel
  • Maintain communication with patient (open disclosure within legal framework)
  • Quality of care matters AND communication matters — both reduce litigation - 'Disclose + offer' programs (e.g., University of Michigan) — proactive disclosure + apology + compensation — reduces overall claims + costs;

(9) Patient safety culture:
  • Just culture
  • Blameless reporting (incident reports, near-miss reporting)
  • Leadership commitment
  • Continuous learning;

(10) Personal impact:
  • Second victim phenomenon — physician/team also affected by adverse outcome
  • Peer support, mental health support, debriefing
  • Burnout prevention
————————————————
Adverse event management + open disclosure — central to modern patient safety + culture. Open disclosure (Australia, UK, US trends — Joint Commission, AHA, ACOG advocate):

1. Acknowledge the event happened

2. Empathize + offer sincere apology for impact

3. Explain in understandable terms what happened (factually, without speculation)

4. Discuss investigation + steps to prevent recurrence

5. Continue care + communication

6. Compensation if appropriate Evidence: open disclosure programs (Michigan, COPIC, Kaiser, VA):
  • Decreased litigation + costs
  • Improved patient satisfaction
  • Improved staff morale
  • Improved safety culture Apology laws: ~ 39 US states + many international jurisdictions protect expressions of empathy/sympathy from being used as admission of liability. Allows physicians to apologize without legal jeopardy. Root Cause Analysis (RCA) principles:
  • System focus, not individual blame
  • Identify contributing factors (5 Whys, Ishikawa fishbone)
  • Generate corrective actions
  • Implement + monitor - 'Just culture' — distinguish system error vs individual reckless behavior vs honest mistake 'Just culture' (Marx, Reason):
  • Three behaviors: human error (console), at-risk (coach), reckless (sanction)
  • Encourages reporting + learning
  • Removes fear of blame for honest mistake Failure Mode + Effects Analysis (FMEA) — proactive: anticipate failure modes before they occur; mitigate risk. PDSA cycle (Plan-Do-Study-Act) — quality improvement framework. M&M Conference — surgical learning tradition; case-based; peer review protection; educational not punitive in optimal practice. Documentation in adverse events: factual + contemporaneous + non-judgmental; avoid speculation, opinion, blame; legal protection by clear documentation. Second victim phenomenon — clinician/team distress after adverse event affecting patient: physical, psychological, professional impact; peer support, debriefing, mental health resources critical; programs like RISE (Resilience in Stressful Events) at Johns Hopkins. Disclose + Offer Programs (University of Michigan, others) — proactive disclosure of error + apology + compensation offer — reduces overall claims, costs, and patient harm.
reference_source: Joint Commission Sentinel Event Reporting; AHRQ Open Disclosure; ACOG Committee Opinion; Boothman RC et al. (Michigan Open Disclosure)

detailed_explanation.summary:
Adverse event management + communication — open disclosure + patient safety:

(1) Immediate response:
  • Address ongoing medical care first (treat patient, stabilize, escalate)
  • Document accurately + factually

scenario:
ระบบ EMS / professionalism: ethics + informed consent

คำถาม: surgical informed consent + complex situations
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Informed consent — ethical + legal cornerstone:

(1) Elements of informed consent:
  • Disclosure: nature of procedure, indication, risks (material risks), benefits, alternatives (including no treatment), prognosis
  • Comprehension: patient understands (assess with teach-back)
  • Voluntariness: not coerced
  • Competence/Capacity: decision-making capacity present
  • Authorization: explicit consent;

(2) Standards for disclosure (vary by jurisdiction):
  • Professional / reasonable physician: what a reasonable physician would disclose (older standard)
  • Reasonable patient (material risk): what a reasonable patient would want to know — modern preferred standard
  • Subjective patient: what this specific patient would want;

(3) Capacity assessment — 4 components (MacArthur):
  • Understand information
  • Appreciate situation + consequences
  • Reason about options
  • Communicate choice;

(4) Capacity vs competence:
  • Capacity is clinical judgment, decision-specific, fluctuates
  • Competence is legal determination
  • Both required for valid consent;

(5) Special situations:
  • Emergency: implied consent for life-saving treatment when capacity absent + no surrogate available
  • Surrogate decision-maker: legal hierarchy (spouse, adult child, parent, sibling per state law); follow advance directive / patient's prior expressed wishes (substituted judgment); best interests if no expressed wishes
  • Pediatric: parent / legal guardian; assent of child as developmentally appropriate; exceptions: emancipated minor, mature minor doctrine, specific medical issues (STI, contraception, mental health, substance use depending on jurisdiction)
  • Pregnancy: maternal autonomy paramount; competing fetal interests legally + ethically complex
  • Cognitive impairment: assess capacity decision-by-decision; involve family + surrogate; advance directives
  • Jehovah's Witness + blood refusal: respect adult capacitated patient; pediatric — court intervention may be needed (best interest of child); document + plan accordingly;

(6) Documentation: signed consent form + procedure note documenting discussion; teach-back; questions answered;

(7) Refusal of treatment: equally valid right; document discussion + understanding; offer second opinion + reconsider; counsel of consequences without coercion;

(8) Withdrawal / withholding life-sustaining treatment: same ethical principle as not initiating; supported by ethics committees, palliative care, legal frameworks; symptom management during withdrawal (opioid + benzodiazepine for dyspnea/anxiety); not euthanasia;

(9) Cultural + religious considerations: explore + respect; interpreter services; family-centered decision-making in some cultures;

(10) Ethics consultation for complex/disputed: hospital ethics committee resource;

(11) Conflicts of interest: disclose; research consent additional layer; IRB oversight
————————————————
Informed Consent — ethical + legal foundation of surgical practice. Required for all surgical procedures (with limited exceptions like emergent life-saving in unconscious patient). Four elements:

1. Disclosure — adequate information

2. Comprehension — patient understands

3. Voluntariness — free of coercion

4. Authorization — explicit consent Disclosure standards (jurisdiction-dependent):
  • Professional / reasonable physician: traditional — what reasonable physician would disclose
  • Material risk / reasonable patient (Canterbury v. Spence 1972, US): modern preferred — what reasonable patient would want to know; includes common risks + serious uncommon risks
  • Subjective patient: rare — what this particular patient would want Capacity — clinical determination, decision-specific, fluctuating:
  • Understand information
  • Appreciate situation + consequences
  • Reason about options
  • Communicate choice MacArthur Competence Assessment Tool — standardized. Competence — legal determination (court). Special situations:

1. Emergency — implied consent for life-saving in incapacitated patient without available surrogate; treatment to extent reasonable + necessary

2. Surrogate decision-making — hierarchy by state law: typically spouse, adult child, parent, sibling, other relative, close friend; follow advance directive if exists; use substituted judgment (what patient would want); best interests if no expressed wishes

3. Pediatric — parental consent + age-appropriate assent; emancipated minor, mature minor doctrine, specific conditions (reproductive, mental health, substance use) — varies by state

4. Cognitive impairment / dementia — capacity decision-by-decision; advance directives; surrogate

5. Blood refusal (Jehovah's Witness) — adult capacitated patient's right; pediatric — best interest of child may override (court intervention if needed)

6. Pregnancy — maternal autonomy paramount; some legal cases test

7. Substance use, intoxication — capacity assessment carefully

8. Mental illness — does not equate to incapacity; assess specifically Refusal of treatment — same right as consent; document + counsel; not coercion. Withdrawing / withholding life-sustaining treatment — ethically same as not initiating; well-established legal + ethical framework; symptom management. Cultural + religious sensitivity — interpreter, family involvement, cultural humility. Ethics consultation — for complex / disputed cases. Conflicts of interest disclosure — relevant to consent (research, financial relationships). Research consent — IRB + additional protections (HIPAA, informed consent specific to research).
reference_source: AMA Code of Medical Ethics; ACS Statement on Principles; Canterbury v. Spence 1972; MacArthur Competence Assessment

  • Competence/Capacity: decision-making capacity present

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 25 ปี Type 1 DM uncontrolled HbA1c 11.4 + multiple admit DKA + history of acute MI 1 year ago + ESRD on dialysis 2 years

Referral for kidney + pancreas transplant evaluation

คำถาม: pre-transplant workup + integrative considerations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney (SPK) transplant evaluation — integrative approach

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney (SPK) transplant evaluation — integrative approach:

(1) Candidacy assessment for SPK:
  • Type 1 DM with ESRD
  • Brittle DM with poor quality of life (hypoglycemia unawareness, severe complications)
  • No major medical/surgical contraindications
  • Adequate functional status to undergo surgery + immunosuppression
  • Psychological + social support;

(2) Pre-transplant evaluation comprehensive:
  • Cardiac: stress test (often pharmacologic — adenosine, dobutamine); coronary angiogram given prior MI (recurrent ischemia risk); echo (LV function, valves); 24h Holter if arrhythmia history
  • Pulmonary: PFT; CXR
  • Infectious: HIV, HBV, HCV, CMV, EBV, VZV, TB (IGRA/skin test), syphilis, strongyloides (depending on region); vaccination updates
  • Cancer screening: age-appropriate (colonoscopy, mammogram, Pap, PSA), thorough skin exam
  • Endocrine: thyroid, parathyroid (HPT common in ESRD)
  • GI: hepatitis serology + LFT; H. pylori;
  • Vascular: peripheral arterial — both for transplant access + future considerations
  • Dental: clearance, source control
  • Psychiatric / psychosocial: depression, anxiety, substance use, adherence assessment, support system
  • Nutrition: optimize
  • Smoking cessation absolute;

(3) Listing for transplant — UNOS / OPTN matching system;
  • HLA typing, panel reactive antibodies (PRA), crossmatch
  • Allocation algorithm by region;
  • Wait list management;

(4) Surgical considerations:
  • Pancreas transplant + kidney transplant — combined operation
  • Pancreas: portal vs systemic venous drainage; bladder vs enteric exocrine drainage
  • Kidney: heterotopic in iliac fossa
  • Iliac vessel access + arterial reconstruction;

(5) Immunosuppression post-transplant:
  • Induction: anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) or basiliximab
  • Maintenance: tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroid (taper)
  • Adjustments per complications
  • Lifelong;

(6) Outcomes:
  • SPK > Pancreas alone in long-term graft survival
  • 5-year patient survival 90%+, pancreas graft 70-80%, kidney 80-85%
  • Quality of life: insulin-free, improved DM complications progression
  • Hypoglycemia unawareness resolved typically;

(7) Post-transplant management:
  • Immunosuppression monitoring (drug levels)
  • Rejection surveillance (creatinine, amylase, glucose)
  • Infection prophylaxis (PJP — TMP-SMX; CMV — valganciclovir; fungal — fluconazole)
  • Cancer surveillance (increased risk)
  • Cardiovascular risk management
  • Lifestyle
  • Adherence support;

(8) Family + support involvement essential;

(9) Long-term complications: chronic rejection, infections, malignancy (especially skin, PTLD), recurrence of underlying disease (rare in T1DM)
————————————————
Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney (SPK) Transplantation — option for selected T1DM with ESRD: free from dialysis + insulin; improved QOL + survival vs dialysis alone or kidney alone for many. Indications:

1. T1DM with ESRD (creatinine clearance < 20-30, on dialysis or pre-emptive)

2. Brittle T1DM with hypoglycemia unawareness or severe complications without ESRD (pancreas alone — PA — option, but lower graft survival) Patient selection considerations:
  • Cardiac risk significant in DM patients — comprehensive evaluation
  • Vascular disease assessment
  • Psychosocial readiness + adherence
  • Avoid in active malignancy, active infection, severe non-correctable comorbidity, substance use Three main pancreas transplant types:

1. SPK (Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney) — best graft survival of pancreas options

2. PAK (Pancreas After Kidney) — if pancreas needs after prior kidney transplant

3. PA / PTA (Pancreas Transplant Alone) — for hypoglycemia unawareness without ESRD; lower graft survival Surgical technique variations:
  • Exocrine drainage: enteric (most common modern — to Roux limb of jejunum) vs bladder (older — easier monitoring of urinary amylase but UTI + dehydration + acidosis problems)
  • Endocrine drainage: portal (more physiologic) vs systemic (iliac vein — more common)
  • Concurrent kidney transplant to ipsilateral or contralateral iliac fossa Immunosuppression typical:
  • Induction: ATG (anti-thymocyte globulin) or basiliximab (anti-IL-2R)
  • Maintenance: tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroid (often tapered to low-dose or stopped) Outcomes (USA, UNOS data):
  • 5-yr patient survival ~ 90%+
  • 5-yr pancreas graft survival ~ 70-80%
  • 5-yr kidney graft survival ~ 80-85%
  • Insulin-free for vast majority while graft functions
  • Improved diabetic complication progression (nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy)
  • Resolved hypoglycemia unawareness Post-transplant management:
  • Immunosuppression monitoring + adjustment
  • Rejection surveillance: creatinine, glucose, amylase (pancreas)
  • Infection prophylaxis: PJP, CMV, fungal
  • Vaccine schedule update
  • Cancer surveillance (skin especially, PTLD)
  • Cardiovascular risk
  • Adherence support Islet cell transplantation — alternative to whole-organ pancreas; minimally invasive (percutaneous portal vein infusion); good for selected (hypoglycemia unawareness without ESRD); insulin independence rates improving but lower than whole-organ; multiple donors usually needed (CIT protocol). Newer: islet auto-transplantation post-pancreatectomy (chronic pancreatitis); stem cell-derived islet therapy (Vertex VX-880 + others in trial).
reference_source: OPTN/UNOS Policies; Gruessner AC + Sutherland DE Pancreas Transplant Outcomes; AST/ASTS Guidelines

detailed_explanation.summary:

(1) Candidacy assessment for SPK:
  • Type 1 DM with ESRD
  • Brittle DM with poor quality of life (hypoglycemia unawareness, severe complications)
  • No major medical/surgical contraindications
  • Adequate functional status to undergo surgery + immunosuppression
  • Psychological + social support

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 40 ปี post-living-donor liver transplant 5 ปี for HCC + HBV cirrhosis — มา outpatient clinic for routine surveillance + planning for second pregnancy

Lab: stable graft function, HBV DNA undetectable on antiviral, immunosuppression on low-dose tacrolimus monotherapy

คำถาม: integrative survivorship + reproductive planning

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-liver transplant survivorship + planning pregnancy — integrative care

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Post-liver transplant survivorship + planning pregnancy — integrative care:

(1) Multidisciplinary clinic: transplant hepatology, transplant surgery, MFM (maternal-fetal medicine), obstetrics, pharmacy, social work, mental health;

(2) Pre-conception counseling:
  • Timing: ≥ 1-2 years post-transplant with stable graft function + immunosuppression
  • Stable lab parameters: normal LFT, stable creatinine, no rejection in 6-12 months
  • Medication review + adjustment (see below)
  • Disease control (HBV undetectable, HCC surveillance)
  • Maternal health optimization (BMI, BP, glycemic, vaccines)
  • Genetic counseling if disease has heritable component;

(3) Immunosuppression in pregnancy:
  • Tacrolimus — safe in pregnancy; clearance increases — may need dose increase (monitor levels)
  • Cyclosporine — safe alternative; similar
  • Azathioprine — safe in pregnancy + breastfeeding (despite black box warning, evidence supports safety for fetus)
  • Mycophenolate (MMF, MPA) — TERATOGENIC — fetal loss + congenital anomalies (cleft, ear, cardiac) — STOP 6 weeks before conception + use alternative (azathioprine)
  • mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) — limited data; preferable to avoid; switch if possible
  • Steroids — low-dose acceptable
  • Belatacept — limited data
  • Antivirals (entecavir, tenofovir for HBV) — tenofovir preferred in pregnancy (better safety data); continue;

(4) HBV-specific considerations:
  • Continue antiviral suppression
  • Tenofovir preferred (TDF or TAF) over entecavir in pregnancy
  • HBV transmission to neonate: HBIG + HBV vaccine at birth + appropriate maternal viral suppression
  • Monitor maternal viral load + LFT
  • HCC surveillance continues;

(5) HCC surveillance:
  • 5-yr post-transplant: AFP + imaging (US/CT/MRI) q6-12 mo standard for transplanted HCC
  • Lifelong surveillance;

(6) Pregnancy management (high-risk):
  • Increased risks: HT, GDM, preeclampsia, preterm birth, SGA, miscarriage, abruption, infection
  • Multidisciplinary high-risk OB
  • Increased fetal surveillance + maternal monitoring
  • Vaccinations updated (acellular pertussis Tdap, influenza inactivated)
  • Avoid live vaccines (yellow fever, MMR, varicella);

(7) Delivery planning:
  • Vaginal delivery preferred when feasible
  • Cesarean for obstetric indications
  • Anti-rejection medication continued throughout
  • Stress-dose steroids may be needed
  • Antibiotic prophylaxis if cesarean (standard);

(8) Postpartum:
  • Continue immunosuppression
  • Lactation: most immunosuppressants compatible (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, azathioprine, prednisone); mycophenolate AVOID
  • Postpartum surveillance for rejection (immune changes post-delivery)
  • HCC surveillance resumes per schedule;

(9) Family planning + contraception:
  • During fertile years + spacing pregnancies
  • Reliable methods; IUD (copper preferred over hormonal — controversial efficacy in immunosuppressed; reasonable); barrier; permanent if family complete;

(10) Long-term: continue lifelong post-transplant care; family + caregiver support; psychological wellbeing
————————————————
Pregnancy after liver transplant — increasingly common as transplant outcomes improve. Pre-conception planning: - ≥ 1-2 years post-transplant
  • Stable graft function (LFT, Cr, immunosuppression levels)
  • No rejection within 6-12 months
  • Disease-specific control (HBV, HCV, autoimmune all in remission)
  • HCC: appropriate surveillance + acceptable disease-free interval (lifelong surveillance) Immunosuppression in pregnancy — major modifications:

1. Tacrolimus: CONTINUE; pregnancy-safe; clearance ↑ → may need dose increase (level monitoring)

2. Cyclosporine: CONTINUE; alternative to tacrolimus; safe

3. Azathioprine: CONTINUE — despite black box warning, evidence supports fetal safety

4. Prednisone / methylprednisolone: low-dose CONTINUE; safe

5. Mycophenolate (MMF, MPA): STOP — TERATOGENIC (cleft palate, microtia, congenital heart, fetal loss); switch to azathioprine ≥ 6 weeks pre-conception 6. mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus): limited data; switch off if possible

7. Belatacept: limited data Specific disease considerations:
  • HBV: continue antiviral suppression; tenofovir TDF/TAF preferred in pregnancy (entecavir limited data); newborn HBIG + HBV vaccine; maternal viral load monitoring
  • HCV: cured most patients with DAAs; if active — defer pregnancy until treated
  • Autoimmune (PBC, PSC, AIH): continue azathioprine; monitor flares
  • NASH/MASH: weight management
  • HCC: continued surveillance regardless of pregnancy Maternal complications increased: HT, preeclampsia, GDM, infection, anemia, preterm birth, bleeding (cesarean), graft dysfunction risk. Fetal complications increased: prematurity, IUGR/SGA, lower birth weight, miscarriage (some studies); congenital anomalies usually not increased (with appropriate medication management). Surveillance during pregnancy: more frequent OB visits, fetal monitoring (growth, biophysical), maternal labs (LFT, Cr, drug levels, glucose, viral load). Delivery: vaginal preferred when feasible; cesarean for obstetric indications; routine perioperative antibiotic; anesthesia consult. Lactation compatibility: tacrolimus, cyclosporine, azathioprine, prednisone, hydroxychloroquine all generally compatible (modify per Lactmed); mycophenolate AVOID. Contraception during transplant + between pregnancies: reliable + safe — IUD (copper or hormonal), barrier, hormonal (caution with drug interactions), permanent if family complete.
reference_source: AST Consensus Conference Pregnancy + Transplantation; AASLD Pregnancy + Liver Disease; Coscia LA et al. Clin Transplant

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 50 ปี post-bariatric sleeve gastrectomy 5 ปีก่อน — มาด้วยอาการ chronic GERD severe + Barrett's segment 2 cm + esophagitis grade C; PPI optimal failed; weight regain 15 kg from nadir

คำถาม: complex management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-sleeve gastrectomy GERD complications + weight regain — multidisciplinary

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Post-sleeve gastrectomy GERD complications + weight regain — multidisciplinary:

(1) Workup:
  • EGD (already done) — confirm Barrett's, biopsy, exclude dysplasia, anatomic assessment (hiatal hernia, sleeve anatomy)
  • High-resolution manometry — esophageal motility
  • 24h pH study off PPI — quantify reflux burden
  • Barium swallow — anatomy of sleeve + reflux dynamics
  • Possible gastric emptying study;

(2) GERD post-sleeve mechanism:
  • Removal of fundus + LES support + His angle disruption
  • High intragastric pressure (narrow tube)
  • Hiatal hernia (often pre-existing)
  • Stenosis at incisura, twisting of sleeve
  • Decreased gastric compliance;

(3) Medical optimization:
  • High-dose PPI (consider esomeprazole 40 mg BID)
  • H2 blocker bedtime adjunct
  • Alginate (Gaviscon Advance) post-meal + bedtime
  • Lifestyle: weight loss if indicated, head of bed elevation, no late meals, avoid trigger foods
  • Sucralfate for esophagitis;

(4) Barrett's surveillance + management:
  • EGD with biopsies (Seattle protocol) q3-5 yr no dysplasia, more frequent if dysplasia
  • Endoscopic ablation (RFA) for dysplasia/intramucosal carcinoma
  • PPI lifelong;

(5) Revision bariatric surgery options for severe GERD:
  • Conversion to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) — gold standard for sleeve failure + GERD: eliminates reflux + addresses weight regain + better metabolic outcomes; technically challenging revision
  • Magnetic sphincter augmentation (LINX device) — for selected without hiatal hernia or severe esophagitis; can be done post-sleeve
  • Hiatal hernia repair + sleeve adjustment — if HH primary issue
  • Fundoplication impossible post-sleeve (no fundus);

(6) Weight regain workup — common after bariatric:
  • Behavioral (eating habits, portion, snacking, beverages)
  • Hormonal adaptation (ghrelin recovers, GLP-1 may decrease)
  • Anatomic (sleeve dilation, gastrogastric fistula)
  • Psychological (food addiction, binge eating, depression)
  • Medical (medication weight gain — antidepressants, antipsychotics);

(7) Weight regain management:
  • Nutrition + behavioral counseling
  • Pharmacotherapy: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, tirzepatide) — increasingly used post-bariatric; counsel on possible reduced response
  • Revision surgery if anatomic issue + severe regain
  • Endoscopic options (re-sleeve, plication) — selected;

(8) Multidisciplinary: bariatric surgery, GI, nutrition, psychology, primary care; long-term integrated follow-up;

(9) Patient counseling: realistic expectations, lifelong management
————————————————
Sleeve gastrectomy + GERD — major issue. Sleeve gastrectomy has GERD as major long-term complication (10-30% develop new or worsened GERD; even higher with longer follow-up). Some develop Barrett's esophagus (~ 3-15% at 5+ years per cohorts), potentially with dysplasia / adenocarcinoma. Pre-op evaluation should screen for GERD severity — patients with severe pre-op GERD may not be ideal sleeve candidates; some advocate RYGB primary for these patients. Mechanisms of post-sleeve GERD:

1. Anatomic — disruption of His angle, removal of fundus, reduced LES support

2. Physiologic — high intragastric pressure (narrow tube), reduced compliance

3. Hiatal hernia (pre-existing or new)

4. Sleeve anatomy issues — stenosis at incisura, twist, dilated proximal pouch

5. Delayed gastric emptying Management ladder:

1. Medical optimization: high-dose PPI (potentially BID), H2 blocker adjunct, alginate, lifestyle, weight management

2. EGD with Barrett's surveillance: Seattle protocol biopsies; treat dysplasia (RFA/EMR)

3. Workup: manometry, pH study, barium swallow, gastric emptying

4. Surgical revision for refractory:
  • Conversion to RYGB — most effective for GERD + addresses weight regain; complex revision but acceptable outcomes
  • LINX (magnetic sphincter augmentation) — selected without severe esophagitis or large hiatal hernia; preserves anatomy
  • Hiatal hernia repair if primary anatomic issue

5. Fundoplication NOT possible post-sleeve (no fundus available) Weight regain post-bariatric — common (20-40% at 10 yr):
  • Behavioral + dietary
  • Hormonal adaptation
  • Anatomic (sleeve dilation, gastrogastric fistula in RYGB)
  • Psychological
  • Medication weight gain Management: behavioral counseling, nutrition support, GLP-1 + dual agonists (semaglutide 2.4 mg, tirzepatide 15 mg increasingly used post-bariatric with good response in many), revision surgery for anatomic issue + severe regain, endoscopic options (re-sleeve, plication). GLP-1 + GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists post-bariatric: increasingly utilized for weight regain or insufficient initial response; semaglutide (Wegovy, Ozempic), tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound); response variable but many achieve meaningful additional weight loss; long-term data accumulating. Multidisciplinary lifelong care — essential for bariatric patient; integrate medical, surgical, nutritional, psychological, behavioral approaches.
reference_source: ASMBS Position Statements on Revisional Bariatric; SAGES Guidelines; Felsenreich DM et al. Obes Surg

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 60 ปี diagnosed with HCC 6 months ago — Child-Pugh B + advanced HCC + portal vein invasion + ascites + recently completed atezolizumab/bevacizumab but progression + ECOG 2 + พ่อแม่และบุตรหลายคน + ครอบครัวยอมรับยาก

คำถาม: integrative palliative + end-of-life care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Advanced HCC progression with limited treatment options — integrative palliative + serious illness conversations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop all medications immediately + send home

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Advanced HCC progression with limited treatment options — integrative palliative + serious illness conversations:

(1) Multidisciplinary team meeting: medical oncology, hepatology, palliative care, social work, chaplain, family medicine;

(2) Goals of care discussion with patient + family — serious illness conversation framework (Ariadne Labs / Atul Gawande):
  • Understanding of illness + prognosis
  • What's important to patient (values, fears, hopes)
  • What outcomes they want to avoid
  • What functional trade-offs acceptable
  • Family wishes + dynamics
  • Cultural + spiritual considerations;

(3) Prognostic estimation:
  • Advanced HCC + Child B + progression on first-line + ECOG 2 → median survival 3-6 months
  • Be honest but compassionate — 'hope for the best, prepare for the worst'
  • Surprise question: 'would I be surprised if patient died in 12 months?' — useful prognostic question;

(4) Treatment options to consider (decreasing intensity):
  • Second-line systemic therapy (lenvatinib, sorafenib, regorafenib, cabozantinib, ramucirumab if AFP > 400) — limited efficacy; weigh toxicity vs benefit; ECOG 2 borderline candidacy
  • Locoregional therapy (TACE, Y90, SBRT) — for liver-predominant; limited utility in advanced
  • Best supportive care + palliative care — focus on QOL + symptom management;

(5) Concurrent palliative care from diagnosis of advanced disease (per Temel JCO 2010 NEJM 2010 — improves QOL + may extend survival); not 'palliative or curative' — both can coexist;

(6) Symptom management:
  • Pain: WHO ladder; opioids — careful dose-adjust in hepatic impairment (avoid morphine accumulation in renal/hepatic, use hydromorphone or fentanyl; morphine OK at adjusted dose with monitoring); coadjuvants for neuropathic
  • Ascites: large-volume paracentesis as needed; sodium restriction + diuretic adjusted for hepatic dysfunction; PleurX catheter consideration for recurrent symptomatic
  • Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose, rifaximin; treat precipitants
  • Pruritus: anti-histamine, cholestyramine, sertraline, naltrexone (caution)
  • Nausea: ondansetron, haloperidol, metoclopramide
  • Fatigue: address reversible (anemia, depression); pacing
  • Anorexia / cachexia: nutritional counseling; appetite stimulants limited efficacy (megestrol, mirtazapine, low-dose steroid); enteral if quality of life maintained
  • Anxiety + depression: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram), counseling
  • Dyspnea: oxygen, opioid (low-dose);

(7) Advance care planning + advance directive:
  • Healthcare proxy designation
  • Living will
  • DNR / POLST discussion
  • Specific care preferences (intubation, dialysis, artificial nutrition);

(8) Hospice eligibility + referral:
  • Medicare benefit if life expectancy ≤ 6 months + focus on comfort
  • Hospice services: comprehensive symptom management, nursing, social work, chaplain, volunteer, bereavement support, equipment, medications
  • Settings: home with hospice (preferred when feasible), inpatient hospice unit, residential, hospital;

(9) Family support:
  • Anticipatory grief + counseling
  • Caregiver burden + respite
  • Family meeting facilitator (palliative care, social work)
  • Children's understanding (age-appropriate communication; child life specialists if hospital-based)
  • Cultural + spiritual rituals respected
  • Practical support (legal, financial, social services);

(10) Spiritual care: chaplain consultation; cultural + religious practices integrated;

(11) Bereavement: continue support for family for 12+ months after death; identify complicated grief;

(12) Care team self-care: addressing 'second patient' issues — clinician grief, burnout prevention
————————————————
Advanced cancer integrative palliative care — concurrent palliative care from diagnosis of advanced disease improves outcomes (Temel NEJM 2010 — early integrated palliative care in NSCLC improved QOL + mood + survival). Serious illness conversation framework (Ariadne Labs, Gawande):

1. Set up the conversation (permission, dedicated time)

2. Assess understanding + information preferences

3. Share prognosis (sensitive but honest)

4. Explore key topics:
  • What's important to you?
  • What are your fears + worries?
  • What goals do you have?
  • What outcomes are unacceptable?
  • How much are you willing to go through for the chance of more time?

5. Close the conversation (recommendation, plan) Prognostication tools:
  • Surprise question: 'would I be surprised if this patient died in 12 months?'
  • Performance status (ECOG, Karnofsky)
  • Disease-specific prognostic models
  • Palliative Performance Scale (PPS)
  • PaP (Palliative Prognostic) Score Symptom management in end-stage liver disease + cancer:
  • Pain (opioid dose adjustment): avoid morphine accumulation in renal/hepatic dysfunction; hydromorphone (renal/hepatic safer), fentanyl (hepatic OK, transdermal stable in cachectic), methadone (caution, prolonged QT, complex pharmacology)
  • Ascites: paracentesis, diuretic (judicious), albumin replacement, PleurX catheter for recurrent
  • Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose, rifaximin; treat precipitants (infection, GI bleed, dehydration, medications, constipation)
  • Pruritus: cholestyramine, sertraline, naltrexone, antihistamine
  • Anorexia / cachexia: nutritional support if QOL maintained; megestrol, mirtazapine, low-dose steroid (limited benefit); cannabinoids variable
  • Fatigue: address reversible (anemia, depression, hypothyroid, hypogonadism); methylphenidate selective benefit
  • Dyspnea: opioid (low-dose morphine 2.5 mg PO q4h or equivalent — central effect on dyspnea perception), oxygen, fan, positioning, anxiolytic Advance care planning:
  • Healthcare proxy designation
  • Living will
  • POLST / MOLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) — actionable medical orders portable across settings
  • Code status discussion Hospice eligibility (Medicare US): life expectancy ≤ 6 months + comfort-focused care + foregoing curative intent — hospice provides comprehensive interdisciplinary services (nursing, social work, chaplain, home health aide, bereavement, equipment, medications). Family / caregiver support: anticipatory grief, complicated grief screening, caregiver burden assessment, respite, social work, financial counseling, children's services (Child Life), bereavement support 12+ months. Cultural + spiritual sensitivity: language, religious practices, family hierarchy, decision-making customs, end-of-life rituals — integrate into care plan. Clinician wellbeing: burnout prevention, debriefing, peer support, mental health resources — recognized critical for sustainable palliative + serious illness care delivery.
reference_source: ASCO Palliative Care Integration; Temel JS et al. NEJM 2010; Ariadne Labs Serious Illness Care; AAHPM

detailed_explanation.summary:
Advanced HCC progression with limited treatment options — integrative palliative + serious illness conversations:

(1) Multidisciplinary team meeting: medical oncology, hepatology, palliative care, social work, chaplain, family medicine

scenario:
ชายไทยอายุ 55 ปี smoker, COPD GOLD C, ผ่า lung transplant 6 weeks ago — มา ED ด้วยอาการ shortness of breath progressive 3 days + fever 38.4°C + cough + decreased exercise tolerance

VS: BP 124/78, HR 102, RR 24, SpO2 88% room air
CXR: bilateral infiltrates new vs prior baseline
Lab: WBC 14,400 (was 8,000), Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.0), tacrolimus level 12 ng/mL (target 10-15)

คำถาม: differential + workup

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-lung transplant respiratory deterioration — broad differential, expedited workup

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Post-lung transplant respiratory deterioration — broad differential, expedited workup:

(1) Differential:
  • Acute rejection (most common cause of decline first 6 mo)
  • cellular (T-cell mediated) more common than antibody-mediated
  • Diagnosis: bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy (gold standard)
  • Treatment: pulse steroid (methylprednisolone 1 g × 3 days), augment immunosuppression; ATG/rituximab for severe/AMR
  • Infection (also common):
  • CMV (q month surveillance for first 6-12 mo); treat valganciclovir/ganciclovir
  • PJP (Pneumocystis jirovecii — prophylaxis TMP-SMX, dapsone if intolerant); treat high-dose TMP-SMX + steroid
  • Fungal (Aspergillus most concerning in lung transplant) — voriconazole, posaconazole
  • Bacterial pneumonia (community + nosocomial) — culture-directed antibiotic
  • Atypical (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia)
  • Reperfusion injury / primary graft dysfunction — usually earlier (< 72h post-transplant)
  • Airway complications: anastomotic dehiscence, stenosis, malacia — bronchoscopy + intervention
  • Pulmonary embolism — high risk post-major surgery + hypercoagulable
  • Cardiac: rejection if heart transplant component, fluid overload
  • Drug toxicity: tacrolimus, mycophenolate, sirolimus pulmonary toxicity;

(2) Workup:
  • CBC + diff, BMP, LFT, lactate, troponin, BNP
  • Respiratory cultures (sputum, bronchoscopy with BAL — culture for bacteria, virus, fungus, AFB)
  • CMV PCR (blood), galactomannan (Aspergillus), β-D-glucan
  • Tacrolimus + other drug levels (already checked — therapeutic)
  • ABG
  • CXR + CT chest with contrast (if Cr permits)
  • ECG, echo if cardiac concern
  • Bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy — for rejection vs infection;

(3) Empiric treatment while workup in progress:
  • Broad-spectrum antibiotic (cefepime + vancomycin)
  • Antifungal if high risk (voriconazole)
  • CMV antiviral if suspected
  • Consider pulse steroid if rejection strongly suspected after infection ruled out;

(4) Multidisciplinary: transplant pulmonology, ID, surgery, ICU;

(5) Long-term: rejection prophylaxis (immunosuppression adherence, drug levels), infection prophylaxis (PJP, CMV, fungal), surveillance bronchoscopies, pulmonary rehab, vaccinations (inactivated), lifestyle
————————————————
Post-lung transplant respiratory deterioration — broad differential; rejection + infection top considerations; both can overlap; biopsy often needed to distinguish. Common causes by time after transplant: - < 30 days: primary graft dysfunction, bacterial pneumonia, anastomotic issues, hyperacute rejection (rare with cross-match)
  • 30 days
  • 6 months: acute cellular rejection peak, CMV, PJP (if not on prophylaxis), bacterial, Aspergillus, herpes
  • 6 months
  • 1 year: late acute rejection, chronic rejection emerging (BOS — bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome / CLAD — chronic lung allograft dysfunction), community-acquired infections, CMV later if no prophylaxis - > 1 year: chronic rejection (CLAD/BOS) main long-term threat, infections, malignancy (PTLD, skin) Acute rejection:
  • Cellular (T-cell mediated) — more common; bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy (grading A0-A4 per ISHLT)
  • Antibody-mediated (AMR) — emerging recognition; DSA + biopsy with C4d staining; harder to diagnose Bronchoscopy with BAL + transbronchial biopsy — gold standard for:
  • Distinguishing rejection from infection
  • Identifying specific pathogen
  • Quantifying inflammation
  • Surveillance bronchoscopies routine first year Common infections post-lung transplant:

1. CMV — common; PCR surveillance; valganciclovir/ganciclovir prophylaxis + treatment

2. PJP (P. jirovecii) — TMP-SMX prophylaxis lifelong; high-dose TMP-SMX + steroid for treatment

3. Aspergillus — voriconazole prophylaxis often in early post-transplant; treatment voriconazole / isavuconazole / amphotericin

4. Bacterial — culture-directed; nosocomial vs community

5. HSV / VZV / EBV (PTLD) — antiviral prophylaxis (acyclovir/valacyclovir) Chronic Lung Allograft Dysfunction (CLAD) — main barrier to long-term survival post-lung transplant:
  • Bronchiolitis Obliterans Syndrome (BOS) — obstructive
  • Restrictive Allograft Syndrome (RAS)
  • 50% by 5 yr; limits long-term survival
  • Treatment options limited; some response to azithromycin (motility, anti-inflammatory), extracorporeal photopheresis, montelukast; re-transplantation in selected Empiric treatment while workup pending: balance treating likely conditions while waiting for tissue diagnosis; multidisciplinary team essential. Drug interactions — extensive: azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole, posaconazole) interact with calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) — increase levels; dose-adjust + monitor closely.
reference_source: ISHLT Lung Transplant Guidelines; AST/ASTS Lung Transplant Guidelines; Greer M et al. Curr Opin Organ Transplant

  • Diagnosis: bronchoscopy + transbronchial biopsy (gold standard)

scenario:
ทารกแรกเกิดอายุ 1 วัน born term, มาด้วย abdominal wall defect — bowel + liver exposed ที่ midline ไม่มี covering membrane

คำถาม: distinguish + management
reference_source: APSA Pediatric Surgery Handbook; Christison-Lagay ER et al. Semin Pediatr Surg

detailed_explanation.summary: Gastroschisis vs Omphalocele — distinct entities: Gastroschisis: defect right of umbilicus (paraumbilical), NO covering membrane, bowel exposed (often inflamed/thickened from amniotic fluid exposure), umbilical cord normal position, LOW association with other anomalies (5-10%) but high bowel atresia
detailed_explanation.reason: Abdominal wall defects — two main: Gastroschisis vs Omphalocele. Differentiation critical for management + prognosis. Gastroschisis: right paraumbilical, no sac, inflamed bowel, fewer associations, primary or staged silo closure. Omphalocele: midline, intact sac, liver + bowel, MANY associated anomalies (cardiac 30-50%, chromosomal — trisomies, Beckwith-Wiedemann, Cantrell pentalogy), echo + karyotype workup essential. Both need NG decompression, IV fluid, thermoregulation, antibiotic, and surgical management (primary or staged silo). Outcomes generally good with modern care; long-term issues: feeding, growth, motility, short gut if extensive bowel resection.

scenario:
หญิงไทยอายุ 25 ปี severe anxiety + abdominal pain RLQ + nausea but normal vitals + normal labs; previously suspected appendicitis but US negative + clinical improved overnight; planning discharge

คำถาม: management of equivocal abdominal pain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Equivocal abdominal pain — observation + serial reassessment + targeted workup

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Equivocal abdominal pain — observation + serial reassessment + targeted workup:

(1) Serial abdominal exam by same examiner — most valuable diagnostic tool;

(2) Active observation in ED / observation unit 4-12h — many self-resolve OR develop clear features;

(3) Repeat labs at 6-12h if not improving — WBC trend, CRP;

(4) Imaging as guided:
  • US first (radiation-free, pelvic + abdominal)
  • CT if clinical worsening or persistent unclear
  • MRI if pregnant;

(5) Other differentials to consider in young woman with RLQ pain:
  • GI: appendicitis (early or atypical), Meckel's, mesenteric adenitis, Crohn's terminal ileitis, infectious enteritis
  • GYN: ovarian cyst (rupture, torsion — surgical emergency), ectopic pregnancy (check β-hCG always), PID, endometriosis, mittelschmerz
  • GU: UTI, ureteral colic
  • Musculoskeletal: psoas, hip
  • Other: functional, anxiety-related;

(6) β-hCG mandatory in reproductive-age female with abdominal pain;

(7) Discharge criteria:
  • Clinical improvement
  • Pain controlled
  • Tolerating PO
  • Reliable patient + access to follow-up
  • Clear return precautions (worsening pain, fever, vomiting, inability to eat, peritonitis signs);

(8) Education + follow-up clinic 24-48h;

(9) Document carefully — return reasons, time of resolution, expected course
————————————————
Equivocal acute abdomen — common scenario; balance between unnecessary surgery (negative laparotomy/laparoscopy ~ 10-15%) and missed serious diagnosis. Diagnostic approach:

1. Serial exam by same provider — most valuable

2. Active observation (4-12 hr)

3. Repeat labs (WBC trend)

4. Imaging escalation (US → CT) 5. β-hCG always in reproductive-age female

6. Consider gynecologic differentials in females Use 'safety netting' — clear return precautions, follow-up. Use 'shared decision-making' regarding observation vs intervention. Documentation important. Pain management during observation: appropriate analgesia does not mask diagnosis (clinical exam preserved with opioid administration per modern evidence — old dogma debunked). Multimodal analgesia preferred.
reference_source: Surgical Decision Making — ACS; AAST Guidelines

detailed_explanation.summary:
Equivocal abdominal pain — observation + serial reassessment + targeted workup:

(1) Serial abdominal exam by same examiner — most valuable diagnostic tool

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 65 ปี chronic alcohol use + cirrhosis + esophageal varices on propranolol + EVBL + adherent — มา clinic for routine surveillance

คำถาม: HCC surveillance + cirrhosis management principles
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Cirrhosis comprehensive management + HCC surveillance:

(1) HCC surveillance in cirrhosis:
  • US ± AFP q6 months for at-risk (cirrhosis any etiology, chronic HBV without cirrhosis if Asian male > 40 / Asian female > 50 / Africans > 20 / family history HCC)
  • Multi-phase contrast CT or MRI if US suboptimal (obesity, fatty liver) or lesion detected
  • LI-RADS classification for indeterminate lesions;

(2) Variceal surveillance:
  • EGD at diagnosis of cirrhosis
  • Repeat per Baveno criteria: small varices, no red signs → 2 yr; large or red signs → primary prophylaxis (NSBB or band)
  • On secondary prophylaxis (post-bleed, this patient) → continue NSBB + serial banding until eradication;

(3) Treat underlying liver disease:
  • HBV: antiviral lifelong
  • HCV: DAA eradication (cure > 95%)
  • Alcohol: cessation absolute
  • NAFLD/MASH: weight loss, GLP-1 agonist (semaglutide, tirzepatide), pioglitazone (selected), resmetirom (FDA-approved 2024 for non-cirrhotic MASH)
  • Autoimmune: immunosuppression;

(4) Decompensation management:
  • Ascites: sodium restriction (2 g/d) + diuretic (spironolactone + furosemide 100:40 ratio); refractory → large-volume paracentesis + albumin replacement; TIPS for refractory;
  • SBP prophylaxis if ascites + low protein or prior SBP — ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX or norfloxacin daily
  • Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + rifaximin; treat precipitants
  • HRS: vasopressor (terlipressin, midodrine + octreotide + albumin) + RRT bridge; transplant
  • HPS (hepatopulmonary syndrome) — transplant evaluation;

(5) Liver transplant evaluation:
  • MELD-Na score (waitlist priority US)
  • Refer when MELD > 15, refractory complications, HCC within criteria
  • Comprehensive evaluation: cardiac, pulm, infectious, psychosocial, cancer;

(6) Nutrition: high-calorie + high-protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg); avoid restrictive low-protein (worsens malnutrition + encephalopathy); zinc, vitamin D, thiamine;

(7) Vaccinations (inactivated): HAV, HBV, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID, Tdap;

(8) Lifestyle: absolute alcohol cessation, smoking cessation, weight management (NAFLD), exercise;

(9) Avoid hepatotoxic medications: NSAIDs (renal + bleeding), high-dose acetaminophen (2 g/day max in cirrhotic), aminoglycosides;

(10) Frailty + sarcopenia — assessment + intervention
————————————————
Cirrhosis comprehensive management — multidimensional. HCC surveillance (AASLD): US ± AFP q6 mo for cirrhosis + at-risk HBV. Sensitivity ~ 60-80% (US alone); higher with AFP. Multi-phase CT/MRI for diagnosis with LI-RADS. Variceal surveillance (Baveno VII):
  • EGD at diagnosis
  • Small varices no red signs: q2 yr
  • Medium/large or red signs: primary prophylaxis (NSBB — carvedilol preferred per recent data, or EVBL)
  • Secondary: NSBB + EVBL until eradication Decompensation management:
  • Ascites: Na restriction + diuretic; TIPS for refractory; albumin with LVP
  • SBP: prophylaxis ciprofloxacin/norfloxacin/TMP-SMX in at-risk
  • Encephalopathy: lactulose + rifaximin; precipitants
  • HRS: terlipressin + albumin; transplant
  • Portal HTN: TIPS for refractory complications Transplant evaluation: MELD-Na > 15, refractory complications, HCC within criteria. Underlying disease specific: HBV antiviral, HCV DAA cure, alcohol cessation, NAFLD weight loss + emerging therapies (resmetirom FDA 2024 for non-cirrhotic MASH; semaglutide promising), AIH/PBC/PSC immunosuppression. Nutrition: do NOT restrict protein (worsens sarcopenia + encephalopathy); 1.2-1.5 g/kg; zinc, vit D, thiamine. Vaccinations inactivated. Avoid hepatotoxic: NSAIDs, high acetaminophen, aminoglycosides. Frailty + sarcopenia: Liver Frailty Index (LFI); intervention.
reference_source: AASLD Cirrhosis 2021; Baveno VII 2022; AASLD HCC 2023; EASL Guidelines

  • Multi-phase contrast CT or MRI if US suboptimal (obesity, fatty liver) or lesion detected
  • LI-RADS classification for indeterminate...

คำถาม: management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prosthetic graft infection — surgical emergency requires combined antimicrobial + surgical management

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Prosthetic graft infection — surgical emergency requires combined antimicrobial + surgical management:

(1) Vascular graft infection (early < 4 mo more severe; late >4 mo possibly less severe but chronic);

(2) Initial workup: blood cultures + wound culture + tissue cultures + CT angiography (perigraft fluid/gas/abscess, anastomotic pseudoaneurysm);

(3) IV broad-spectrum antibiotic — cover staph (incl MRSA), gram-negative — empiric vancomycin + cefepime or pip-tazo; tailor per culture; long course (6 weeks IV + 6 weeks oral suppression often; lifelong oral if cannot remove graft);

(4) Surgical management options by severity + extent:
  • Graft excision + extra-anatomic bypass — gold standard for severe infection of prosthetic graft (e.g., axillobifemoral bypass for aortobifemoral graft infection); 'remove infection, reroute flow'
  • In-situ replacement with autologous vein (femoral vein, GSV, cryopreserved) — for selected (lower infection burden, anatomy suitable, organism less virulent)
  • In-situ replacement with new prosthetic / rifampin-soaked prosthetic — controversial, less infection burden, soft-tissue coverage; selected
  • Local debridement + irrigation + muscle flap coverage + retained graft — selected (low-virulence, localized, no anastomosis involvement); risk of recurrence
  • Suppressive antibiotic alone — if graft cannot be safely removed (elderly, prohibitive surgery) — chronic infection accepted;

(5) Multidisciplinary: vascular surgery, ID, plastic surgery (flaps), wound care;

(6) Limb salvage consideration if graft removal compromises perfusion → bypass first to preserve limb then address infection;

(7) Prevention: meticulous technique, prophylactic antibiotic, avoid groin incision when feasible (high infection rate), tunnel away from skin, treat infections elsewhere pre-op, glycemic control, smoking cessation
————————————————
Vascular graft infection — 1-5% incidence; mortality 10-25%; limb loss 10-20%. Classification:
  • Szilagyi: I — skin only; II — subcutaneous; III — graft involvement
  • Samson: I-V — granular detail of extent
  • Early (< 4 mo): more virulent, sepsis common, often Staph
  • Late (> 4 mo): indolent, low-virulence organisms (coag-neg Staph), chronic sinus tract Imaging:
  • CT angiography — perigraft fluid, gas, abscess, pseudoaneurysm, aortoenteric fistula
  • PET-CT — infection localization (high sensitivity)
  • WBC scan Management principles:

1. Antibiotic — broad-spectrum + culture-directed; long duration

2. Surgery — usually graft removal + revascularization

3. Extra-anatomic bypass — definitive for severe (e.g., axillobifem for aortic graft infection)

4. In-situ replacement — autologous vein (femoral or GSV) or cryopreserved arterial; or rifampin-soaked prosthetic in selected

5. Selected conservative — debridement + flap + retain graft for low-virulence, localized Aortoenteric fistula — feared complication of aortic graft infection — bleed from GI tract (small hemorrhage 'herald bleed' before catastrophic); emergency. Prevention: aseptic technique, antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin pre-incision, redose long surgery), groin avoidance when possible (high infection — alternatives like extraperitoneal access), wound closure, glycemic control, smoking cessation, MRSA decolonization in high-risk. Long-term: lifelong oral antibiotic suppression in selected (cannot remove graft + chronic low-grade infection); surveillance for recurrence + complications.
reference_source: SVS Vascular Graft Infection; ESVS Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia; FitzGerald SF et al. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg

(1) Vascular graft infection (early < 4 mo more severe

คำถาม: pediatric cardiac arrest + resuscitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pediatric cardiac arrest — PALS protocol with airway emphasis (often respiratory etiology in children)

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Pediatric cardiac arrest — PALS protocol with airway emphasis (often respiratory etiology in children):

(1) Most pediatric arrest is respiratory in origin (asphyxia, drowning, choking, sepsis) — different from adult primary cardiac;

(2) Immediate steps:
  • Bystander/EMS high-quality CPR (chest compressions 100-120/min, depth 1/3 AP diameter — 4 cm infant, 5 cm child, 5-6 cm adolescent)
  • Coordinated compressions:breaths 30:2 (single rescuer) or 15:2 (2 rescuers child)
  • Early effective ventilation critical
  • Automated external defibrillator (AED) — pediatric pads/attenuator for < 25 kg or < 8 yr (use adult if pediatric not available)
  • Defibrillation for shockable rhythms (VF/VT) — 2 J/kg first shock, 4 J/kg subsequent;

(3) In hospital:
  • Continue compressions
  • Secure airway: bag-mask first (effective oxygenation often), advanced airway (ET tube, supraglottic) when feasible
  • IV access (peripheral or intraosseous — IO is fast + reliable in pediatric, especially when peripheral difficult)
  • Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO q3-5 min
  • Amiodarone 5 mg/kg IV/IO for VF/VT (after 3rd shock); lidocaine alternative
  • Treat reversible causes (H's + T's): hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen ion (acidosis), hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, toxins, tamponade, tension pneumo, thrombosis (PE, coronary), trauma;

(4) Post-cardiac arrest care (PALS):
  • Targeted temperature management — pediatric: maintain normothermia or therapeutic hypothermia (32-34°C × 48h then rewarm) per THAPCA pediatric trials (modest benefit/equipoise)
  • Oxygenation target SpO2 94-99%
  • Hemodynamic optimization
  • Neurological assessment + supportive care
  • Identify + treat underlying cause
  • Family support, psychosocial;

(5) Strangulation / hanging-specific:
  • Cervical spine immobilization (potential injury)
  • Airway: edema common, may need surgical airway; ENT consult
  • CT brain + cervical spine when stable
  • Neuro-protective care + neurological prognostication;

(6) Outcomes:
  • Pediatric in-hospital arrest survival ~ 30-50% to discharge (better than adult)
  • Out-of-hospital pediatric ~ 10-15% survival
  • Neurological outcome variable;

(7) Trauma resuscitation considerations: TXA in massive bleeding (pediatric dose 15 mg/kg max 1 g), blood products, surgical bleeding control;

(8) Family presence during resuscitation — supported by AHA + pediatric guidelines — improves family coping
————————————————
Pediatric cardiac arrest — distinct from adult:

1. Etiology: respiratory most common (asphyxia, drowning, choking, sepsis, SIDS) vs adult primary cardiac (VF)

2. Airway + ventilation early + effective — central to pediatric resuscitation

3. PALS (Pediatric Advanced Life Support) — AHA algorithm Key differences from adult ACLS:
  • Compression depth: 1/3 AP diameter (4 cm infant, 5 cm child, 5-6 cm adolescent)
  • Compression:Ventilation ratio: 30:2 single rescuer, 15:2 two rescuers (child); adult always 30:2
  • Defibrillation: 2 J/kg first, 4 J/kg subsequent; pediatric pads/attenuator < 25 kg if available
  • Drug dosing weight-based
  • Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO
  • Amiodarone 5 mg/kg IV/IO for VF/VT
  • IO access common (fast, reliable, equivalent to IV) H's + T's (reversible causes): hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen (acidosis), hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, toxins, tamponade, tension pneumothorax, thrombosis (PE, coronary), trauma. Post-arrest care (PALS):
  • Targeted temperature management — pediatric: THAPCA trials show no clear benefit of hypothermia 32-34°C vs normothermia 36-37.5°C; either acceptable
  • Avoid hyperthermia (fever post-arrest worsens outcomes)
  • Oxygen target 94-99% (avoid hyperoxia)
  • Hemodynamic, neurological support Strangulation/hanging-specific considerations: cervical spine injury, airway edema (may need surgical airway), CT imaging, neuroprotective care. Trauma: TXA (15 mg/kg max 1 g pediatric), blood products, surgical control. Family presence during resuscitation: increasingly supported (AHA, pediatric guidelines) — improves family coping + closure. Outcomes: pediatric in-hospital arrest ~ 30-50% survival to discharge (better than adult); out-of-hospital ~ 10-15%. Variable neurological outcome — depends on cause, downtime, witnessed status, bystander CPR.
reference_source: AHA PALS Guidelines 2020; THAPCA-OH + IH Trials NEJM; AAP Trauma Guidelines

(1) Most pediatric arrest is respiratory in origin (asphyxia, drowning, choking, sepsis) — different from adult primary cardiac

scenario:
Basic science: immunology + transplant

คำถาม: organ rejection mechanisms + immunosuppression
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Transplant rejection mechanisms + immunosuppression:

(1) Types of rejection:
  • Hyperacute (minutes-hours): preformed antibodies (ABO incompatibility, HLA mismatch) → complement + thrombosis; modern era rare (crossmatch screening); treatment: graft removal
  • Acute cellular (T-cell mediated): days-weeks; T-cells recognize donor MHC; treatable with steroid + ATG; tubulitis + interstitial infiltrate (kidney)
  • Acute antibody-mediated (AMR): B-cell + antibody mediated; weeks-months; C4d deposition + DSA; harder treatment (plasmapheresis + IVIG + rituximab + bortezomib)
  • Chronic rejection (months-years): combined immune + non-immune; obliterative arteriopathy, fibrosis; harder to reverse; gradual graft dysfunction;

(2) HLA + immune recognition:
  • HLA Class I (A, B, C) — most cells
  • HLA Class II (DR, DQ, DP) — antigen-presenting cells
  • Direct + indirect alloantigen recognition
  • Crossmatch testing (CDC + flow + virtual) pre-transplant essential
  • DSA (donor-specific antibodies) — risk of AMR;

(3) Immunosuppression categories: a. Induction (peri-transplant):
  • Antibody therapy: anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG — polyclonal T-cell depleting), basiliximab (anti-IL-2 receptor), alemtuzumab (anti-CD52, more depleting)
  • Steroid bolus: methylprednisolone b. Maintenance (lifelong):
  • Calcineurin inhibitors (CNI): tacrolimus, cyclosporine — backbone; nephrotoxicity major side effect
  • Antimetabolites: mycophenolate (MMF, MPA) > azathioprine
  • Steroids: prednisone (often tapered to low-dose or stopped)
  • mTOR inhibitors: sirolimus, everolimus — anti-tumor; SE: poor wound healing, pneumonitis, mouth ulcers, hyperlipidemia
  • Costimulation blockade: belatacept (CD80/86) c. Rejection treatment:
  • Acute cellular: pulse steroid + ATG
  • AMR: plasmapheresis + IVIG + rituximab + bortezomib/daratumumab
  • Refractory: alemtuzumab;

(4) Side effects of immunosuppression to manage:
  • Infection (opportunistic — CMV, PJP, fungal; bacterial; reactivation HBV)
  • Malignancy (skin, PTLD, increased cancer risk overall)
  • Cardiovascular (HT, hyperlipidemia, DM)
  • Renal (CNI nephrotoxicity)
  • Neurological (tremor, headache, CNI neurotoxicity, PRES)
  • Hematologic (cytopenias)
  • Metabolic (NODAT — new-onset DM after transplant)
  • Bone (osteoporosis from steroid);

(5) Therapeutic drug monitoring: tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus, mycophenolate levels;

(6) Tolerance — holy grail: mixed chimerism, regulatory T cells, donor BM transplant + organ — experimental; clinical trials ongoing;

(7) Long-term: cancer surveillance, infection prophylaxis, cardiovascular risk, drug interactions awareness; adherence support
————————————————
Transplant immunology + immunosuppression — foundational. Types of rejection:

1. Hyperacute: minutes-hours; preformed Ab; ABO + HLA mismatch; thrombosis; graft loss — prevented by crossmatch

2. Acute cellular (TCMR): T-cell mediated; days-months; treatable with pulse steroid

3. Acute antibody-mediated (AMR): B-cell + Ab; days-months; harder treatment (PEX, IVIG, rituximab)

4. Chronic: months-years; multifactorial; gradual graft loss HLA Class I (A, B, C) all nucleated cells. HLA Class II (DR, DQ, DP) APCs (B cells, macrophages, dendritic). Alloantigen recognition: direct (donor APC presenting donor HLA to recipient T cell) + indirect (recipient APC processing donor antigen). Pre-transplant testing: ABO, HLA, PRA (panel reactive antibodies), crossmatch (CDC, flow, virtual), DSA assessment. Immunosuppression — 3 phases:

1. Induction (peri-op):
  • ATG (T-cell depleting, polyclonal)
  • Basiliximab (anti-IL2R, less depleting)
  • Alemtuzumab (anti-CD52, profound depletion)
  • Methylprednisolone bolus

2. Maintenance (lifelong, 'triple therapy' common):
  • CNI (tacrolimus > cyclosporine) — backbone
  • Antiproliferative (mycophenolate > azathioprine)
  • Steroid (prednisone — often tapered)
  • Alternatives: mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus, everolimus), belatacept

3. Rejection treatment:
  • Acute cellular: pulse steroid + ATG
  • AMR: plasmapheresis + IVIG + rituximab ± bortezomib + steroids Side effect profile (major considerations):
  • Tacrolimus: nephrotoxic, neurotoxic, NODAT, HT, tremor, hair loss, hyperK
  • Cyclosporine: similar + hirsutism, gum hyperplasia
  • Mycophenolate: GI, leukopenia, anemia, teratogenic
  • Sirolimus: wound healing impaired, pneumonitis, hyperlipidemia, mouth ulcers
  • Steroids: HT, DM, osteoporosis, cataract, weight, mood, AVN
  • Belatacept: PTLD risk (especially EBV-naive recipient) Therapeutic drug monitoring: trough levels (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus, everolimus); higher targets early post-transplant, lower later. Tolerance — operational tolerance without immunosuppression: holy grail; mixed chimerism (Stanford, Mass Gen + others) achieving partial success in selected; not clinical routine yet.
reference_source: AST/ASTS Immunosuppression Guidelines; OPTN/UNOS; Halloran PF NEJM

Transplant rejection mechanisms + immunosuppression:

(1) Types of rejection:
  • Hyperacute (minutes-hours): preformed antibodies (ABO incompatibility, HLA mismatch) → complement + thrombosis

3. Acute antibody-mediated (AMR): B-cell + Ab; days-months; harder treatment (PEX, IVIG, rituximab)

4. Chronic: months-years; multifactorial; gradual graft loss HLA Class I (A, B, C) all nucleated cells. HLA Class II (DR, DQ, DP) APCs (B cells, macrophages, dendritic). Alloantigen recognition: direct (donor APC presenting donor HLA to recipient T cell) + indirect (recipient APC processing donor antigen). Pre-transplant testing: ABO, HLA, PRA (panel reactive antibodies), crossmatch (CDC, flow, virtual), DSA assessment. Immunosuppression — 3 phases:

1. Induction (peri-op):
  • ATG (T-cell depleting, polyclonal)
  • Basiliximab (anti-IL2R, less depleting)
  • Alemtuzumab (anti-CD52, profound depletion)
  • Methylprednisolone bolus

2. Maintenance (lifelong, 'triple therapy' common):
  • CNI (tacrolimus > cyclosporine) — backbone
  • Antiproliferative (mycophenolate > azathioprine)
  • Steroid (prednisone — often tapered)
  • Alternatives: mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus, everolimus), belatacept

3. Rejection treatment:
  • Acute cellular: pulse steroid + ATG
  • AMR: plasmapheresis + IVIG + rituximab ± bortezomib + steroids Side effect profile (major considerations):
  • Tacrolimus: nephrotoxic, neurotoxic, NODAT, HT, tremor, hair loss, hyperK
  • Cyclosporine: similar + hirsutism, gum hyperplasia
  • Mycophenolate: GI, leukopenia, anemia, teratogenic
  • Sirolimus: wound healing impaired, pneumonitis, hyperlipidemia, mouth ulcers
  • Steroids: HT, DM, osteoporosis, cataract, weight, mood, AVN
  • Belatacept: PTLD risk (especially EBV-naive recipient) Therapeutic drug monitoring: trough levels (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus, everolimus); higher targets early post-transplant, lower later. Tolerance — operational tolerance without immunosuppression: holy grail; mixed chimerism (Stanford, Mass Gen + others) achieving partial success in selected; not clinical routine yet.
