scenario:
Basic science: medical genetics + hereditary cancer syndromes

คำถาม: hereditary cancer syndromes + surgical implications
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Hereditary cancer syndromes — surgical implications:

(1) HBOC (Hereditary Breast Ovarian Cancer) — BRCA1/2:
  • Lifetime breast cancer 45-72% (BRCA1) / 45-69% (BRCA2); ovarian 39-44% / 11-17%
  • Risk-reducing mastectomy (RRM) — reduces breast Ca 90%+
  • Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) — at 35-40 (BRCA1) / 40-45 (BRCA2) after childbearing; reduces ovarian Ca > 80% + breast Ca ~ 50% (if premenopausal)
  • PARP inhibitors (olaparib, talazoparib) — adjuvant high-risk + metastatic
  • Increased pancreatic + prostate (BRCA2 esp), male breast (BRCA2), melanoma (BRCA2);

(2) Lynch Syndrome (HNPCC) — MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM:
  • Colorectal lifetime 50-80%
  • Endometrial 40-60%, ovarian, gastric, small bowel, urothelial, brain, sebaceous skin (Muir-Torre)
  • Surveillance: colonoscopy q1-2 yr from 20-25; gynecologic surveillance; consider prophylactic hysterectomy + BSO after childbearing
  • Total colectomy with IRA if cancer diagnosed (synchronous + metachronous risk)
  • Immunotherapy (anti-PD-1 — pembrolizumab) for MSI-H/dMMR cancers — striking responses;

(3) FAP (Familial Adenomatous Polyposis) — APC gene:
  • Colorectal cancer near 100% if untreated by 35-45
  • Prophylactic total colectomy (with IRA — ileorectal anastomosis) or proctocolectomy with IPAA
  • Duodenal adenoma + cancer surveillance (EGD with side-viewer)
  • Desmoid tumor risk (especially attenuated FAP)
  • Thyroid Ca, brain tumor (Turcot variant), hepatoblastoma, osteoma, fibroma;

(4) MEN1 (Menin):
  • Parathyroid (primary HPT)
  • Pituitary adenoma
  • Pancreatic neuroendocrine (gastrinoma, insulinoma, others)
  • Adrenal, foregut carcinoid
  • Genetic counseling + screening
  • Parathyroidectomy + pancreaticoduodenectomy timing case-by-case;

(5) MEN2 (RET):
  • MEN2A: medullary thyroid Ca (MTC) + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid
  • MEN2B: MTC + pheo + mucosal neuromas, marfanoid
  • Familial MTC only
  • Prophylactic thyroidectomy in MEN2A by 5 yr, MEN2B by 1 yr
  • Pheochromocytoma screening lifelong;

(6) VHL (Von Hippel-Lindau): clear cell renal carcinoma, hemangioblastoma (CNS, retina), pheochromocytoma, pancreatic NET, endolymphatic sac tumor — multi-system surveillance + intervention;

(7) NF1 (Neurofibromatosis 1): neurofibromas, MPNST risk, pheochromocytoma, optic glioma, etc.;

(8) NF2: vestibular schwannomas bilateral, meningioma;

(9) TP53 (Li-Fraumeni): sarcoma, breast, brain, adrenocortical, leukemia — multi-cancer; intensive surveillance (whole-body MRI);

(10) CDH1 (Hereditary Diffuse Gastric Ca): gastric (diffuse-type) + lobular breast — prophylactic total gastrectomy considered + breast surveillance/RRM;

(11) PTEN (Cowden): breast, thyroid, endometrial, renal, colon, melanoma — surveillance + selective intervention;

(12) STK11 (Peutz-Jeghers): GI hamartomatous polyps, breast, ovarian, pancreatic, cervical Ca;

(13) Genetic counseling + cascade testing of family — essential
————————————————
Hereditary cancer syndromes — ~ 5-10% of all cancers; genetic testing increasingly accessible (NGS multi-gene panels). Each syndrome has specific cancer profile + surveillance + risk-reduction options. NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment — detailed criteria for testing. Increasingly: 'all-comer' testing in certain cancers (pancreatic, ovarian, triple-negative breast, prostate metastatic) regardless of family history. Major syndromes (described above) — surgical implications:

1. HBOC (BRCA1/2): RRM, RRSO

2. Lynch: extensive colon resection if cancer; prophylactic hysterectomy + BSO after childbearing; intensive surveillance

3. FAP: prophylactic colectomy mandatory by 20s; subtotal with IRA vs total proctocolectomy + IPAA; duodenal surveillance

4. MEN1: pancreatic + parathyroid surgery timing

5. MEN2: prophylactic thyroidectomy in childhood; pheo workup before any surgery

6. CDH1: prophylactic total gastrectomy considered; breast surveillance/RRM

7. VHL: intervention for selected RCCs (nephron-sparing), hemangioblastoma, pheo

8. Li-Fraumeni: cancer-by-cancer; intensive surveillance whole-body MRI Genetic counseling principles:
  • Pre-test counseling: implications, limitations, alternatives
  • Variant interpretation (pathogenic, likely pathogenic, VUS, benign) — VUS management challenging
  • Cascade testing of family — important for risk stratification
  • Reproductive options (PGT — preimplantation genetic testing)
  • Psychosocial + insurance considerations (GINA US protects against insurance discrimination — health, not life or long-term care) Polygenic risk scores (PRS) — emerging for common cancers; combines multiple low-penetrance SNPs; may augment surveillance decisions; not standard yet. Multi-gene panel testing — practical efficiency vs single-gene; identifies syndromes beyond what's clinically suspected; manage incidental findings.
reference_source: NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment; ACMG Practice Guidelines; ASCO Genetic Testing

Hereditary cancer syndromes — surgical implications:

(1) HBOC (Hereditary Breast Ovarian Cancer) — BRCA1/2:
  • Lifetime breast cancer 45-72% (BRCA1) / 45-69% (BRCA2)

scenario:
Basic science: surgical anatomy — abdominal wall + hernias

คำถาม: abdominal wall anatomy + hernia classification
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Abdominal wall anatomy + hernia types:

(1) Layers (outside to inside): skin, subcutaneous (Camper's fascia + Scarpa's fascia), external oblique aponeurosis, internal oblique, transversus abdominis, transversalis fascia, peritoneum;

(2) Rectus abdominis sheath: above arcuate line — anterior (EO + ½ IO) + posterior (½ IO + TA); below arcuate line — all anterior (rectus directly on transversalis fascia + peritoneum) — explains lower abdominal hernias predilection;

(3) Linea alba midline fibrous raphe; linea semilunaris lateral border of rectus; arcuate line (semicircular line of Douglas) ~ midway between umbilicus + pubis;

(4) Inguinal canal anatomy:
  • Floor (posterior wall): transversalis fascia + conjoint tendon (medial)
  • Roof: internal oblique + transversus abdominis (conjoint)
  • Anterior: external oblique aponeurosis
  • Posterior: transversalis
  • Contents: spermatic cord (M) / round ligament (F)
  • Hesselbach's triangle: medial — rectus, inferior — inguinal ligament, lateral — inferior epigastric vessels;

(5) Hernias — definitions:
  • Reducible: contents return to abdomen
  • Incarcerated: stuck, irreducible, no ischemia
  • Strangulated: ischemic, surgical emergency
  • Sliding: part of sac is wall of viscus;

(6) Types of abdominal wall hernias: a. Inguinal:
  • Indirect (lateral to inferior epigastric vessels — through deep ring, follows spermatic cord) — congenital patent processus vaginalis; common in pediatric + young adult
  • Direct (medial, through Hesselbach's triangle) — acquired weakness; older
  • Femoral (below inguinal ligament, through femoral canal) — more in women; high incarceration risk → repair upon diagnosis b. Umbilical — congenital in children (usually closes by 5 yr); acquired in adults (pregnancy, obesity, ascites); repair if symptomatic or > 1-1.5 cm c. Epigastric — midline above umbilicus through linea alba defect; small fat-only often; repair if symptomatic d. Spigelian — at semilunar line below arcuate line; rare; high incarceration risk → repair e. Lumbar (Petit, Grynfeltt triangles) — rare; congenital or acquired f. Incisional — through prior surgical scar (10-20% of laparotomies); repair with mesh; component separation for large defects g. Parastomal — adjacent to stoma; common; complex repair h. Perineal — through pelvic floor; post-pelvic surgery or rare congenital i. Internal hernias (e.g., Petersen's post-RYGB) — no abdominal wall defect, mesenteric defect inside;

(7) Surgical principles:
  • Repair indication: symptomatic, complications, asymptomatic for incarceration-prone (femoral, Spigelian)
  • Mesh > primary suture in adults for lower recurrence
  • Pediatric: high ligation, NO mesh
  • Approach: open (Lichtenstein) vs laparoscopic (TEP, TAPP); robotic;
  • Component separation (anterior — Ramirez; posterior — TAR transversus abdominis release) for large complex ventral hernias
  • Mesh placement: onlay, sublay (preferred for ventral — retro-rectus), inlay (rare), preperitoneal/TEP (inguinal)
————————————————
Abdominal wall anatomy + hernias — surgical staple. Foundation: anatomic layers (skin → subQ → muscles + aponeuroses → transversalis fascia → peritoneum). Key structures: rectus sheath (varies above/below arcuate line), inguinal canal, linea alba, linea semilunaris, Hesselbach's triangle. Hernia types:

1. Inguinal (most common): indirect (lateral to epigastric vessels), direct (medial, Hesselbach), femoral (below inguinal ligament — repair always, high incarceration)

2. Umbilical (children + adults)

3. Epigastric (midline above umbilicus)

4. Spigelian (semilunar line — high incarceration)

5. Lumbar (rare)

6. Incisional (10-20% post-laparotomy)

7. Parastomal (adjacent to stoma)

8. Perineal

9. Internal hernias (mesenteric defects, e.g., post-RYGB Petersen's) Surgical approaches:
  • Open Lichtenstein (tension-free mesh) — gold standard inguinal
  • Laparoscopic TEP (totally extraperitoneal) or TAPP (transabdominal preperitoneal) — equal/better outcomes (chronic pain, bilateral repair, recurrent); lower learning curve
  • Robotic — increasing
  • Component separation (anterior Ramirez, posterior TAR) — for large complex ventral
  • Mesh placement options: onlay (top), sublay/retromuscular (preferred), inlay (rare), preperitoneal Modern principles:
  • Tension-free mesh for adult primary + incisional (lower recurrence)
  • Light-weight mesh — less chronic pain
  • Bioprosthetic (Strattice, biological) — contaminated field selectively
  • Avoid mesh in pediatric (growth)
  • Watchful waiting acceptable for asymptomatic minimally symptomatic inguinal in elderly (Fitzgibbons RCT)
  • Repair always for femoral, Spigelian (high incarceration) Complications: chronic pain (10-15%), recurrence (1-5%), seroma, infection, mesh complications (migration, erosion, infection, fistula). Quality measures: AHSQC (Americas Hernia Society Quality Collaborative), EHS standards.
reference_source: International HerniaSurge Guidelines 2018; SAGES Guidelines; Skandalakis Surgical Anatomy

Abdominal wall anatomy + hernia types:

(1) Layers (outside to inside): skin, subcutaneous (Camper's fascia + Scarpa's fascia), external oblique aponeurosis, internal oblique, transversus abdominis, transversalis fascia, peritoneum

scenario:
Basic science: surgical pharmacology + drug interactions

คำถาม: perioperative medication management

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start all medications day of surgery

reference_source: ASA Perioperative Medication Management; ASRA + ASA GLP-1 + SGLT2 Statements 2023; ACCP Perioperative Anticoagulation

detailed_explanation.summary:
Perioperative medication management — specific decisions:

(1) Antihypertensives:
  • ACE-I / ARB: HOLD on day of surgery — intraoperative hypotension
detailed_explanation.reason:
Perioperative medication management — common source of errors; impacts outcomes. Categories (continue, hold, modify):

1. HOLD day of surgery: ACE-I/ARB, sulfonylureas, SGLT2, GLP-1 (1-2 weeks), diuretics (variable), most NSAIDs (elective), warfarin (5 days), DOACs (1-2 days based on procedure), oral hypoglycemics (mostly day of)

2. CONTINUE through surgery: beta-blocker (if chronic), statin, ACE-I/ARB sometimes for HF (newer evidence — controversy), SSRIs, antiepileptics, antipsychotics, immunosuppressants, antiretrovirals, calcium channel blockers, antiparkinsonian (don't withdraw — worsening symptoms)

3. MODIFY: insulin (reduce long-acting + hold short-acting + sliding scale day of), steroids (stress dose if HPA-suppressed) Key specifics — recent updates:
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: HOLD 3-4 days pre-op due to euglycemic DKA risk (FDA warning + clinical case series)
  • GLP-1 agonists: HOLD 1 week for daily formulations, 2 weeks for weekly (semaglutide, dulaglutide, tirzepatide) due to delayed gastric emptying + aspiration risk (ASA + ASRA 2023 guidance)
  • Beta-blocker: continue if chronic; DO NOT INITIATE NEW perioperatively (POISE trial NEJM 2008 — increased death + stroke)
  • Aspirin: continue except for high-bleed surgery (intracranial, posterior chamber eye, transurethral prostate); POISE-2 supported continuation generally Steroid stress dose: traditional approach for chronic ≥ 20 mg prednisone or equivalent × 3 weeks within last year — debated; modern approach: hydrocortisone 50-100 mg pre-op + post-op taper; some prefer simpler 'continue usual dose' approach with monitoring (Marik + others suggested over-treatment historically). Herbal supplements: many affect bleeding (gingko, garlic, ginseng, ginger), sedation (kava, valerian), HPA (ginseng, licorice), drug interactions (St. John's wort) — hold 1-2 weeks pre-op. Anesthesia + drug interactions: MAOI + sympathomimetic + meperidine (serotonin syndrome) — hold MAOI 2 weeks if possible; antiretroviral + opioid metabolism considerations; pharmacist consultation for polypharmacy. Reconciliation at admission + discharge — important to prevent omission + duplication. Patient education — clear instructions for what to take/not take morning of surgery.

scenario:
Basic science: trauma evaluation — primary + secondary survey

คำถาม: ATLS principles + trauma evaluation

choices.label: B
choices.text: ATLS (Advanced Trauma Life Support) — systematic approach

reference_source: ATLS 10th Edition; ACS Committee on Trauma Resources for Optimal Care

detailed_explanation.summary:
ATLS (Advanced Trauma Life Support) — systematic approach:

(1) Pre-arrival: communication with EMS, team activation, prepare equipment, blood products, OR notification
detailed_explanation.reason:
ATLS — global standard for trauma initial evaluation + management. Systematic ABCDE primary survey identifies + treats life threats simultaneously; secondary survey for complete injury identification; tertiary survey within 24h for missed injuries (5-10% miss rate). Primary survey life-threats:
  • Airway: obstruction → maneuvers → airway adjuncts → intubation → surgical airway
  • Breathing: tension pneumothorax (decompress), open pneumothorax (3-sided dressing + chest tube), massive hemothorax (chest tube; > 1500 mL or > 200 mL/h × 4h → OR), flail chest (PPV if respiratory failure), cardiac tamponade (pericardiocentesis + thoracotomy)
  • Circulation: external bleeding control (pressure, tourniquet, hemostatic), source identification (FAST, CXR, pelvis), IV access (2 large bore), balanced resuscitation, TXA, MTP, calcium
  • Disability: GCS, pupils, gross motor
  • Exposure: undress, log roll, rewarm Adjuncts: FAST/eFAST, CXR, pelvis, ECG, ABG, lactate, type + cross, Foley (if no urethral injury), NG. Pelvic binder for unstable pelvic fracture before any imaging — temporizes hemorrhage. REBOA (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of Aorta) — emerging adjunct for non-compressible torso hemorrhage in extremis — Zone 1 (above diaphragm) or Zone 3 (below renal); bridge to definitive control. Damage control resuscitation (DCR): balanced products 1:1:1 (PROPPR), permissive hypotension (no TBI), TXA early (CRASH-2 NEJM 2010 within 3h; CRASH-3 NEJM 2019 within 3h of mild-moderate TBI), avoid lethal triad (coagulopathy + hypothermia + acidosis), calcium replacement. Damage control surgery (DCS): abbreviated initial operation — control hemorrhage + contamination, temporary closure → ICU resuscitation → definitive 24-48h. Trauma center matters: outcomes 20-25% better in dedicated trauma centers (MacKenzie NEJM 2006). Special populations: pediatric (weight-based, family centered), geriatric (under-triage risk, anticoagulation, lower threshold to trauma center, modified Frailty Index), pregnant (resuscitate mom first, left lateral tilt, Rh status), anticoagulated (reverse early).

scenario:
Basic science: ICU + nutritional support

คำถาม: critical care nutrition principles
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Critical Care Nutrition (ASPEN/SCCM Guidelines):

(1) Initiation timing:
  • Early enteral nutrition (EN) within 24-48h of ICU admission if hemodynamically stable + no contraindication
  • Parenteral nutrition (PN) only if EN contraindicated / insufficient ≥ 7 days (no benefit + harm with early PN — EPaNIC trial);

(2) Routes:
  • EN: NG, NJ (post-pyloric — useful in gastroparesis, high aspiration risk, pancreatitis), gastrostomy, jejunostomy
  • PN: central (peripheral if short-term + low osmolarity);

(3) Caloric target:
  • Early phase (acute, days 1-3): permissive underfeeding 50-70% (recent evidence — full feeds may worsen outcomes by overfeeding stressed metabolism)
  • Late phase: full feeds 25-30 kcal/kg/day;
  • Indirect calorimetry gold standard if available
  • Avoid hyperglycemia + overfeeding-associated complications (hepatic steatosis, hypertriglyceridemia, CO2 retention);

(4) Protein: higher need in critical illness — 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day (up to 2 g/kg in burns, multi-trauma, CRRT) — preserve lean body mass;

(5) Composition:
  • Carb: 40-60% of calories
  • Fat: 25-30% (including essential FA — linoleic, α-linolenic)
  • Standard polymeric formulas — most patients
  • Specialty:
  • High-protein
  • Concentrated (fluid restriction)
  • Fiber-containing
  • Renal-specific (low K, PO4, fluid)
  • Immunonutrition (arginine, omega-3 PUFA, RNA nucleotides — Impact, Oxepa) — selected (elective major GI cancer surgery shows benefit; mixed in critical care — controversial in sepsis);

(6) Electrolyte management: monitor + adjust (Na, K, PO4, Mg) — refeeding syndrome prevention;

(7) Micronutrients + vitamins: thiamine (especially before glucose load), vitamin D + others as deficient; selenium, zinc; antioxidants — RCT evidence mixed;

(8) Glycemic control: target 140-180; avoid hyper + hypoglycemia (NICE-SUGAR — strict glycemic control 80-110 increased mortality);

(9) Gastric residual volumes (GRV) — historically used to assess tolerance; recent evidence: NOT necessary for routine EN tolerance check (REGANE trial — outcomes not different with vs without GRV monitoring); promotility (metoclopramide, erythromycin short-term) if intolerance;

(10) Aspiration prevention: head of bed 30-45°, postpyloric feeding if at risk, ETT cuff pressure adequate, monitor for high gastric residuals selectively;

(11) Constipation: laxatives, prokinetics, bowel regimen;

(12) Refeeding syndrome: severely malnourished — phosphate, K, Mg drops + thiamine deficiency → cardiac + neurological dysfunction; prevent: slow advancement + thiamine + electrolyte replacement;

(13) Special populations:
  • Burns: hypermetabolic; high protein + calorie
  • Trauma: aggressive nutrition
  • Sepsis: early enteral, hypocaloric early phase
  • AKI/CRRT: protein loss higher with CRRT (1.5-2.5 g/kg)
  • Liver failure: balanced (don't restrict protein)
  • Pancreatitis: early enteral (NJ tube past leak); reduces complications + mortality
  • Obesity: hypocaloric, high protein (1.5-2 g/kg ideal body weight);

(14) Discharge transition: oral intake, oral supplements, home tube feeding, home TPN if needed; outpatient nutritional follow-up
————————————————
Critical Care Nutrition (ASPEN/SCCM 2016 Guidelines + updates) — important but historically under-prioritized. Principles:

1. Early enteral nutrition (EN) within 24-48h of ICU admission if stable + no contraindication — preserves gut barrier, reduces translocation, immune function, infection

2. Parenteral nutrition (PN) — only if EN insufficient/contraindicated ≥ 7 days; EPaNIC trial (NEJM 2011) showed early PN harmful (over-feeding, infection);

3. Caloric target: phase-dependent — early hypocaloric, advance later

4. Protein target: higher (1.2-1.5 g/kg, up to 2 g/kg in burns/CRRT) — preserve lean body mass during catabolic state

5. Glycemic control: 140-180 (NICE-SUGAR — strict harmful)

6. Gastric residual volumes: not routinely needed (REGANE)

7. Aspiration prevention: HOB 30-45°, postpyloric for high-risk Specialty considerations:
  • Pancreatitis: early enteral (PYTHON trial NEJM 2014; EarlyNJ studies) — reduces complications
  • Burns: hypermetabolic — high protein + calorie
  • Sepsis: early enteral, hypocaloric early
  • Obesity: hypocaloric + high protein per ideal body weight
  • Renal: protein NOT restricted in CRRT (high loss); restrict K, PO4 per renal function
  • Liver: don't restrict protein; balanced Refeeding syndrome: in severely malnourished resuming nutrition → cells uptake K, Mg, PO4 + thiamine deficiency → cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure, neurologic; prevention: slow advance + thiamine pre-feed + monitor electrolytes + replace. Immunonutrition (arginine, ω-3 PUFA, nucleotides):
  • Elective major GI cancer surgery — benefit (Drover meta-analysis Ann Surg)
  • Critical illness/sepsis — controversial; some signal harm with arginine in septic shock; case-by-case Glutamine supplementation in ICU — REDOXS (NEJM 2013) showed harm with high-dose IV glutamine — discontinue routine use. Selenium + antioxidants — mixed evidence in critical care; not routine. Indirect calorimetry — gold standard for energy expenditure measurement when available + feasible; predictive equations (Harris-Benedict, Mifflin, ASPEN) widely used. Discharge transition: oral intake assessment, oral supplements, home tube feeding, home TPN if indicated; outpatient nutrition follow-up.
reference_source: ASPEN/SCCM Critical Care Nutrition Guidelines 2016; ESPEN ICU Guidelines; Casaer MP et al. NEJM 2011 (EPaNIC)

scenario:
Basic science: surgical microbiology — antimicrobial stewardship

คำถาม: antibiotic resistance + stewardship in surgery
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Antimicrobial Stewardship — surgical perspective:

(1) Why stewardship:
  • Resistance increasing globally (WHO priority — top 10 health threats)
  • C. difficile infection (antibiotic exposure major risk)
  • Cost
  • Pharmacy + ID team integration;

(2) Stewardship principles: a. Right drug, right dose, right duration:
  • Narrow spectrum when organism known
  • Broad-spectrum empirically when severe + then de-escalate per culture
  • Dose for site (some abscesses need high dose) + patient (renal, hepatic, weight)
  • Shortest effective duration b. De-escalation when culture + sensitivity available c. Source control — surgical, percutaneous drainage, removal of infected device d. Pharmacokinetic / pharmacodynamic considerations:
  • Time-dependent (β-lactams): time above MIC critical — extended infusion or continuous
  • Concentration-dependent (aminoglycoside, fluoroquinolone): peak/MIC ratio
  • AUC/MIC (vancomycin, linezolid);

(3) Common resistant organisms in surgical patients: a. MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staph aureus):
  • SSI, IV catheter infections, pneumonia
  • Vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid, ceftaroline, tedizolid
  • Decolonization protocols (mupirocin nasal + chlorhexidine bath) reduces SSI b. VRE (Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus):
  • Less virulent typically but limited options
  • Linezolid, daptomycin, tigecycline c. ESBL-producers (Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamase) — E. coli, Klebsiella:
  • Resist most cephalosporins + penicillins
  • Carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem, imipenem), pip-tazo (selected), ceftolozane-tazobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam d. CRE / CPE (Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales, Carbapenemase-Producing Enterobacterales):
  • Limited options: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam, eravacycline, plazomicin, polymyxin (last resort, nephrotoxic) e. Pseudomonas aeruginosa:
  • Intrinsic resistance + acquired
  • Pip-tazo, cefepime, ceftazidime, meropenem, levofloxacin, aminoglycoside, ceftolozane-tazobactam f. MDR Acinetobacter:
  • Carbapenem-resistant common; very limited; colistin, tigecycline, sulbactam, eravacycline g. Stenotrophomonas maltophilia:
  • TMP-SMX first-line h. Candida:
  • Fluconazole (susceptible — albicans, parapsilosis often)
  • Echinocandin (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) — resistant Candida, severe
  • C. auris emerging multi-drug resistant;

(4) High-risk surgical populations: prolonged hospitalization, prior antibiotic exposure, ICU, immunosuppressed, transplant, traveled from endemic regions;

(5) Stewardship implementation:
  • Antimicrobial Stewardship Program (ASP) — required by Joint Commission + Medicare
  • Prospective audit + feedback
  • Formulary restriction + pre-authorization
  • Education
  • Surveillance + reporting
  • Order sets + clinical decision support
  • De-escalation protocols
  • IV-to-oral conversion
  • Allergy delabeling
  • C. difficile prevention bundles
————————————————
Antimicrobial stewardship — central to modern infectious disease + surgical practice. WHO top 10 global health threats includes antimicrobial resistance. Drivers: overuse, misuse, broad-spectrum overreliance, agriculture, lack of new antibiotics. 5 Rs of antibiotic use:

1. Right diagnosis — culture before antibiotic when feasible

2. Right drug — narrow when organism known

3. Right dose — patient-specific

4. Right duration — shortest effective (recent evidence supports shorter courses for many infections; e.g., 5-7 days for uncomplicated pneumonia, abdominal infection if source control adequate)

5. Right route — IV-to-oral conversion early when patient stable + absorption adequate De-escalation — switch from broad to narrow when sensitivity known. Source control — surgical/IR drainage, debridement, device removal — critical; antibiotic alone often inadequate. Common resistant organisms (priority):

1. ESKAPE pathogens — common nosocomial multi-drug resistant:
  • Enterococcus (VRE)
  • Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)
  • Klebsiella pneumoniae (ESBL, CRE)
  • Acinetobacter baumannii (MDR/XDR)
  • Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MDR)
  • Enterobacter species

2. Candida including auris (emerging MDR) New antibiotics:
  • Ceftolozane-tazobactam (Pseudomonas, ESBL)
  • Ceftazidime-avibactam (CRE, KPC)
  • Meropenem-vaborbactam (CRE)
  • Imipenem-relebactam
  • Eravacycline (CRE, Acinetobacter)
  • Cefiderocol — siderophore cephalosporin (CRE, Acinetobacter)
  • Tedizolid (MRSA, VRE)
  • Delafloxacin Stewardship program elements (CDC core elements): leadership commitment, accountability, drug expertise, action (prospective audit + feedback, formulary), tracking, reporting, education. MRSA decolonization (CHG bathing + mupirocin nasal) — reduces SSI + MRSA infections in select surgical populations (cardiac, orthopedic). Allergy delabeling — important: > 90% of self-reported penicillin allergies are NOT true IgE allergies; delabeling enables use of effective + narrow first-line agents. C. difficile prevention: minimize broad-spectrum + duration; contact precautions; hand hygiene (soap > alcohol for C. diff spores); environmental cleaning.
reference_source: IDSA Antimicrobial Stewardship Guidelines 2016; CDC Core Elements; WHO Global Action Plan AMR

(1) Why stewardship:
  • Resistance increasing globally (WHO priority — top 10 health threats)
  • C

scenario:
Basic science: surgical decision-making + evidence-based medicine

คำถาม: evidence-based surgery + clinical practice guidelines
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Evidence-Based Surgery — principles + application:

(1) Hierarchy of evidence (highest → lowest):
  • Systematic reviews + meta-analyses of RCTs
  • RCTs (large, well-designed)
  • Cohort studies (prospective > retrospective)
  • Case-control studies
  • Case series
  • Case reports
  • Expert opinion / textbook (NB: hierarchy not absolute — well-conducted observational with large effect > poor RCT);

(2) Surgical evidence challenges:
  • RCTs in surgery harder than medical (sham surgery ethics, learning curve, surgeon variability)
  • Crossover difficult
  • Equipoise variable
  • Blinding nearly impossible
  • Quality variation
  • Volume + experience effects;

(3) Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPG) — synthesize evidence:
  • GRADE methodology: assess quality of evidence (high, moderate, low, very low) + strength of recommendation (strong vs conditional/weak)
  • Major surgical societies: ACS, SAGES, ASCRS, AAST, EAST, WSES, NCCN, SVS, AHPBA, ESC, etc.
  • International + regional adaptations;

(4) Apply evidence to patient — EBM cycle:
  • Ask focused clinical question (PICO: Patient, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome)
  • Acquire best evidence
  • Appraise validity + relevance
  • Apply to patient (considering values + circumstances)
  • Assess + reflect on outcomes;

(5) Shared decision-making:
  • Patient preferences + values matter
  • Especially for elective / quality-of-life decisions
  • Decision aids (Ottawa, Mayo Choice)
  • Outcomes-focused conversation;

(6) Variation in practice:
  • Substantial unwarranted variation in surgical decisions (Dartmouth Atlas)
  • Quality improvement to reduce variation when evidence clear;

(7) Quality improvement:
  • ACS NSQIP — risk-adjusted outcomes benchmark
  • STS, MBSAQIP, MIPS, others
  • PDSA cycles, FMEA, RCA
  • Audit + feedback (Cochrane evidence supports);

(8) Research integration:
  • Clinical trial enrollment
  • Translational research
  • Implementation science (closing evidence-to-practice gap — Cochrane EPOC);

(9) Critical appraisal:
  • Internal validity: bias, confounding, randomization, blinding, ITT analysis
  • External validity: generalizability to your patient population
  • Magnitude of effect + clinical relevance
  • Cost-effectiveness;

(10) Beyond efficacy — patient-reported outcomes (PROs):
  • Quality of life
  • Functional outcomes
  • Patient satisfaction
  • Long-term implications
  • Capturing what matters to patients
————————————————
Evidence-Based Surgery — synthesis of best evidence + clinical expertise + patient values. Hierarchy of evidence: meta-analysis > RCT > cohort > case-control > case series > case report > expert opinion. Not absolute — methodologic quality + applicability also matter. GRADE methodology (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development + Evaluation) — used by WHO + many guidelines: assess quality (high → moderate → low → very low) + strength (strong / conditional) of recommendations. Surgical research challenges:
  • RCT difficult (sham surgery ethics, learning curve, surgeon variability, blinding hard)
  • Observational + registry data often important
  • IDEAL framework (Idea → Development → Exploration → Assessment → Long-term study) for surgical innovation evaluation EBM cycle (PICO + 5 A's):

1. Ask focused question (PICO)

2. Acquire evidence

3. Appraise critically

4. Apply to patient

5. Assess + reflect Shared decision-making:
  • Patient values + preferences part of EBM definition
  • Decision aids
  • Especially important: elective, quality-of-life, preference-sensitive conditions (e.g., BPH treatment, prostate cancer, knee replacement, bariatric surgery) Critical appraisal:
  • Internal validity: randomization, allocation concealment, blinding, ITT, attrition
  • External validity: generalizability
  • Effect size + confidence interval
  • NNT (number needed to treat)
  • Cost-effectiveness Major guideline organizations: ACS, SAGES, ASCRS, AAST, EAST, WSES, NCCN, SVS, AHPBA, ESC, ESVS, AHA/ACC, ATS, RCOG, ACOG, AAP. Quality improvement:
  • ACS NSQIP — gold standard for surgical outcomes benchmarking
  • STS Database (cardiothoracic)
  • MBSAQIP (bariatric)
  • MIPS (Medicare quality reporting)
  • Process measures (SCIP, WHO checklist)
  • Outcomes measures
  • Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) Implementation science: closing evidence-practice gap — getting proven interventions into routine practice. Cochrane EPOC (Effective Practice + Organization of Care). Volume-outcome relationship: higher-volume surgeons + hospitals → better outcomes for complex procedures (Whipple, esophagectomy, hepatectomy, complex cardiac); regionalization of complex surgery debated.
reference_source: Sackett DL et al. (EBM); Cochrane Handbook; GRADE Working Group; ACS Clinical Congress Education

scenario:
ระบบ EMS / ethics: organ donation

คำถาม: organ donation + end-of-life
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Organ donation — modern approach:

(1) Types of donation: a. Deceased donor:
  • Donation after brain death (DBD) — most common
  • Donation after circulatory death (DCD) — increasing globally
  • Tissue donation — corneas, skin, bone, heart valves — wide donor pool (vs solid organ stricter); b. Living donor — kidney (most common), liver lobe, lung lobe, small bowel segment, pancreas segment;

(2) Brain death determination:
  • Clinical criteria (irreversible cessation of all brain function):
  • Cause of coma known + irreversible
  • Absence of confounders (hypothermia, drugs, metabolic)
  • Coma without response
  • Absent brainstem reflexes (pupillary, corneal, vestibulo-ocular, gag, cough)
  • Apnea test positive (no respiratory drive with PaCO2 > 60)
  • Confirmatory tests if needed (EEG, cerebral blood flow study, brain death angiogram);

(3) DCD (Donation after Circulatory Death):
  • Donor not brain-dead but has irrecoverable injury + decision to withdraw life support
  • Withdrawal in OR or ICU; declaration of death after circulatory cessation (5 min asystole typical)
  • Warm ischemia time minimized for organ recovery
  • Expanding donor pool — outcomes good in selected (kidney, liver, lung, heart with NRP — normothermic regional perfusion);

(4) Organ procurement organizations (OPO):
  • Coordinate donor evaluation + family approach + organ allocation
  • Independent from medical team caring for potential donor (separation of duties);

(5) Family approach — best practice:
  • Compassionate, after death declaration / family understanding of prognosis
  • Specialized requesters (OPO coordinators) trained
  • Right to refuse respected; not coerced
  • Discuss patient's wishes (donor card, advance directive, organ donor registry);

(6) Consent:
  • Opt-in (most countries — US) — explicit consent required
  • Opt-out (presumed consent — Spain, Austria, others) — higher donor rates
  • Family may override registered donor wishes (varies by jurisdiction);

(7) Allocation:
  • UNOS / OPTN in US (similar systems globally — Eurotransplant, Australia/NZ ANZDATA, etc.)
  • Algorithms balance: medical urgency, time on list, blood type/HLA matching, geographic distance, pediatric priority
  • Special considerations: HCC exception (MELD), Status 1A for fulminant liver failure, etc.;

(8) Living donor:
  • Evaluation: medical, psychological, social, financial
  • Donor advocate / independent assessment
  • Risks: surgical complications, long-term (kidney function decline modest, but lifelong follow-up needed)
  • Donor protection — primary goal;

(9) Special situations:
  • Anencephalic newborn donation — controversial
  • Death by neurologic criteria in children
  • Pregnant donor;

(10) Increase donation:
  • Public education + awareness
  • Family discussions
  • Donor registry
  • Required request laws
  • Routine notification of OPO
  • Donor management protocols;

(11) Ethics: separation of donor + recipient care teams, dead donor rule, no payment for organs (with limited exceptions in some countries), pediatric considerations
————————————————
Organ donation — central to transplantation; complex ethical, legal, medical framework. Types:

1. Deceased donor:
  • DBD (Donation after Brain Death)
  • most common
  • DCD (Donation after Circulatory Death)
  • increasing

2. Living donor: kidney (most common), liver lobe, lung lobe, others

3. Tissue donation: corneas, skin, bone, heart valves Brain death (death by neurologic criteria):
  • Cause of coma known + irreversible
  • Confounders excluded (hypothermia, drugs, severe metabolic)
  • Clinical exam: coma, absent brainstem reflexes, positive apnea test
  • Confirmatory studies as needed (EEG, cerebral blood flow, angiogram)
  • Uniform Determination of Death Act (US) + similar laws globally DCD:
  • Maastricht categories I-V (controlled vs uncontrolled)
  • Withdrawal of life support → asystole → declaration of death (typically 5 min observation) → rapid organ recovery
  • Warm ischemia time critical for organ viability
  • Expanding pool — outcomes good for kidney + liver; recently good for heart + lung with NRP (Normothermic Regional Perfusion) Living donation:
  • Kidney most common (~ 30-40% of US transplants)
  • Liver lobe (right or left lobe — adult-to-adult, left lateral segment adult-to-child)
  • Donor evaluation comprehensive — medical, psychological, social, financial
  • Independent donor advocate
  • Long-term donor follow-up + outcomes tracking
  • Slight increase in long-term ESRD risk in kidney donors (still low absolute risk) Ethical principles:

1. Dead donor rule — vital organs only from confirmed dead

2. Voluntary consent — no coercion, no payment

3. Separation of teams — donor care team independent from recipient/transplant

4. Family approach by OPO + sensitive

5. Equitable allocation

6. Respect for autonomy + patient wishes Donor management: aggressive resuscitation post-brain death → optimize organ function for procurement (hemodynamic, hormonal — thyroid, steroid; lung protective ventilation; glycemic control). OPO + OPTN/UNOS (US): coordinate, allocate, regulate. Increasing donation strategies: opt-out systems (Spain has highest donor rates globally), public education, family discussions, donor registry awareness, required request laws, hospital protocols. Newer: extended criteria donor (ECD), hepatitis C+ donor to + recipient with subsequent DAA cure, paired exchange (living kidney swap), domino transplant.
reference_source: UNOS/OPTN Policies; Uniform Determination of Death Act; AAN Brain Death Criteria 2010 + 2023 Update; AST/ASTS

scenario:
EMS: surgical workforce + burnout

คำถาม: physician wellness + burnout in surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physician wellness + burnout in surgery — critical professional issue

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Physician wellness + burnout in surgery — critical professional issue:

(1) Definition (Maslach Burnout Inventory):
  • Emotional exhaustion
  • Depersonalization (cynicism toward patients)
  • Reduced personal accomplishment;

(2) Prevalence:
  • Surgeons 40-60% in US studies (varies by specialty + cohort)
  • General surgeons high; emergency, critical care, oncology elevated
  • Residents particularly vulnerable;

(3) Consequences:
  • Personal: depression, anxiety, substance use, suicide (physicians higher rate than general population), divorce, decreased life satisfaction
  • Professional: medical errors (~ 2× increased), patient safety, decreased patient satisfaction, decreased empathy, turnover, early retirement, decreased career engagement
  • Healthcare system: increased costs, decreased quality, workforce shortage;

(4) Drivers:
  • System: EHR burden, productivity demands, administrative tasks, regulatory burden, malpractice concerns, RVU pressure, work-hour intensity, on-call burden
  • Workplace culture: hierarchical, blame culture, lack of autonomy, inadequate teamwork
  • Individual: maladaptive coping, perfectionism, work-life imbalance, social isolation, financial stress;

(5) Prevention + intervention — multilevel: a. Individual strategies:
  • Self-care: sleep, exercise, nutrition, hobbies, relationships
  • Mindfulness, meditation, CBT, professional therapy
  • Resilience training
  • Peer support, mentorship
  • Professional coaching
  • Setting limits, learning to say no; b. Organizational/system:
  • Reduce administrative burden (scribes, voice recognition)
  • Improve EHR usability
  • Wellness officers (Chief Wellness Officer increasing in academic centers)
  • Schedule design (limit on-call burden, flexible scheduling)
  • Peer support programs
  • Mental health services (low barrier, confidentiality protected)
  • Just culture (vs blame)
  • Leadership training
  • Compensation models that don't solely reward volume; c. Professional/cultural:
  • Destigmatize seeking help
  • Mental health resources for clinicians (e.g., RISE — Resilience in Stressful Events at Hopkins; Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation; ACS Surgeon Wellbeing)
  • Peer support after adverse events (second victim phenomenon)
  • Bereavement support after patient death
  • Professional society initiatives;

(6) Surgeon-specific challenges:
  • Long training (5-10+ years)
  • Sleep deprivation
  • Adverse outcomes psychological impact
  • Lawsuit risk
  • Demanding patient care + family demands
  • Identity tied to work;

(7) Special populations:
  • Trainees (high burnout, hidden curriculum exposure)
  • Women + minority physicians (often face additional stressors — discrimination, harassment, work-family balance)
  • Mid-career
  • Late career;

(8) Wellness as patient safety + quality issue — increasingly recognized; not just individual responsibility;

(9) Mental health crisis among physicians:
  • Suicide rate physicians higher than general population (especially female physicians ~ 2× rate)
  • License restrictions for mental health treatment historically (modernizing — many states now ask only about current impairment vs ever diagnosis
  • help-seeking should be supported)
  • Substance use
  • Depression
  • Anxiety;

(10) Building wellness culture: leadership, accountability, measurement (annual surveys), evidence-based interventions, allocate resources, integrate into strategic planning
————————————————
Physician burnout + wellness — major contemporary issue in medicine + surgery; recognized as quality + safety issue, not just individual challenge. Burnout definition (Maslach): emotional exhaustion + depersonalization + reduced personal accomplishment. Prevalence in surgery: 40-60% — highest in general surgery, EM, critical care, oncology; residents particularly vulnerable. Consequences:
  • Personal: depression, anxiety, substance use, divorce, suicide (physicians 2-4× general population, especially female physicians)
  • Patient: medical errors 2× increased, decreased satisfaction, decreased empathy
  • Healthcare: turnover, early retirement, costs, workforce shortage System drivers (largest factors):
  • EHR burden + administrative tasks
  • Productivity demands
  • Regulatory + malpractice burden
  • On-call + sleep disruption
  • Workflow inefficiencies
  • Inadequate staffing
  • Culture (hierarchical, blame) Multilevel intervention:

1. Individual: self-care, mindfulness, CBT, peer support, professional help-seeking, boundary-setting

2. Organizational/system:
  • Reduce EHR + admin burden
  • Schedule + workload optimization
  • Wellness officers + programs
  • Mental health services (confidential, low-barrier)
  • Peer support programs
  • Just culture
  • Leadership training
  • Recognition + appreciation

3. Professional/societal: destigmatize, mental health licensure reform, peer support, professional society initiatives (ACS, AMA, surgical specialty societies) Second victim phenomenon: clinicians distressed by patient adverse events — need debriefing, peer support, mental health resources (RISE program Hopkins, etc.). Mental health licensure: historically asked about ever diagnosis or treatment → barrier to seeking help; modernizing — questions should focus on current impairment, not history of treatment (American Medical Association advocates; FSMB updated guidance). Trainee burnout: very high rates; ACGME duty hour limits, wellness curricula, mental health resources, harassment + mistreatment policies (hidden curriculum exposure significant issue). Quadruple aim — original Triple Aim (better care, better health, lower cost) expanded to add clinician wellbeing as essential. Quality + safety connection: physician wellbeing correlates with patient outcomes; burnout associated with increased errors, lower patient satisfaction, decreased empathy + adherence to evidence — wellness is patient safety issue. Special populations — women, minority, LGBTQ+ physicians often face additional stressors (discrimination, harassment, work-family expectations).
reference_source: ACS Surgeon Wellbeing Resources; National Academy of Medicine Action Collaborative on Clinician Wellbeing; Maslach C et al.

  • Reduced personal accomplishment

scenario:
EMS / quality: telesurgery + technology

คำถาม: surgical innovation + emerging technology

choices.label: E
choices.text: No training needed for new tech

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Surgical innovation + emerging technology:

(1) Robotic surgery (current state):
  • da Vinci dominant — multiple procedures (urologic, gynecologic, general, cardiac selected, head + neck)
  • Versius (CMR), Hugo (Medtronic), Senhance, Stereotaxis — emerging competitors
  • Benefits: enhanced visualization (3D), improved ergonomics, articulating instruments, motion scaling, tremor reduction
  • Disadvantages: cost, training, no haptic feedback, conversion if equipment failure
  • Best evidence: urology (prostatectomy), gynecology (hysterectomy); growing in colorectal, hepatobiliary, thoracic, cardiac selected; LACC trial showed lap inferior to open for radical hysterectomy cervical cancer — surprising, prompted reconsideration of MIS for cervical Ca;

(2) Imaging + navigation:
  • Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence — perfusion, lymph node, biliary, tumor margin
  • 3D reconstruction + virtual reality pre-op planning
  • Augmented reality intra-op overlay
  • Hyperspectral imaging
  • Cone-beam CT intra-op;

(3) Artificial Intelligence + machine learning:
  • Image interpretation (pathology, radiology) — FDA-approved AI for some
  • Surgical decision support
  • Predictive analytics (risk, complications, recurrence)
  • Computer vision (intraoperative — phase recognition, instrument tracking, anatomy identification)
  • Natural language processing (documentation, charting)
  • Personalized medicine;

(4) Minimally invasive trends:
  • Laparoscopic → robotic
  • Endoscopic procedures (POEM, ESD, EMR, endoscopic anti-reflux, endoscopic bariatric)
  • NOTES (Natural Orifice Translumenal Endoscopic Surgery) — niche;
  • Single-incision laparoscopic (SILS) — niche;

(5) Tissue engineering + regenerative medicine:
  • Bioprinting (cartilage, skin, vascular grafts experimental; whole organs distant future)
  • Stem cell therapy (selected — bone, heart in trials)
  • Decellularized scaffolds (heart valves, vessels, esophagus, trachea)
  • Mesenchymal stem cells for fistula (darvadstrocel for Crohn's perianal);

(6) Diagnostics + biomarkers:
  • Liquid biopsy (ctDNA)
  • Precision oncology
  • Pharmacogenomics;

(7) Telemedicine + telesurgery:
  • Pre + post-op virtual visits
  • Remote second opinion
  • Tele-trauma consultation
  • Telementoring (experienced surgeon guides remote surgery)
  • Telesurgery (true remote surgery — Lindbergh Operation 2001 NY to France; modern: 5G enables; growing in selected countries);

(8) Surgical training innovation:
  • Simulation (low + high fidelity, VR)
  • Video-based assessment (Coaching + feedback)
  • Crowdsourced surgical assessment
  • AI-assisted skill evaluation;

(9) Implants + devices:
  • 3D-printed patient-specific
  • Smart implants (sensors, drug-eluting)
  • Magnetic devices (LINX, anastomosis)
  • Bioabsorbable
  • Wireless monitoring;

(10) Ethical + regulatory considerations:
  • FDA regulatory (510k vs PMA pathways)
  • Learning curve + quality outcomes
  • Data privacy + cybersecurity
  • Equity + access (technology gap)
  • Cost-effectiveness
  • Informed consent for novel
  • Conflict of interest disclosure
  • IDEAL framework for surgical innovation evaluation (Stages 1-5: Idea, Development, Exploration, Assessment, Long-term study)
————————————————
Surgical innovation + emerging technology — rapid evolution; balance enthusiasm with rigorous evaluation. Robotic surgery:
  • da Vinci dominant; new competitors entering market
  • Strong evidence in urology (prostatectomy), gynecology (hysterectomy)
  • Growing in colorectal, hepatobiliary, thoracic
  • LACC trial NEJM 2018 — surprising: laparoscopic radical hysterectomy for cervical cancer INFERIOR to open for OS + recurrence; prompted reconsideration of MIS in cervical Ca + cautioned 'MIS always better' assumption
  • Cost-effectiveness debated for many indications AI + machine learning in surgery:
  • Image interpretation (pathology — Paige.AI, others; radiology — many FDA-cleared)
  • Surgical decision support, risk prediction
  • Computer vision intraoperative (phase recognition, instrument tracking, anatomy ID — Cas Squared, Theator, others)
  • Clinical decision support
  • Documentation NLP (Nuance DAX, Augmedix) Imaging adjuncts:
  • ICG fluorescence — practical, expanding: perfusion (anastomosis, flap), lymph node mapping (sentinel), biliary visualization, tumor margins, parathyroid identification
  • Augmented reality — pre-op planning + intraoperative overlay
  • 3D printing — patient-specific implants, anatomical models for planning Minimally invasive evolution:
  • Laparoscopic → robotic for many
  • Endoscopic procedures expanding (POEM achalasia, ESD early Ca, endoscopic anti-reflux, endoscopic bariatric — sleeve gastroplasty + others) Tissue engineering:
  • Bioprinting still early
  • Decellularized scaffolds (heart valves, vessels — TAVR + TAVI growing)
  • Stem cell + regenerative — selected applications (mesenchymal stem cells for Crohn's fistula — darvadstrocel; others) Telesurgery:
  • Lindbergh Operation 2001 (proof-of-concept)
  • 5G + low latency enabling near-real-time
  • Currently telementoring + tele-consultation widespread; true telesurgery limited but growing Innovation evaluation framework — IDEAL Collaboration (Stages 1-5: Idea → Development → Exploration → Assessment → Long-term Study) — analog to drug Phase I-IV; structured approach to surgical innovation evaluation; rigorous + safe vs unevaluated adoption. FDA regulation: 510(k) clearance (substantial equivalence) vs PMA (premarket approval — higher risk devices); De Novo for novel low/moderate risk; debates about appropriate rigor + post-market surveillance. Equity + access: cost + technology gaps concern; rural + LMIC populations may not benefit equally. Privacy + cybersecurity — increasingly important with connected devices + data.
reference_source: IDEAL Collaboration; ACS Innovation; FDA Surgical Devices

scenario:
Integrative: complex multimorbid elderly

คำถาม: octogenarian with multiple cancers + comorbidities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Octogenarian with multimorbidity — individualized integrative care

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Octogenarian with multimorbidity — individualized integrative care:

(1) Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA):
  • Functional (ADL, IADL, gait, balance)
  • Cognitive (MMSE, MoCA, delirium screen)
  • Psychological (depression, anxiety)
  • Social (caregiver, support, financial)
  • Medical (comorbidity Charlson, polypharmacy STOPP/START)
  • Nutritional (MNA-SF)
  • Frailty (Fried, CFS);

(2) Estimated life expectancy assessment:
  • General health, comorbidities, function
  • ePrognosis tools (Lee, Schonberg) - 'Lag time to benefit' — does intervention's benefit timeline exceed life expectancy? (e.g., colon cancer screening benefit ~ 10 yr → may not benefit those with limited life expectancy);

(3) Cancer-specific considerations:
  • Aggressive vs indolent cancer
  • Treatment goals: cure vs control vs palliation
  • Treatment tolerability based on functional reserve
  • Geriatric oncology (G8, CARG, CRASH tools predict toxicity);

(4) Polypharmacy + deprescribing:
  • Beers Criteria — potentially inappropriate medications
  • STOPP/START
  • Simplify regimens
  • Reassess each medication's continued benefit
  • Common: anticholinergics (cognition, falls), benzodiazepines (cognition, falls, dependence), opioids (cognition, falls, constipation), NSAIDs (renal, GI), statins (debated in very elderly with limited life expectancy);

(5) Goals of care discussion:
  • Patient's values + preferences
  • What outcomes matter (function, independence, comfort, time, family)
  • Trade-offs acceptable
  • Cultural + family dynamics
  • Advance care planning;

(6) Function-focused care:
  • Maintain/restore function as primary goal
  • PT, OT, rehabilitation
  • Home safety + adaptation
  • Assistive devices
  • Fall prevention (multi-component intervention reduces falls 25-30% per meta-analysis);

(7) Cognitive impairment management:
  • Identify (screening, diagnosis, type — Alzheimer's, vascular, Lewy body, frontotemporal, mixed)
  • Cognition + behavioral support
  • Delirium prevention (HELP, ABCDEF bundles, mobility, sleep, hydration, vision/hearing aids, family presence, restraint avoidance)
  • Caregiver education + support
  • Advance care planning while capacity intact;

(8) Multimorbidity management — chronic disease optimization:
  • HF, CKD, COPD, DM, AF, etc.
  • Coordinated care;
  • Avoid disease-specific guideline-driven care that conflicts (e.g., aspirin for primary prevention controversial in very elderly);

(9) Social + caregiver:
  • Caregiver burden assessment
  • Respite
  • Community resources
  • Home health services
  • Long-term care planning (independent living, assisted, skilled nursing, hospice);

(10) Integrated geriatric care models:
  • Geriatric consultation services
  • Acute Care for Elders (ACE) units
  • PACE (Programs of All-Inclusive Care for Elderly)
  • Primary care + geriatric clinics
  • Geriatric trauma + surgery programs (ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification);

(11) Quality of life > quantity often preferred by elderly — should drive decisions
————————————————
Geriatric surgical / oncologic care — individualization paramount given heterogeneity of elderly. Heterogeneity: chronologic age ≠ biological age. Many 80-yr-olds robust, some 60-yr-olds frail. Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) — multidimensional; predicts outcomes better than chronological age alone. Tools:
  • Frailty: Fried, Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS — Rockwood), Modified Frailty Index, Edmonton Frail
  • Cognition: MMSE, MoCA, Mini-Cog, delirium (CAM)
  • Function: ADL/IADL, gait speed (slow < 0.8 m/s independent risk), grip strength
  • Comorbidity: Charlson, CIRS-G
  • Polypharmacy: Beers, STOPP/START
  • Cancer-specific: G8, CARG (chemo toxicity), CRASH score Geriatric oncology principles:
  • Tumor biology + behavior
  • Patient's functional + cognitive reserve
  • Life expectancy estimation (ePrognosis tools — Lee, Schonberg) - 'Lag time to benefit' — does intervention's benefit timeline align with patient's expected longevity?
  • Treatment intensity individualization (full, modified, supportive)
  • Geriatric assessment improves treatment outcomes + patient satisfaction (Mohile et al evidence) Polypharmacy + deprescribing:
  • Beers Criteria — potentially inappropriate medications (PIM) for elderly
  • STOPP/START — Screening Tool of Older Person's Prescriptions + Screening Tool to Alert to Right Treatment
  • Common deprescribing targets: anticholinergics, BZD, opioids, NSAIDs, sliding scale insulin alone, multiple antihypertensives, statin in limited life expectancy + frailty
  • Reduces falls, cognitive impairment, drug interactions, costs Delirium prevention — HELP (Hospital Elder Life Program), ABCDEF bundle, NICE delirium guidelines: orientation, mobilization, hydration, vision/hearing aids, sleep hygiene, minimize anticholinergic + benzodiazepine, family presence, avoid restraints + Foley + restrictive environment. Goals of care + advance care planning: ongoing conversations; documented; portable (POLST/MOLST); honor patient's values. Function-focused care: primary aim — maintain/restore function vs disease cure alone. PT, OT, rehab, home safety, assistive devices, fall prevention (multifactorial intervention works). Models of care:
  • ACE (Acute Care for Elders) units — inpatient geriatric model improves outcomes
  • PACE (Programs of All-Inclusive Care for Elderly) — community-based
  • Geriatric consult services
  • Co-management (orthogeriatrics, geriatric trauma)
  • ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification Program (since 2019) — institutional standards.
reference_source: ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification; ASCO Geriatric Oncology; Mohile SG et al. JCO; ePrognosis tools

scenario:
Integrative: gender-affirming care + surgery

คำถาม: gender-affirming surgical care principles
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Gender-affirming surgical care — multidisciplinary patient-centered:

(1) Terminology:
  • Gender identity vs sex assigned at birth
  • Cisgender (identity matches assigned sex) vs transgender (does not)
  • Non-binary — outside binary
  • Gender dysphoria — distress from incongruence (DSM-5 diagnostic criterion historically required for some insurance/surgical access — moving away from pathologization);

(2) Care framework:
  • WPATH (World Professional Association for Transgender Health) Standards of Care 8 (2022) — global guidance
  • Endocrine Society Guidelines for hormone therapy
  • Multidisciplinary: mental health, endocrinology, plastic/urologic/gynecologic surgery, primary care, voice + speech therapy, fertility specialist;

(3) Components of care (not all individuals desire all):
  • Social transition — name, pronouns, presentation
  • Mental health support
  • Hormone therapy:
  • Feminizing: estradiol + anti-androgen (spironolactone, GnRH agonist)
  • Masculinizing: testosterone
  • Monitoring + complications
  • Pubertal suppression in adolescents (GnRH agonist) — reversible; allows time for decision-making
  • Surgery — multiple options;

(4) Gender-affirming surgeries: a. Top surgery:
  • Masculinizing: bilateral mastectomy + chest contouring
  • Feminizing: breast augmentation b. Bottom surgery:
  • Masculinizing: hysterectomy + BSO, vaginectomy, scrotoplasty, phalloplasty (radial forearm free flap, ALT, others) or metoidioplasty (clitoral release + extension), testicular prosthesis
  • Feminizing: orchiectomy, penectomy, vaginoplasty (penile inversion most common, peritoneal or intestinal alternatives), labiaplasty, clitoroplasty, vulvoplasty c. Facial feminization / masculinization (FFS / FMS): forehead, jaw, brow, rhinoplasty, tracheal shave/augmentation, chin contouring d. Voice surgery — feminizing (cricothyroid approximation, Wendler glottoplasty) — adjunct to voice therapy;

(5) Pre-surgical requirements (WPATH 8 — modernized, less restrictive than prior):
  • Persistent + well-documented gender dysphoria
  • Capacity to consent
  • Age of majority (or pediatric protocols with multidisciplinary team for adolescents)
  • Significant medical/mental health conditions reasonably well controlled
  • 12 months of continuous hormone therapy as appropriate (for some procedures) — though increasingly individualized;

(6) Post-op care:
  • Surgery-specific protocols
  • Long-term care: dilation regimens (vaginoplasty), continued hormone therapy, follow-up
  • Mental health support
  • Continued cancer screening based on anatomy (Pap if cervix remains, prostate if prostate remains, breast if breast tissue remains);

(7) Reproductive considerations:
  • Fertility preservation counseling before hormone therapy or surgery
  • Options: sperm cryopreservation, oocyte/embryo cryopreservation, ovarian/testicular tissue (selected);

(8) Insurance + access:
  • Coverage variable
  • Advocacy + legal protections (Affordable Care Act US protects against discrimination — currently contested)
  • International variation significant
  • Equity concerns
  • Health disparities;

(9) Adolescent care — particular complexity:
  • Pubertal suppression — reversible
  • Hormone therapy — partially reversible
  • Surgery — generally deferred until adulthood, with exceptions (chest surgery sometimes considered earlier with multidisciplinary input + family);

(10) Outcomes: high satisfaction; reduces mental health distress + suicide risk in many studies; ethics + politics ongoing; cultural competency essential
————————————————
Gender-affirming surgical care — increasingly recognized component of comprehensive medical care for transgender + gender diverse individuals. WPATH Standards of Care 8 (2022) — globally recognized framework; updated less restrictive criteria + recognizes diversity of gender identity + expression; multidisciplinary care model; individualized approach. Comprehensive care components:

1. Social transition — supported by mental health

2. Hormone therapy:
  • Feminizing: estradiol (oral, transdermal, injection) + anti-androgen (spironolactone or GnRH agonist; bicalutamide; finasteride for some)
  • Masculinizing: testosterone (injection, gel, patch)
  • Monitoring: hormone levels, hematocrit (testosterone), lipid + LFT, BP, mood, cardiovascular risk
  • Long-term: cancer risks, cardiovascular, fertility

3. Pubertal suppression in adolescents (GnRH agonist — leuprolide, others) — reversible; allows time + reduces distress

4. Surgery — various procedures; not all individuals desire all Surgical procedures:
  • Top surgery: masculinizing (chest masculinization with mastectomy + reconstruction) or feminizing (augmentation)
  • Bottom surgery:
  • Vaginoplasty — penile inversion (most common), peritoneal pull-through, intestinal (sigmoid colon — usually for prior failed PIV, complex; risk of mucus, malignancy long-term)
  • Phalloplasty — radial forearm free flap (gold standard, sensate), anterolateral thigh (ALT), latissimus, fibula osteocutaneous
  • Metoidioplasty — clitoral release + extension (testosterone-hypertrophied clitoris), urethroplasty, vaginectomy
  • Hysterectomy ± BSO
  • Orchiectomy
  • Penectomy, scrotoplasty
  • Facial feminization / masculinization: multiple procedures
  • Voice surgery: cricothyroid approximation, Wendler glottoplasty
  • Hair: electrolysis (genital area pre-vaginoplasty), scalp transplant for masculinizing Pre-surgical evaluation: persistent gender dysphoria, capacity, age of majority (or adolescent protocols), medical/mental health stability, hormone therapy if appropriate. WPATH 8 removed previous requirement of separate mental health letters for many procedures + emphasizes individualized assessment by qualified provider. Post-op care:
  • Vaginoplasty: dilation regimen (initially several times daily, tapering — lifelong some dilation needed), wound care
  • Phalloplasty: stages (urethroplasty, glansplasty, erectile/testicular prosthesis), complications (urethral stricture, fistula common)
  • Continued hormone therapy + monitoring
  • Mental health support
  • Continued cancer screening based on remaining anatomy: Pap if cervix, breast if tissue, prostate if prostate Fertility preservation: counseling before hormone therapy or surgery; gamete cryopreservation, ovarian/testicular tissue (experimental). Outcomes: high satisfaction in majority; substantial mental health benefit in studies (reduces depression, anxiety, suicidality); regret rates low (< 1-2%) in modern series with appropriate evaluation. Advocacy + access: insurance coverage variable + contested; legal protections vary; substantial health disparities; cultural competency essential for providers.
reference_source: WPATH Standards of Care 8 (2022); Endocrine Society Transgender Guidelines 2017; ACS Statement on Care of Transgender Patients

scenario:
ชายไทยอายุ 30 ปี เพิ่งฟื้นจากภาวะปลายเท้าที่อับชื้น + DM ใหม่ + แผลที่นิ้วโป้งเท้า 2 เดือน + ตอนนี้ดำคล้ำ

Exam: 2 cm necrotic ulcer + surrounding cellulitis + foot deformity + decreased sensation; bone palpable through ulcer (probe-to-bone positive)
ABI 0.85 (normal); pulse palpable
Lab: WBC 14,400, CRP 120, ESR 88, HbA1c 11.2, glucose 280
MRI foot: osteomyelitis distal phalanx + soft tissue infection

คำถาม: management of diabetic foot infection
reference_source: IDSA + IWGDF Diabetic Foot Guidelines 2023; ADA Standards of Care; Lipsky BA et al. CID

detailed_explanation.summary:
Diabetic foot ulcer + osteomyelitis — multimodal:

(1) Hospitalization for moderate-severe infection
detailed_explanation.reason:
Diabetic foot infection — major morbidity in DM; affects 15-25% of DM patients in lifetime; 50-70% of non-traumatic amputations are DM-related. Classification (IDSA/IWGDF):
  • Mild — superficial, < 2 cm cellulitis, no systemic
  • Moderate — deeper structures involved, larger area, no systemic
  • Severe — systemic toxicity, severe ischemia Probe-to-bone test — positive (palpate bone through ulcer) has high specificity for osteomyelitis (PPV 90%+ in this clinical context). MRI gold standard for diagnosis. Pathogens: usually polymicrobial — Staph aureus (incl MRSA), strep, gram-negative (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas), anaerobes (Bacteroides). Treatment:

1. Hospital admission for moderate-severe + bone involvement

2. IV broad-spectrum antibiotic per IDSA guidelines; cover MRSA in high-risk (vanco, daptomycin, linezolid) + gram-neg + anaerobes; duration: soft tissue 1-2 weeks, osteomyelitis 4-6 weeks (extended in inadequate debridement)

3. Surgical debridement + culture + biopsy

4. Bone resection / amputation as needed (toe → ray → transmetatarsal → BKA → AKA — preserve as much as possible)

5. Glycemic control (insulin commonly)

6. Vascular assessment + revascularization if PAD; arterial inflow critical for healing

7. Wound care: NPWT, offloading, advanced dressings

8. Multidisciplinary diabetic foot team improves outcomes Charcot foot — separate entity: neuropathic arthropathy, joint destruction, deformity; mimics infection on imaging; immobilization + offloading; avoid surgery during acute phase. Prevention: foot care education, daily inspection, appropriate footwear, regular podiatry, glycemic control, smoking cessation, BP + lipid management.

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 30 ปี post-laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 6 ชั่วโมงก่อน — มา ED ด้วยอาการ tachycardia + abdominal pain + agitation; vital signs: HR 138, BP 88/52, Temp 38.4, lactate 5.4

คำถาม: differential + management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Early post-bariatric tachycardia + hypotension + lactate elevation — multiple potentially catastrophic etiologies

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Early post-bariatric tachycardia + hypotension + lactate elevation — multiple potentially catastrophic etiologies:

(1) Anastomotic leak (most concerning early): gastrojejunostomy or jejunojejunostomy leak — peritonitis, sepsis, multi-organ dysfunction;
  • Diagnosis: CT with oral contrast (rapid + sensitive — extravasation, free air, fluid), upper GI contrast study;
  • High index of suspicion — tachycardia + new abdominal symptoms + lactate / fever after bariatric = leak until proven otherwise - 'Tachycardia is the most sensitive sign of leak' — HR > 120 should trigger workup
  • Treatment: emergent re-exploration (laparoscopic or open) + drainage + repair + drain + NPO + TPN + antibiotic;

(2) Bleeding (intra-abdominal or GI luminal):
  • Stapler line bleeding most common
  • Mesenteric, omental, spleen capsule, trocar site
  • Diagnosis: drop in Hb, CT, endoscopy if luminal
  • Treatment: stable → observe + transfuse; unstable → re-explore + control;

(3) Pulmonary embolism — high risk in bariatric (obesity + immobility + surgery + hypercoagulable); CTPA; treatment: anticoagulation; massive PE → thrombolytics, embolectomy;

(4) Bowel obstruction — early often from anastomotic stenosis or technical issue (less common in immediate post-op vs late internal hernia);

(5) Other: cardiac (MI), sepsis non-surgical (UTI, pneumonia, line), aspiration, rhabdomyolysis (long surgery, positioning);

(6) Initial response: ABC, resuscitation + IV fluid + IV antibiotic broad-spectrum + type & cross + STAT CT abdomen + surgical consult + transfer to ICU;

(7) Re-exploration threshold low: in bariatric, post-op deterioration warrants liberal re-exploration — delay = mortality;

(8) Multidisciplinary: bariatric surgery, anesthesia, ICU, IR, cardiology as needed
————————————————
Early post-bariatric deterioration — life-threatening differential. Key principle: tachycardia after bariatric surgery = leak until proven otherwise. Sentinel signs of anastomotic leak:
  • Tachycardia (most sensitive — HR > 120)
  • Hypotension
  • Abdominal pain (variable in laparoscopic post-op patient — incision pain confounds)
  • Fever
  • Tachypnea
  • Anxiety / agitation
  • Decreased UO
  • Lactate elevation Diagnosis of leak:

1. CT with oral + IV contrast — most sensitive; can detect extravasation, free fluid, free air

2. Upper GI contrast (Gastrografin) — alternative

3. Diagnostic laparoscopy — gold standard when high suspicion + imaging negative Management of leak:

1. Resuscitation (fluid, vasopressor, antibiotic broad-spectrum)

2. Re-exploration: identify leak, repair primarily if early + healthy, drain, omental coverage, JJ tube for feeding

3. ICU monitoring

4. TPN initially → enteral via JJ tube → oral as healing

5. Drain management

6. Antifungal if persistent / mycotic

7. Endoscopic stent for contained / late leak — selected Other early complications:
  • Bleeding (staple line, mesenteric, trocar, spleen)
  • PE (high risk in obese + bariatric)
  • Mechanical bowel obstruction (rare immediate, common late internal hernia)
  • Cardiac, pulmonary, infectious Late complications:
  • Marginal ulcer (gastrojejunal anastomosis)
  • Internal hernia (Petersen's, JJ, transverse mesocolon)
  • Anastomotic stenosis
  • Nutritional deficiency
  • Weight regain
  • Dumping syndrome
  • Cholelithiasis
  • Hypoglycemia (post-bariatric reactive)
  • Bone density loss Prevention of leak: meticulous technique, proper staple line testing (leak test intraop), drain placement (selective), perioperative VTE prophylaxis. VTE prophylaxis essential — bariatric high-risk: mechanical + chemical (enoxaparin or LDUH); some centers use extended prophylaxis 7-10 days postop; consider for obese, prior VTE, immobility. Patient education: signs to return — fever, tachycardia, severe pain, vomiting, dysphagia, melena.
reference_source: ASMBS Position Statement on Anastomotic Leak; MBSAQIP Standards; Aurora AR et al. Obes Surg

detailed_explanation.summary:
Early post-bariatric tachycardia + hypotension + lactate elevation — multiple potentially catastrophic etiologies:

(1) Anastomotic leak (most concerning early): gastrojejunostomy or jejunojejunostomy leak — peritonitis, sepsis, multi-organ dysfunction
detailed_explanation.reason:
Early post-bariatric deterioration — life-threatening differential. Key principle: tachycardia after bariatric surgery = leak until proven otherwise. Sentinel signs of anastomotic leak:
  • Tachycardia (most sensitive — HR > 120)
  • Hypotension
  • Abdominal pain (variable in laparoscopic post-op patient — incision pain confounds)
  • Fever
  • Tachypnea
  • Anxiety / agitation
  • Decreased UO
  • Lactate elevation Diagnosis of leak:

1. CT with oral + IV contrast — most sensitive; can detect extravasation, free fluid, free air

2. Upper GI contrast (Gastrografin) — alternative

3. Diagnostic laparoscopy — gold standard when high suspicion + imaging negative Management of leak:

1. Resuscitation (fluid, vasopressor, antibiotic broad-spectrum)

2. Re-exploration: identify leak, repair primarily if early + healthy, drain, omental coverage, JJ tube for feeding

3. ICU monitoring

4. TPN initially → enteral via JJ tube → oral as healing

5. Drain management

6. Antifungal if persistent / mycotic

7. Endoscopic stent for contained / late leak — selected Other early complications:
  • Bleeding (staple line, mesenteric, trocar, spleen)
  • PE (high risk in obese + bariatric)
  • Mechanical bowel obstruction (rare immediate, common late internal hernia)
  • Cardiac, pulmonary, infectious Late complications:
  • Marginal ulcer (gastrojejunal anastomosis)
  • Internal hernia (Petersen's, JJ, transverse mesocolon)
  • Anastomotic stenosis
  • Nutritional deficiency
  • Weight regain
  • Dumping syndrome
  • Cholelithiasis
  • Hypoglycemia (post-bariatric reactive)
  • Bone density loss Prevention of leak: meticulous technique, proper staple line testing (leak test intraop), drain placement (selective), perioperative VTE prophylaxis. VTE prophylaxis essential — bariatric high-risk: mechanical + chemical (enoxaparin or LDUH); some centers use extended prophylaxis 7-10 days postop; consider for obese, prior VTE, immobility. Patient education: signs to return — fever, tachycardia, severe pain, vomiting, dysphagia, melena.

scenario:
หญิงไทยอายุ 50 ปี ครอบครัวประวัติ medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid disease; ตรวจ genetic testing พบ RET proto-oncogene mutation

คำถาม: management of MEN2A

choices.label: B
choices.text: MEN2A (Sipple syndrome) — RET proto-oncogene autosomal dominant; multi-system surveillance + intervention

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

MEN2A (Sipple syndrome) — RET proto-oncogene autosomal dominant; multi-system surveillance + intervention:

(1) Three classic features:
  • Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — ~ 100% penetrance; calcitonin-secreting; develops young
  • Pheochromocytoma — 50%; often bilateral
  • Primary hyperparathyroidism — 20-30%; usually multiglandular;

(2) Genetic testing — RET mutation analysis:
  • Confirm proband + cascade testing of family
  • Specific codon mutation predicts MTC age + aggressiveness (ATA risk classification):
  • Highest risk (MEN2B): codon 918 (M918T) → MTC < 1 yr
  • High risk: codon 634 → MTC < 5 yr
  • Moderate risk: other codons → MTC variable timing;

(3) Prophylactic thyroidectomy:
  • MEN2B: by 1 yr of age (some advocate within 6 months)
  • MEN2A high-risk codons: by 5 yr
  • MEN2A moderate-risk codons: individualized — calcitonin-guided (basal + stimulated)
  • Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection if calcitonin elevated or palpable disease;

(4) Pre-thyroidectomy — RULE OUT PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA first:
  • Plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive) or 24h urine metanephrines
  • Imaging if biochemistry abnormal (CT/MRI + MIBG)
  • Unrecognized pheo → catastrophic hypertensive crisis during anesthesia - α-blockade then β-blockade if pheo + adrenalectomy BEFORE thyroidectomy;

(5) Adrenal surveillance for pheochromocytoma:
  • Annual screening from young age (5+)
  • Bilateral often (cortical-sparing adrenalectomy in selected to preserve adrenal function);

(6) Parathyroid surveillance:
  • Annual Ca + PTH from teen years
  • Parathyroidectomy if symptomatic / meets criteria
  • Often multi-gland → subtotal vs total parathyroidectomy + autotransplant;

(7) Familial screening + counseling: cascade testing first-degree relatives; genetic counselor; predictive testing children at appropriate age (typically before age of expected MTC onset based on codon);

(8) Lifelong surveillance:
  • Annual calcitonin (post-thyroidectomy — should be undetectable if cured; rising = recurrence)
  • Annual pheo screen
  • Annual Ca/PTH
  • Education re: pheo symptoms;

(9) Recurrent/metastatic MTC:
  • Selpercatinib, pralsetinib (selective RET inhibitors — FDA-approved, dramatic responses in RET-altered MTC + lung Ca)
  • Vandetanib, cabozantinib (older multikinase inhibitors)
  • External beam RT for unresectable
  • Palliative + supportive;

(10) MEN2B distinct features: marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas (lips, tongue, intestinal), ganglioneuromatosis (megacolon); MTC very aggressive — prophylactic thyroidectomy in infancy
————————————————
MEN2 (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2) — autosomal dominant; RET proto-oncogene mutation (10q11.2). MEN2A (Sipple): MTC + pheo + HPT. MEN2B: MTC + pheo + mucosal neuromas + marfanoid + ganglioneuromatosis (NO parathyroid disease). Familial MTC: MTC only. RET codon-genotype-phenotype correlation — guides timing of prophylactic thyroidectomy (ATA risk classification):
  • MEN2B (codon M918T): very high; MTC develops in infancy; prophylactic thyroidectomy by 6-12 mo
  • High-risk MEN2A (codon 634): high; MTC < 5 yr; thyroidectomy by 5 yr
  • Moderate-risk MEN2A: variable timing; calcitonin-guided; typically thyroidectomy when calcitonin rises or by adolescence Critical pre-thyroidectomy step: ALWAYS screen + treat pheochromocytoma BEFORE thyroidectomy (or any surgery) — unrecognized pheo causes catastrophic hypertensive crisis under anesthesia. Pheochromocytoma in MEN2: often bilateral; cortical-sparing adrenalectomy (subtotal) considered to preserve adrenal function; rule out pheo before any surgery on these patients lifelong. Parathyroid in MEN2A: multi-gland hyperplasia; subtotal (3.5 gland) or total with autotransplant. Family screening + cascade testing — essential; predictive genetic testing of children at appropriate developmental age. Recurrent / metastatic MTC:
  • Selpercatinib (Retevmo) — selective RET inhibitor; FDA-approved 2020; dramatic + durable responses (~ 70-80% response rates)
  • Pralsetinib (Gavreto) — similar
  • Vandetanib, cabozantinib — older multikinase inhibitors; less specific
  • External beam RT, palliative
  • Calcitonin + CEA — surveillance markers MEN1 (Wermer) — distinct from MEN2: MENIN gene; 3 P's — Parathyroid (HPT, most common), Pituitary adenoma, Pancreatic neuroendocrine (gastrinoma, insulinoma, others); also adrenal, foregut carcinoid. Pheochromocytoma NOT a feature of MEN1.
reference_source: ATA MEN2 Guidelines 2015; NCCN Neuroendocrine; Wells SA et al. (Selpercatinib LIBRETTO-001)

scenario:
ผู้ป่วยอายุ 65 ปี post-renal transplant 2 ปี on tacrolimus + MMF + low-dose pred; รอบล่าสุด PCR EBV viremia + new neck lymphadenopathy + LDH elevated
