คำถาม: management of PTLD
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) — EBV-driven; multimodal:

(1) Reduce immunosuppression first-line — restores immune control:
  • Reduce CNI by 25-50%
  • Stop antimetabolite (MMF)
  • Maintain low-dose steroid
  • Goal: shrink lymphoma without losing graft
  • Single most important first intervention;

(2) Rituximab (anti-CD20) monotherapy for early lesions:
  • Single agent first per RAR risk-adapted protocol
  • Response 50-60%
  • Tolerable;

(3) R-CHOP or other chemotherapy for:
  • Polymorphic/monomorphic PTLD not responding to RI + rituximab
  • Aggressive disease
  • Bulky disease
  • Risk: graft loss with chemo myelosuppression + immunosuppression reduction;

(4) EBV-specific T-cell therapy — emerging:
  • Cytotoxic T-lymphocytes (CTL) — third-party EBV-CTL banks (Tabelecleucel for EBV-PTLD post-HCT/SOT; FDA-approved 2024)
  • Promising for refractory;

(5) Antiviral therapy — limited evidence; some use ganciclovir/valganciclovir for EBV PTLD (controversial benefit);

(6) Multidisciplinary: transplant nephrology, hematology/oncology, ID, surgery (lymph node biopsy diagnostic);

(7) PTLD types (WHO):
  • Early lesions (hyperplastic — plasmacytic hyperplasia, IM-like) — RI usually sufficient
  • Polymorphic — mixed; usually RI + rituximab
  • Monomorphic — looks like lymphoma (most often DLBCL, Burkitt-like, plasmacytic); needs chemo
  • Hodgkin-like
  • Plasmablastic;

(8) Risk factors for PTLD:
  • EBV mismatch (EBV-seronegative recipient + EBV+ donor — high risk)
  • Pediatric (lower seroprevalence at transplant)
  • Type of organ (intestine + lung highest risk; kidney lowest)
  • Intensity of immunosuppression
  • Use of T-cell depleting induction (ATG, alemtuzumab) — increased risk;

(9) Surveillance: EBV PCR routine for high-risk (q monthly initially); rising → preemptive RI;

(10) Outcomes: variable based on subtype + response; mortality 20-50% historically; improving with modern + EBV-CTL therapy
————————————————
Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) — most common malignancy post-transplant; predominantly B-cell, EBV-driven (~ 70-80% — primary infection or reactivation in immunosuppressed host). Incidence: ~ 1-2% kidney, 3-5% liver/heart, 5-10% lung/intestine, 5-15% pediatric. Risk factors: EBV mismatch (EBV-naive recipient receiving EBV+ organ — primary infection drives PTLD), pediatric (lower seroprevalence), specific organs (intestine + lung > liver + heart > kidney), intensity of immunosuppression, T-cell depleting agents (ATG, alemtuzumab). WHO classification:

1. Early lesions: plasmacytic hyperplasia, IM-like — RI often sufficient

2. Polymorphic PTLD: mixed B-cell populations

3. Monomorphic PTLD: looks like lymphoma — most often DLBCL > Burkitt-like > plasmacytic > others; require lymphoma-like treatment

4. Classical Hodgkin-like PTLD Diagnosis: biopsy + immunohistochemistry + EBV staining (EBER ISH gold standard); PET-CT staging. Management — step-wise (treatment intensity individualized to disease):

1. Reduce immunosuppression (RI) — single most important; restore immune control; balance against rejection; usually reduce CNI 25-50% + stop antimetabolite, maintain steroid

2. Rituximab (anti-CD20) — monotherapy for early/limited disease; well-tolerated; risk-adapted approach (Trappe RR2 trial) — rituximab × 4, then assess; responders continue rituximab; non-responders add chemo

3. R-CHOP or alternative chemotherapy for:
  • Poor risk
  • Bulky
  • Monomorphic
  • Refractory
  • Burkitt-like (DA-EPOCH-R)
  • Plasmablastic

4. EBV-specific cytotoxic T lymphocytes (EBV-CTL) — emerging breakthrough:
  • Tabelecleucel (third-party allogeneic EBV-CTL bank) — FDA-approved 2024 for EBV-positive PTLD after HCT/SOT
  • Autologous EBV-CTL (older approach, requires viable patient samples)
  • Dramatic responses in refractory PTLD

5. Surgery / RT — selected for localized; rare

6. Antiviral (ganciclovir/valganciclovir) — controversial, mostly adjunct Surveillance: EBV PCR monitoring in high-risk (especially EBV-naive recipients first year); rising PCR → preemptive RI or rituximab. Outcomes: improving; varies by subtype + response. Pediatric outcomes better than adult monomorphic.
reference_source: AST PTLD Consensus; Trappe RU et al. JCO (PTLD-1); Prockop SE et al. (Tabelecleucel)

scenario:
Basic science: pediatric surgical anatomy + congenital anomalies

คำถาม: common congenital anomalies + presentations
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Common pediatric surgical congenital anomalies:

(1) GI tract:
  • Esophageal atresia ± TEF — drooling, choking, can't pass NG; types I-V Gross
  • Pyloric stenosis — 2-12 wk, projectile non-bilious vomiting, olive RUQ, hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
  • Duodenal atresia — bilious vomiting day 1, 'double bubble' X-ray (gas in stomach + duodenum proximal), 30% Down syndrome
  • Malrotation ± volvulus — bilious vomiting newborn, corkscrew on UGI = surgical emergency
  • Jejunoileal atresia — bilious vomiting, distention, no distal gas
  • Meconium ileus — distal SBO at birth, microcolon, 80% CF
  • Hirschsprung disease — failure to pass meconium > 48h, distention, biopsy aganglionosis
  • Anorectal malformation — imperforate anus spectrum; associated VACTERL; cloacal in female
  • Necrotizing enterocolitis — preterm, pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, perforation
  • Meckel diverticulum — 'rule of 2's' (2% population, 2 ft proximal IC valve, 2 in long, 2 yr presents, 2:1 M:F); painless GI bleed, intussusception, obstruction; technetium scan;

(2) Abdominal wall:
  • Gastroschisis — right paraumbilical, no membrane
  • Omphalocele — midline, membrane, associations (cardiac, chromosomal)
  • Umbilical hernia — usually close by 5 yr
  • Inguinal hernia — patent processus vaginalis, repair to prevent incarceration;

(3) Hepatobiliary:
  • Biliary atresia — progressive jaundice in infant, conjugated bilirubinemia, light stool, dark urine; Kasai portoenterostomy by 60-90 days improves outcome but most progress to liver transplant
  • Choledochal cyst — types I-V (Todani); cystic dilation of biliary tree; risk cholangiocarcinoma; cyst excision + Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy;

(4) Genitourinary:
  • Posterior urethral valves — boys; bladder outlet obstruction; renal damage; ablation
  • Vesicoureteral reflux — graded I-V; UTIs; surveillance vs ureteral reimplant
  • Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) — orchiopexy by 6-18 mo to preserve fertility + reduce malignancy risk
  • Hypospadias — urethral meatus on ventral; repair 6-18 mo
  • Ambiguous genitalia / DSD — multidisciplinary evaluation; CAH (adrenal hyperplasia) life-threatening;

(5) Diaphragm + thorax:
  • CDH (congenital diaphragmatic hernia) — bowel in chest, pulmonary hypoplasia, PHTN
  • Pulmonary sequestration, CCAM/CPAM — congenital lung lesions; selective resection
  • Pectus excavatum / carinatum — chest wall deformity; Nuss bar repair or Ravitch;

(6) Neck:
  • Thyroglossal duct cyst — midline, moves with swallowing/tongue protrusion; Sistrunk procedure
  • Branchial cleft anomalies — lateral; 2nd most common; excise tract
  • Cystic hygroma / lymphatic malformation — sclerotherapy (OK-432, doxycycline), surgery;

(7) CNS: spina bifida, hydrocephalus, encephalocele, Chiari malformation — multidisciplinary peds neurosurgery;

(8) Cardiac: TOF, TGA, HLHS, AVSD, VSD, ASD — pediatric cardiology + cardiac surgery;

(9) Limbs / spine: clubfoot, hip dysplasia, scoliosis, polydactyly, syndactyly — ortho;

(10) Genetic syndromes common — chromosomal microarray, WGS increasingly considered; multidisciplinary care
————————————————
Pediatric congenital anomalies — diverse surgical conditions; early recognition + appropriate intervention improves outcomes. Key principles:

1. VACTERL association — vertebral, anal, cardiac, tracheoesophageal, renal, limb — screen for all if one identified

2. Trisomy associations — Down (duodenal atresia, AVSD, Hirschsprung), Edwards (multiple anomalies), Patau

3. Prenatal diagnosis — increasing role of imaging + counseling

4. Multidisciplinary teams — peds surgery, neonatology, cardiology, neurology, urology, ortho, genetics, social work

5. Family-centered care — communication, support, decision-making Selected critical conditions:
  • Bilious vomiting in newborn = malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise — UGI emergent
  • Failure to pass meconium > 48h — Hirschsprung, meconium ileus, meconium plug, anorectal malformation, jejunal/ileal atresia, prematurity-related
  • Acholic stools + conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in infant — biliary atresia; Kasai by 60-90 days; most progress to LT
  • Imperforate anus — physical exam at birth; cloacal complex in females; VACTERL workup
  • Cryptorchidism — by 6-18 mo orchiopexy; reduces malignancy risk (still elevated but earlier intervention improves) + preserves fertility
  • NEC — preterm, life-threatening, NPO + abx + supportive; surgery if perforation
  • Intussusception — peak 5-9 mo, lethargy + currant jelly stool + sausage RUQ; air enema reduction
  • Pyloric stenosis — projectile non-bilious vomiting, hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; pyloromyotomy AFTER correcting electrolytes (not emergent surgery) Meckel diverticulum 'rule of 2's' — 2% of population, 2 ft proximal to IC valve, 2 in length, presents by age 2, 2:1 M:F; painless lower GI bleed (ectopic gastric mucosa) most common; intussusception lead point; obstruction; diverticulitis; technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel scan) for ectopic gastric. Biliary atresia: progressive obliteration of extrahepatic bile ducts; presents 2-8 wk; Kasai portoenterostomy (Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy) by 60-90 days for best outcomes; even with successful Kasai, ~ 70-80% eventually need liver transplant. Early recognition critical — surgical timing affects outcome. Choledochal cyst — Todani classification I-V; risk of cholangiocarcinoma (10-20%) — excision + Roux-en-Y reconstruction.
reference_source: APSA Pediatric Surgery Handbook; Coran AG et al. Pediatric Surgery Textbook

3. Prenatal diagnosis — increasing role of imaging + counseling

4. Multidisciplinary teams — peds surgery, neonatology, cardiology, neurology, urology, ortho, genetics, social work

5. Family-centered care — communication, support, decision-making Selected critical conditions:
  • Bilious vomiting in newborn = malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise — UGI emergent
  • Failure to pass meconium > 48h — Hirschsprung, meconium ileus, meconium plug, anorectal malformation, jejunal/ileal atresia, prematurity-related
  • Acholic stools + conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in infant — biliary atresia; Kasai by 60-90 days; most progress to LT
  • Imperforate anus — physical exam at birth; cloacal complex in females; VACTERL workup
  • Cryptorchidism — by 6-18 mo orchiopexy; reduces malignancy risk (still elevated but earlier intervention improves) + preserves fertility
  • NEC — preterm, life-threatening, NPO + abx + supportive; surgery if perforation
  • Intussusception — peak 5-9 mo, lethargy + currant jelly stool + sausage RUQ; air enema reduction
  • Pyloric stenosis — projectile non-bilious vomiting, hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; pyloromyotomy AFTER correcting electrolytes (not emergent surgery) Meckel diverticulum 'rule of 2's' — 2% of population, 2 ft proximal to IC valve, 2 in length, presents by age 2, 2:1 M:F; painless lower GI bleed (ectopic gastric mucosa) most common; intussusception lead point; obstruction; diverticulitis; technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel scan) for ectopic gastric. Biliary atresia: progressive obliteration of extrahepatic bile ducts; presents 2-8 wk; Kasai portoenterostomy (Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy) by 60-90 days for best outcomes; even with successful Kasai, ~ 70-80% eventually need liver transplant. Early recognition critical — surgical timing affects outcome. Choledochal cyst — Todani classification I-V; risk of cholangiocarcinoma (10-20%) — excision + Roux-en-Y reconstruction.

scenario:
Basic science: surgical complications + classification systems

คำถาม: classification of surgical complications + reporting
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Surgical complications — standardized classification + reporting:

(1) Clavien-Dindo Classification — universal language for postoperative complications based on therapy required:
  • Grade I: any deviation from normal post-op course without need for pharmacologic intervention beyond antiemetic, antipyretic, diuretic, electrolyte, physiotherapy (e.g., wound infection treated bedside)
  • Grade II: requires pharmacologic intervention (antibiotic, transfusion, TPN)
  • Grade IIIa: intervention not requiring general anesthesia (bedside drainage, IR procedure)
  • Grade IIIb: intervention requiring general anesthesia (return to OR)
  • Grade IVa: life-threatening complication requiring ICU with single organ dysfunction
  • Grade IVb: multi-organ dysfunction in ICU
  • Grade V: death;

(2) Comprehensive Complication Index (CCI) — Slankamenac et al — sums all complications weighted by severity → single score 0-100; more granular than reporting only most severe; useful for research + comparison;

(3) Definition of specific complications:
  • SSI (CDC NHSN): superficial, deep, organ/space
  • Anastomotic leak — defect in suture line with extravasation
  • Pneumonia — multiple criteria
  • AKI (KDIGO) — Cr rise + UO criteria; staging
  • DVT/PE — confirmed
  • Sepsis (Sepsis-3): organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response
  • Postoperative MI — troponin + symptoms/ECG/imaging
  • Delirium (CAM);

(4) ACS NSQIP — risk-adjusted outcomes benchmarking; standardized data collection: 30-day outcomes (mortality, complications), risk stratification, hospital comparison feedback;

(5) STS National Database (cardiothoracic specific), MBSAQIP (bariatric), TSACO (Trauma Surgery + Critical Care Open), NTDB (trauma);

(6) Outcome reporting principles:
  • Define complications upfront
  • Capture all (under-reporting bias)
  • Risk-adjust for fair comparison
  • Include long-term + patient-reported outcomes
  • Quality of life metrics;

(7) Reporting frameworks:
  • STROBE (observational)
  • CONSORT (RCTs)
  • PRISMA (systematic reviews/meta-analyses)
  • CARE (case reports)
  • IDEAL (surgical innovation);

(8) Internal hospital reporting: M&M conferences, incident reports, quality committees;

(9) External reporting: NSQIP feedback, accreditation (Joint Commission, ACS), CMS measures, public reporting (mixed evidence on impact);

(10) Use of data: quality improvement, individual surgeon feedback, certification + maintenance of certification, research, peer review
————————————————
Standardized surgical complication classification + reporting — essential for quality improvement + research + benchmarking. Clavien-Dindo Classification (Dindo D et al, Ann Surg 2004) — universal language for surgical complications, based on therapy intensity required to manage. Validated, simple, widely adopted globally. Grades I-V as described. CCI (Comprehensive Complication Index) — Slankamenac et al — improvement over reporting most severe complication only; sums all complications weighted by severity (Clavien-Dindo grade × frequency) → 0-100 score. More accurate reflection of complication burden; useful for research comparing groups. Specific complication definitions — standardized definitions improve consistency:
  • SSI — CDC NHSN: superficial (within 30 days, involving skin + subQ), deep (≥ 30 days or up to 90 days with implant, involves fascia + muscle), organ/space (deeper)
  • Anastomotic leak — definition varies by anatomy; consensus statements exist for specific procedures (rectal, esophageal, gastric, etc.)
  • AKI — KDIGO: ≥ 0.3 mg/dL Cr increase within 48h OR ≥ 1.5× baseline within 7 days OR UO < 0.5 mL/kg/h × 6h; staging 1-3
  • Sepsis-3 (2016) — life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection; SOFA score change ≥ 2
  • Septic shock — vasopressor needed for MAP ≥ 65 + lactate > 2 despite fluid
  • MI (4th universal definition) — troponin + ischemia evidence (symptoms, ECG, imaging)
  • Delirium — CAM or CAM-ICU Quality improvement databases:

1. ACS NSQIP — National Surgical Quality Improvement Program — risk-adjusted outcomes; benchmark + feedback; participating hospitals improve over time; > 800 hospitals globally

2. STS National Database — cardiothoracic

3. MBSAQIP — bariatric

4. VSGNE — vascular New England

5. TQIP — trauma

6. NTDB — National Trauma Data Bank Research reporting standards (EQUATOR Network):
  • CONSORT — randomized trials
  • STROBE — observational
  • PRISMA — systematic reviews + meta-analyses
  • CARE — case reports
  • STROCSS — surgical case series
  • IDEAL — surgical innovation
  • TRIPOD — prediction models Risk adjustment — important for fair comparison; ASA class, comorbidities, age, urgency adjust for case mix. Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) — increasingly emphasized: quality of life, functional outcomes, satisfaction; complement traditional clinical endpoints.
reference_source: Dindo D et al. Ann Surg 2004 (Clavien-Dindo); Slankamenac K et al. Ann Surg (CCI); ACS NSQIP

  • Grade I: any deviation from normal post-op course without need for pharmacologic intervention beyond antiemetic, antipyretic, diuretic, electrolyte, physiotherapy (e.g., wound infection treated bedside)
  • Grade...

scenario:
Basic science: cancer screening + prevention

คำถาม: evidence-based cancer screening principles
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Cancer screening — evidence-based principles:

(1) General principles of screening (WHO Wilson + Jungner criteria):
  • Disease has high prevalence + significant morbidity/mortality
  • Recognizable preclinical phase
  • Effective treatment if found early
  • Acceptable screening test (sensitivity + specificity + safety + cost)
  • Adequate facilities for follow-up
  • Cost-effectiveness;

(2) USPSTF + national guidelines for major cancers: a. Colorectal:
  • Average risk: start age 45 (lowered from 50 per 2021 USPSTF); options:
  • Colonoscopy q10 yr (gold standard — both detect + remove polyps)
  • FIT (fecal immunochemical test) annually
  • FIT-DNA (Cologuard) q3 yr
  • Sigmoidoscopy q5 yr ± FIT q3 yr
  • CT colonography q5 yr
  • High risk (family history, IBD, polyposis syndrome): earlier + more frequent b. Breast:
  • USPSTF 2024: biennial mammogram age 40-74 (recently lowered from 50)
  • ACS: annual 45-54, then q1-2 yr 55+
  • High risk: earlier + MRI
  • Self-exam debated (no proven mortality benefit but body awareness) c. Cervical:
  • Pap every 3 yr ages 21-29
  • Pap + HPV co-test q5 yr OR Pap q3 yr OR HPV alone q5 yr ages 30-65
  • Stop > 65 if adequate prior negative + no high-risk
  • HPV vaccination prevents most cervical Ca + others (anal, oropharyngeal) — vaccinate age 9-12 d. Lung:
  • USPSTF 2021: annual low-dose CT for ages 50-80 + 20 pack-year smoking history + currently smoking or quit < 15 yr ago
  • Significant mortality reduction (NLST, NELSON trials) e. Prostate:
  • PSA-based screening — controversial
  • USPSTF 2018: shared decision-making age 55-69; individualized; avoid > 70
  • Mortality benefit modest; overdiagnosis + overtreatment significant
  • Black men + family history — start earlier + lower threshold f. Skin: visual exam selectively (high-risk: family history, prior, immunosuppressed) g. HCC in cirrhosis: US ± AFP q6 mo h. Pancreatic: only in high-risk (family history, hereditary syndromes); EUS + MRI/MRCP — investigational i. Ovarian: no general screening (TVUS + CA-125 not effective in average risk); BRCA+ — risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy considered;

(3) Risk-based modifications:
  • Family history
  • Genetic syndrome (Lynch — q1-2 yr colonoscopy from 20-25, etc.)
  • Personal history of cancer
  • Immunosuppression (transplant — q6-12 mo derm, more frequent other)
  • Exposure (asbestos, radiation, chemicals);

(4) Limitations + harms of screening:
  • Overdiagnosis (detection of cancers that would not have caused harm)
  • Overtreatment
  • False positives → workup harm, anxiety, costs
  • False negatives → false reassurance
  • Cost;

(5) Shared decision-making — especially for PSA + mammography at age extremes + benefit-harm equipoise;

(6) Active surveillance for low-risk indolent cancers (prostate Gleason 6, micropapillary thyroid Ca, low-risk DCIS — emerging) — avoid overtreatment;

(7) Liquid biopsy for early detection — emerging (Galleri MCED — multi-cancer early detection; PATHFINDER study; not standard yet)
————————————————
Cancer screening — selective application of tests to detect cancer or precursors in asymptomatic at-risk individuals. Must meet WHO Wilson + Jungner criteria for valid screening. Mortality-reducing evidence (RCTs + meta-analyses):
  • Colorectal: colonoscopy + FIT — substantial mortality reduction
  • Breast: mammography — modest mortality reduction (more in 50+ than 40s)
  • Cervical: Pap + HPV — dramatic reduction; HPV vaccination prevents
  • Lung: low-dose CT in heavy smokers — NLST 20% reduction; NELSON 24%
  • HCC in cirrhosis: US + AFP — modest benefit; recommended in at-risk Controversial / debated:
  • Prostate (PSA): mortality benefit modest; overdiagnosis substantial; shared decision-making essential
  • Skin: visual exam — selectively high-risk
  • Ovarian: no general screening (PLCO trial — no benefit)
  • Pancreatic: only high-risk (hereditary, syndromes) Risk stratification — modifies screening intensity:
  • Family history
  • Genetic syndromes (BRCA, Lynch, FAP, MEN, others)
  • Prior cancer
  • Exposures (radiation, asbestos, smoking)
  • Comorbidities (immunosuppression, IBD)
  • Lifestyle Screening-related harms:

1. Overdiagnosis — finding cancers that wouldn't cause harm in patient's lifetime (some breast DCIS, some thyroid microcarcinoma, some prostate Gleason 6); leads to overtreatment

2. False positives — additional workup, biopsy harm, anxiety, costs

3. False negatives — false reassurance, delayed diagnosis

4. Radiation (low-dose CT screening) — cumulative

5. Patient burden + cost Active surveillance — emerging paradigm for low-risk indolent cancers:
  • Prostate Gleason 6 — vast majority safe with surveillance vs immediate treatment (ProtecT trial — active monitoring non-inferior to surgery/RT at 10 years for low-risk)
  • Micropapillary PTC ≤ 1 cm — Japanese protocols (Miyauchi, Ito)
  • DCIS low-risk — LORIS/LORD trials investigating
  • Renal small mass — selected HPV vaccination: prevents > 90% cervical Ca + substantial anal, vulvar, vaginal, oropharyngeal Ca; routine ages 9-12 (catch-up to 26+); now WHO-recommended global strategy to eliminate cervical cancer. Multi-cancer Early Detection (MCED) — emerging liquid biopsy (cfDNA methylation): Galleri (PATHFINDER, NHS-Galleri trials); not standard yet; questions about lead-time bias + downstream cascade; promising but evolving.
reference_source: USPSTF; ACS; NCCN Cancer Screening; ESMO Guidelines

Cancer screening — evidence-based principles:

(1) General principles of screening (WHO Wilson + Jungner criteria):
  • Disease has high prevalence + significant morbidity/mortality
  • Recognizable preclinical phase
  • Effective treatment if found early
  • Acceptable screening test (sensitivity + specificity + safety + cost)
  • Adequate facilities for follow-up
  • Cost-effectiveness

scenario:
Basic science: surgical pharmacology — chemotherapy + targeted therapy intro

คำถาม: principles of systemic cancer therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Systemic cancer therapy — major classes + principles

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Systemic cancer therapy — major classes + principles:

(1) Cytotoxic chemotherapy — DNA damage / mitosis disruption:
  • Alkylating agents (cisplatin, cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, oxaliplatin, temozolomide): DNA cross-linking; SE: myelosuppression, nephro, neuropathy (oxali, cisplatin), nausea
  • Antimetabolites (5-FU, capecitabine, methotrexate, gemcitabine, pemetrexed): inhibit DNA synthesis; SE: mucositis, myelo, hand-foot
  • Anti-tumor antibiotics (doxorubicin, epirubicin, bleomycin): topoisomerase II inhibition; SE: cardiotoxicity (anthracyclines — cumulative dose dependent, baseline echo), pulmonary fibrosis (bleo)
  • Mitotic inhibitors (taxanes — paclitaxel, docetaxel, nab-paclitaxel; vinca alkaloids — vincristine, vinblastine): microtubule disruption; SE: peripheral neuropathy (taxanes, vinca), myelo, hypersensitivity (taxanes)
  • Topoisomerase inhibitors (irinotecan, etoposide): SE: diarrhea (irinotecan), myelo
  • Platinums see alkylating;

(2) Targeted therapy — molecular alteration-directed:
  • Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKI) — many; imatinib (BCR-ABL CML, GIST), erlotinib/gefitinib/osimertinib (EGFR NSCLC), sorafenib/sunitinib (multikinase — RCC, HCC, GIST), trastuzumab + tucatinib (HER2), BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib, encorafenib + MEK inhibitor), ALK inhibitors (crizotinib, alectinib, lorlatinib), ROS1, RET (selpercatinib, pralsetinib), NTRK (larotrectinib, entrectinib), KRAS G12C (sotorasib, adagrasib), FLT3, IDH1/2, FGFR, MET ex14 (capmatinib, tepotinib), HER2 (ado-trastuzumab emtansine + others)
  • Monoclonal antibodies: rituximab (CD20, B-cell lymphoma), trastuzumab (HER2 breast/gastric), bevacizumab (VEGF), cetuximab + panitumumab (EGFR colorectal), pembrolizumab + nivolumab + atezolizumab + durvalumab + ipilimumab (immune checkpoint), daratumumab (CD38 myeloma), polatuzumab (CD79b)
  • Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs): trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd — HER2 + HER2-low), sacituzumab govitecan (TROP2), enfortumab vedotin (nectin-4 bladder), polatuzumab vedotin;

(3) Hormone therapy:
  • Breast: SERM (tamoxifen, raloxifene), aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane), fulvestrant, GnRH agonist (premenopausal ovarian suppression — goserelin)
  • Prostate: GnRH agonist/antagonist (leuprolide, degarelix), anti-androgen (bicalutamide, enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide), CYP17 inhibitor (abiraterone)
  • PARP inhibitors: olaparib, talazoparib, niraparib, rucaparib — BRCA+ + others;

(4) Immunotherapy:
  • Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) — PD-1, PD-L1, CTLA-4: durable responses; IRAEs (immune-related adverse events) — colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrine (thyroid, pituitary, adrenal), nephritis, dermatitis, myocarditis (rare but severe); manage with steroid + sometimes biologics (infliximab, mycophenolate)
  • CAR-T (Chimeric Antigen Receptor T cell): tisagenlecleucel + others for B-cell ALL, lymphoma, multiple myeloma; SE: CRS (cytokine release syndrome — tocilizumab), ICANS (neurotoxicity)
  • TILs (tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes): lifileucel — first FDA-approved for advanced melanoma (2024);

(5) Combination + sequencing:
  • Curative regimens (Hodgkin ABVD, DLBCL R-CHOP, testicular BEP, neoadjuvant breast TCHP, etc.)
  • Adjuvant
  • Palliative;

(6) Personalized medicine — molecular profiling guides therapy: tumor sequencing (NGS panels — Foundation, Tempus, MSK-IMPACT), liquid biopsy, biomarkers (MSI, TMB, HRD, PD-L1, HER2, ER/PR, EGFR, etc.);

(7) Toxicity management — supportive care, antiemetics (5-HT3 + NK1 + steroid), GCSF, ESA selective, mucositis care, neuropathy, cardiac monitoring (echo for anthracyclines + HER2 agents), pneumonitis (steroid), endocrine (replacement), infusion reactions
————————————————
Systemic cancer therapy — multiple modalities; molecularly-targeted treatments transformed oncology. Major classes:

1. Cytotoxic chemotherapy — non-specific cell killing (preferentially fast-dividing cells); backbone for many; common SE: myelosuppression, mucositis, nausea, hair loss, neuropathy

2. Targeted therapy — molecularly directed:
  • TKIs (oral, manageable)
  • Monoclonal antibodies
  • ADCs (antibody-drug conjugates)
  • PARP inhibitors (BRCA, HRD)

3. Hormone therapy — breast (HR+), prostate (AR+)

4. Immunotherapy — ICIs (PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA-4), CAR-T, TILs, bispecifics

5. Radiopharmaceuticals — Lu-177-PSMA prostate (PSMAfore, VISION), Lu-177-DOTATATE NET (NETTER-1)

6. Combinations + sequencing based on disease + biology Precision oncology principles:
  • Tumor molecular profiling (NGS, IHC, FISH)
  • Biomarker-driven (MSI-H, TMB-high, HER2+, EGFR mutation, ALK fusion, BRAF V600E, KRAS G12C, etc.)
  • Tumor-agnostic approvals based on biomarker (pembrolizumab MSI-H, larotrectinib NTRK fusion, dabrafenib + trametinib BRAF V600E, dostarlimab dMMR rectal)
  • Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) for monitoring + resistance Common toxicities + management:
  • Nausea/vomiting: 5-HT3 (ondansetron) + NK1 (aprepitant) + dexamethasone + olanzapine emerging
  • Myelosuppression: GCSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim) for neutropenia prophylaxis in high-risk regimens
  • Mucositis: oral care, palifermin selected
  • Peripheral neuropathy (taxanes, vinca, oxaliplatin): often dose-limiting; duloxetine for symptoms; preventive trials largely negative
  • Cardiotoxicity (anthracyclines, HER2 agents, fluoropyrimidines): baseline + serial echo; dexrazoxane selected
  • Pneumonitis (bleomycin, mTOR inhibitor, ICIs): steroid, drug discontinuation
  • Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine, sorafenib, sunitinib): topical care, dose adjust Immune-related adverse events (IRAEs) from ICIs:
  • Colitis: infliximab if steroid-refractory
  • Hepatitis: steroid; mycophenolate if refractory
  • Pneumonitis: steroid
  • Endocrine (thyroid most common, pituitary, adrenal, T1DM): replacement therapy
  • Dermatitis: topical + systemic steroid
  • Myocarditis: rare but severe; high-dose steroid + ATG/IVIG/mycophenolate; cardiology
  • Nephritis, hematologic, neurologic Multidisciplinary involvement essential for IRAE management. CAR-T cell therapy — autologous T-cells engineered to recognize tumor antigen (e.g., CD19 B-cell ALL, lymphoma; BCMA multiple myeloma). Toxicities: CRS (tocilizumab), ICANS (neurologic). Logistical complexity, cost, specialized centers.
reference_source: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines; ASCO + ESMO; FDA approvals

detailed_explanation.summary:
Systemic cancer therapy — major classes + principles:

(1) Cytotoxic chemotherapy — DNA damage / mitosis disruption:
  • Alkylating agents (cisplatin, cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, oxaliplatin, temozolomide): DNA cross-linking
detailed_explanation.reason:
Systemic cancer therapy — multiple modalities; molecularly-targeted treatments transformed oncology. Major classes:

1. Cytotoxic chemotherapy — non-specific cell killing (preferentially fast-dividing cells); backbone for many; common SE: myelosuppression, mucositis, nausea, hair loss, neuropathy

2. Targeted therapy — molecularly directed:
  • TKIs (oral, manageable)
  • Monoclonal antibodies
  • ADCs (antibody-drug conjugates)
  • PARP inhibitors (BRCA, HRD)

3. Hormone therapy — breast (HR+), prostate (AR+)

4. Immunotherapy — ICIs (PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA-4), CAR-T, TILs, bispecifics

5. Radiopharmaceuticals — Lu-177-PSMA prostate (PSMAfore, VISION), Lu-177-DOTATATE NET (NETTER-1)

6. Combinations + sequencing based on disease + biology Precision oncology principles:
  • Tumor molecular profiling (NGS, IHC, FISH)
  • Biomarker-driven (MSI-H, TMB-high, HER2+, EGFR mutation, ALK fusion, BRAF V600E, KRAS G12C, etc.)
  • Tumor-agnostic approvals based on biomarker (pembrolizumab MSI-H, larotrectinib NTRK fusion, dabrafenib + trametinib BRAF V600E, dostarlimab dMMR rectal)
  • Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) for monitoring + resistance Common toxicities + management:
  • Nausea/vomiting: 5-HT3 (ondansetron) + NK1 (aprepitant) + dexamethasone + olanzapine emerging
  • Myelosuppression: GCSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim) for neutropenia prophylaxis in high-risk regimens
  • Mucositis: oral care, palifermin selected
  • Peripheral neuropathy (taxanes, vinca, oxaliplatin): often dose-limiting; duloxetine for symptoms; preventive trials largely negative
  • Cardiotoxicity (anthracyclines, HER2 agents, fluoropyrimidines): baseline + serial echo; dexrazoxane selected
  • Pneumonitis (bleomycin, mTOR inhibitor, ICIs): steroid, drug discontinuation
  • Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine, sorafenib, sunitinib): topical care, dose adjust Immune-related adverse events (IRAEs) from ICIs:
  • Colitis: infliximab if steroid-refractory
  • Hepatitis: steroid; mycophenolate if refractory
  • Pneumonitis: steroid
  • Endocrine (thyroid most common, pituitary, adrenal, T1DM): replacement therapy
  • Dermatitis: topical + systemic steroid
  • Myocarditis: rare but severe; high-dose steroid + ATG/IVIG/mycophenolate; cardiology
  • Nephritis, hematologic, neurologic Multidisciplinary involvement essential for IRAE management. CAR-T cell therapy — autologous T-cells engineered to recognize tumor antigen (e.g., CD19 B-cell ALL, lymphoma; BCMA multiple myeloma). Toxicities: CRS (tocilizumab), ICANS (neurologic). Logistical complexity, cost, specialized centers.

scenario:
Basic science: thrombosis + hemostasis

คำถาม: coagulation system + bleeding disorders in surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coagulation system + bleeding disorders — surgical relevance

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Coagulation system + bleeding disorders — surgical relevance:

(1) Hemostasis components:
  • Primary: platelets + vWF + endothelium → platelet plug (BT measures; PFA-100)
  • Secondary: coagulation cascade → fibrin clot (PT — extrinsic + common; aPTT — intrinsic + common; TT — thrombin to fibrin)
  • Fibrinolysis: plasmin breaks fibrin (D-dimer);

(2) Coagulation cascade:
  • Extrinsic (tissue factor → VIIa → X)
  • Intrinsic (XII → XI → IX → VIII → X)
  • Common (X → V → II/prothrombin → fibrin);

(3) Vitamin K-dependent factors: II, VII, IX, X + protein C + S (warfarin affects these);

(4) Coagulopathy assessment:
  • History (bleeding, family, medications, liver/renal disease)
  • Labs: PT/INR, aPTT, platelet, fibrinogen, D-dimer
  • Specialized: factor levels, vWF, mixing studies, viscoelastic (TEG, ROTEM)
  • TEG/ROTEM increasingly used (trauma, cardiac, hepatic) — point-of-care guides targeted product use;

(5) Inherited bleeding disorders:
  • Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) — X-linked; severity by factor level; treat: factor VIII replacement, emicizumab (HemA), gene therapy emerging
  • Hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency) — X-linked; factor IX replacement
  • von Willebrand disease (vWF deficiency) — most common inherited bleeding disorder; subtypes 1-3 (1 quantitative mild, 2 qualitative, 3 severe); treat: desmopressin (type 1 + 2A), vWF concentrate (Humate-P), gene therapy;
  • Other rare: factor XI, factor VII, etc.;

(6) Acquired coagulopathies:
  • Liver disease — synthesis of factors; thrombocytopenia + functional issues
  • Vitamin K deficiency — malnutrition, malabsorption, antibiotic, neonatal
  • DIC — sepsis, trauma, malignancy, OB complications; consumptive; treat underlying + supportive
  • Massive transfusion-related — dilution + consumption
  • Anticoagulant medications
  • uremia — platelet dysfunction;

(7) Thrombocytopenia:
  • ITP — autoimmune; treat: steroid, IVIG, TPO agonists (romiplostim, eltrombopag), rituximab, splenectomy selected
  • TTP — pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, AKI, fever, neurological); ADAMTS13 deficiency; treat: plasmapheresis emergent, caplacizumab, immunosuppression;
  • HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia) — type II IgG-mediated; thrombosis paradox; stop heparin, alternative anticoag (argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux); HIT antibody testing
  • DIC, sepsis, drug-induced, malignancy, infection

(8) Hypercoagulable states:
  • Inherited: factor V Leiden (most common), prothrombin G20210A, AT III deficiency, protein C/S deficiency, hyperhomocysteinemia
  • Acquired: APLS (antiphospholipid syndrome — lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein), cancer-related, pregnancy, OCP/HRT, nephrotic, MPN (JAK2), PNH;

(9) Reversal agents:
  • Warfarin: vitamin K + 4F-PCC (preferred over FFP)
  • DOAC: idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet alfa (Xa inhibitors), 4F-PCC alternative
  • UFH: protamine
  • LMWH: protamine (partial)
  • Antiplatelet: platelet transfusion (limited efficacy), DDAVP
  • DIC: treat underlying + product replacement;

(10) TXA (tranexamic acid) — antifibrinolytic; reduces bleeding in: trauma (CRASH-2, CRASH-3), PPH (WOMAN trial), surgical (cardiac, ortho, GU); within 3h of bleeding ideal
————————————————
Coagulation + hemostasis — fundamental for surgery. Components:

1. Primary hemostasis — platelet plug formation: vWF binds collagen + GPIb on platelets; GPIIb/IIIa cross-linking via fibrinogen

2. Secondary hemostasis — coagulation cascade → fibrin: classic cascade intrinsic (XII, XI, IX, VIII) + extrinsic (TF + VII) → common (X, V, II) → fibrin. Modern cell-based model emphasizes initiation, amplification, propagation

3. Fibrinolysis — plasmin (from plasminogen via tPA) breaks fibrin → D-dimer Lab tests:
  • PT/INR — extrinsic + common (VII most sensitive — short half-life; warfarin)
  • aPTT — intrinsic + common (heparin)
  • TT — thrombin → fibrin
  • Fibrinogen
  • Platelet count + function (PFA-100, light transmission aggregometry)
  • Bleeding time (rarely used)
  • Specific factor levels
  • Viscoelastic (TEG, ROTEM) — global hemostasis at bedside; trauma + cardiac + hepatic surgery uses Major inherited bleeding disorders:
  • Hemophilia A (factor VIII): X-linked; severity by factor level (severe < 1%, moderate 1-5%, mild > 5%); treat factor replacement, emicizumab (bispecific antibody mimicking VIII), gene therapy emerging (valoctocogene roxaparvovec)
  • Hemophilia B (factor IX): X-linked; factor IX replacement, gene therapy emerging
  • vWD: most common inherited bleeding disorder; subtypes 1 (mild quantitative — desmopressin works), 2 (qualitative — A/B/M/N — vWF concentrate), 3 (severe deficiency — vWF concentrate) Acquired coagulopathies in surgical patients:

1. Liver disease — synthetic dysfunction + thrombocytopenia + functional

2. Vitamin K deficiency — malnutrition, antibiotic-related, neonatal

3. DIC — sepsis, trauma, OB complications, malignancy

4. Massive transfusion — dilutional + consumptive; manage with balanced products + TXA + calcium

5. Uremia — platelet dysfunction; DDAVP can improve Thrombocytopenia in surgical patients:
  • ITP, TTP, HIT, drug-induced, dilutional, sepsis (DIC), liver disease, marrow suppression
  • HIT (heparin-induced) — type II IgG-mediated; PARADOXICAL thrombosis risk; stop heparin + alternative anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux); 4T score guides suspicion TXA — game-changer in surgical bleeding:
  • Trauma — CRASH-2 (within 3h reduces mortality), CRASH-3 (TBI within 3h reduces head injury death)
  • PPH — WOMAN trial (reduces bleeding death)
  • Cardiac surgery — standard
  • Orthopedic (joint replacement) — reduces transfusion
  • Some urological Hypercoagulable workup — selected post-VTE: provoked vs unprovoked, age, family history, recurrent — testing factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C/S, AT III, APLS, homocysteine.
reference_source: ASH Hemophilia Guidelines; ISTH; ACS Surgery Principles + Practice

  • Primary: platelets + vWF + endothelium → platelet plug (BT measures

scenario:
Basic science: pediatric trauma

คำถาม: pediatric trauma — key differences from adult
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Pediatric trauma — distinct considerations:

(1) Anatomic + physiologic differences:
  • Larger head:body ratio → head injury common
  • Open fontanelles + sutures (infant) → expandable cranium initially → late deterioration possible
  • Higher % cartilage → less rib fractures but more underlying lung injury
  • More compliant chest wall → pulmonary contusion common without rib fracture
  • Smaller body mass + thinner abdominal wall → more multi-organ injury from single insult
  • Solid organs proportionally larger
  • Higher surface area:mass → faster heat loss → hypothermia risk
  • Higher BP, lower HR baselines; physiological vitals age-specific
  • Compensatory mechanisms maintain BP until ~ 30% blood loss → late decompensation
  • Spine immature — SCIWORA (Spinal Cord Injury Without Radiographic Abnormality)
  • Airway anatomy: larger tongue, more anterior + cephalad larynx (C3-4), funnel shape narrowest at cricoid until ~ 8 yr;

(2) Common mechanisms: MVA (passenger + pedestrian), falls, drowning, burns, abuse, sports, recreational;

(3) Triage + transport: pediatric trauma centers improve outcomes for severely injured; field triage criteria with pediatric considerations;

(4) Resuscitation:
  • Weight-based dosing (Broselow tape, length-based)
  • Vital sign norms age-specific
  • IV/IO access — IO common + reliable in pediatric (proximal tibia, distal femur, proximal humerus)
  • Fluid resuscitation: 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus × 2-3, then blood products (10-20 mL/kg PRC, FFP, platelets)
  • Permissive hypotension less established in pediatric than adult
  • TXA: pediatric dose 15 mg/kg (max 1 g)
  • Damage control resuscitation principles applied;

(5) Imaging: minimize radiation (ALARA — as low as reasonably achievable); modified low-dose CT protocols + selective imaging; ultrasound (FAST) + MRI for spine + brain when feasible;

(6) Specific injuries — pediatric considerations:
  • Head injury: CT for moderate-severe; PECARN rules for selective CT in minor head injury
  • C-spine: NEXUS pediatric, Canadian C-spine rule modifications; SCIWORA in young children
  • Thoracic: pulmonary contusion + flail rare due to compliant ribs; hemothorax + pneumothorax + cardiac/great vessel injury
  • Abdominal: solid organ injury (liver, spleen, kidney) — high NOM success in pediatric vs adult; conservative management threshold lower
  • Pelvic: less common (immature pelvis); when present often severe
  • Long bone: ossifying skeleton; growth plate injuries (Salter-Harris); minimize CT
  • Burns: larger surface area:mass → higher fluid requirement + heat loss; pediatric-specific burn formulas; Modified Brooke or Parkland with Lund-Browder chart;

(7) Non-accidental trauma (child abuse) consideration — always assess:
  • Inconsistent history
  • Multiple stages of injuries
  • Specific patterns (bilateral, posterior rib fractures, metaphyseal corner fractures, retinal hemorrhages, abusive head trauma)
  • Mandatory reporting + child protective services involvement
  • Skeletal survey + careful documentation
  • Specialty consult (child abuse pediatricians);

(8) Pain control: weight-based; multimodal; family-centered;

(9) Psychological + family-centered care: PTSD + emotional impact; family presence during procedures + resuscitation (supported by evidence); Child Life specialists;

(10) Outcomes: pediatric better than adult for similar severity due to physiologic reserve + healing; high NOM success solid organ; long-term follow-up for development, growth, scar contracture (burns)
————————————————
Pediatric trauma — distinct from adult; specialized knowledge + dedicated centers improve outcomes. Key anatomic + physiologic differences:

1. Larger head:body ratio — TBI most common cause of death in pediatric trauma

2. Compliant chest wall — pulmonary + cardiac injury common without rib fracture; rib fracture is a marker of high-energy mechanism (worry about underlying injury)

3. Compensated shock — children maintain BP until ~ 30-40% blood loss; tachycardia + delayed capillary refill + mental status changes are EARLIER signs; HYPOTENSION IS LATE FINDING — don't be reassured by 'normal' BP

4. Higher surface area:mass — rapid heat loss; aggressive warming + fluid warmers + ambient room temp; hypothermia worsens coagulopathy

5. Airway anatomy — larger tongue, anterior larynx, funnel shape (narrowest at cricoid until 8 yr); pediatric airway management differences

6. SCIWORA — Spinal Cord Injury Without Radiographic Abnormality — younger children; MRI for clinical suspicion despite normal X-ray/CT

7. Open growth plates — Salter-Harris classification I-V for physeal injuries; ortho involvement

8. Vital signs age-specific — pediatric norms (HR + RR higher, BP lower than adult; weight-based pressure norms) Pediatric trauma triage — to pediatric trauma centers when severely injured; verified pediatric trauma centers improve outcomes for major injuries (especially head, abdomen). Resuscitation differences:
  • Weight-based dosing (Broselow color-coded length tape)
  • IV access often difficult — IO (intraosseous) reliable + fast
  • Fluid: 20 mL/kg crystalloid boluses up to 60 mL/kg; then blood (10-20 mL/kg PRC)
  • TXA: 15 mg/kg (max 1 g)
  • DCR principles apply PECARN rules — pediatric head injury — selective CT vs observation in minor head trauma; reduces radiation exposure. Imaging principles: ALARA — minimize radiation, especially CT; US (FAST) + MRI when feasible; weighing diagnostic yield vs radiation. Pediatric NOM (non-operative management) — solid organ injury (spleen, liver, kidney) success > 90% in pediatric across grades (vs lower in adult); preserve organ when possible. Non-accidental trauma (NAT) / Child abuse — must consider in any pediatric trauma; specific patterns + history-injury mismatch raise suspicion; multidisciplinary including child abuse pediatricians + social work + mandatory reporting + protective services + skeletal survey + ophthalmology (abusive head trauma — retinal hemorrhages). Burns in children:
  • Lund-Browder more accurate than Rule of Nines (head proportionally larger in young)
  • Higher fluid requirement per body weight
  • Heat loss greater
  • Specific formulas (Parkland modified for pediatric: 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA + maintenance with D5LR)
  • Family-centered burn care; abuse consideration. Family-centered care + Child Life specialists — psychological + emotional support; family presence during resuscitation supported by pediatric guidelines + ACS recommendations.
reference_source: APSA Pediatric Trauma Guidelines; ATLS Pediatric Chapter; PECARN; APSA Bell Stage NEC

scenario:
ระบบ EMS: telemedicine + remote consultation

คำถาม: telemedicine in surgical care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Telemedicine in surgery — emerging integrated care

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Telemedicine in surgery — emerging integrated care:

(1) Synchronous (real-time video) vs asynchronous (store-and-forward) consultation;

(2) Common applications:
  • Pre-operative consultation — initial visit, history, education; physical exam + imaging review limited by modality
  • Post-operative follow-up — uncomplicated wound check, suture removal scheduling, recovery progress, medication adjustment; reduces travel burden + costs
  • Surgical second opinions — specialty consultation, complex case multidisciplinary review
  • Inpatient consultation — tele-trauma, tele-ICU, e-consults
  • Remote monitoring — wearables, home pulse-ox, smart bandages, post-op symptoms checklists
  • Tele-mentoring + tele-proctoring — experienced surgeon guides remote surgeon; useful global health + rural
  • Telesurgery (true remote operation) — proof-of-concept (Lindbergh 2001 NY to Strasbourg); 5G enabling; growing in selected countries (China, others); not mainstream yet;

(3) Benefits:
  • Access (rural, mobility-limited, time-constrained patients)
  • Cost-effectiveness
  • Time-efficient for selected encounters
  • Multi-site coordination
  • Mental health + psychological support integration
  • Reduced infection exposure (post-COVID acceleration);

(4) Limitations:
  • Physical exam limited
  • Procedure planning may need in-person
  • Imaging review limited by display + connectivity
  • Digital divide / equity concerns
  • Privacy + security
  • Reimbursement evolving
  • Licensure across jurisdictions;

(5) Quality + safety considerations:
  • Patient selection (appropriateness for telemedicine)
  • Documentation standards
  • Workflow integration
  • Backup for technology failure
  • Standards (American Telemedicine Association, ACS Telehealth Steering Committee);

(6) Tele-trauma + tele-ICU:
  • Remote trauma consultation for rural hospitals — improves outcomes (Latifi et al studies)
  • Tele-ICU model (eICU) — real-time monitoring + intervention; mortality benefit in some implementations;

(7) Specific surgical specialty uses:
  • Dermatology (skin lesion images, asynchronous)
  • Wound care
  • Bariatric pre/post-op surveillance
  • Oncology survivorship
  • Pediatric surgical screening
  • ENT (endoscopic video review);

(8) Implementation considerations:
  • Equity — broadband access, device access, digital literacy, language
  • Quality assurance + outcome tracking
  • Workflow + EHR integration
  • Reimbursement + regulation (CMS, state)
  • Cross-jurisdiction licensure (state lines US — increasingly harmonized)
  • Cybersecurity + HIPAA
  • Cultural + linguistic sensitivity;

(9) COVID-19 catalyzed rapid telemedicine expansion; many policies relaxed temporarily; some sustained; sustainability + appropriateness ongoing;

(10) Future: continued integration; AI integration (triage, image analysis); hybrid in-person + virtual care; cross-border health systems; global tele-surgical mentorship
————————————————
Telemedicine in surgical care — increasingly integrated; accelerated by COVID-19 pandemic. Applications across surgical care continuum:

1. Pre-operative: initial consultation, history, education, multidisciplinary case review

2. Operative: tele-proctoring + tele-mentoring (experienced surgeon supervises remote surgeon — used in fellowship training + rural / LMIC support)

3. Post-operative: routine follow-up for uncomplicated procedures; remote monitoring

4. Specialty consultations: tele-trauma, tele-ICU, e-consults (asynchronous) Telesurgery (true remote operation via robotic systems):
  • Lindbergh Operation 2001 (Marescaux, NY to Strasbourg) — proof-of-concept cholecystectomy
  • Modern: 5G enabling lower latency; growing in selected (China + others)
  • Not mainstream in most healthcare systems yet
  • Issues: latency, safety, redundancy, regulatory Tele-ICU (eICU): centralized off-site critical care team monitors + intervenes in remote ICUs; some studies show mortality benefit + improved metrics (Lilly + others); cost-effectiveness debated; staffing model varies. Tele-trauma: rural ED consultation with trauma surgeon at level I center; improves decision-making + reduces unnecessary transfers; Latifi et al + others evidence. Benefits:
  • Access — rural, mobility-limited, isolated
  • Cost + time efficiency
  • Specialist availability
  • Pandemic response (reduced exposure) Limitations + challenges:
  • Physical exam limitations
  • Quality of video / connection
  • Digital divide (broadband, device, literacy)
  • Privacy + cybersecurity
  • Reimbursement (variable + evolving)
  • Cross-state licensure (US — Interstate Medical Licensure Compact helps; full reciprocity not yet)
  • International licensure
  • Quality assurance + outcome tracking Equity considerations: digital divide may worsen disparities for rural, low-income, elderly, non-English speaking populations; mitigation: community broadband, device lending, translators, hybrid care, kiosks, mobile clinics. AI integration: triage chatbots, image analysis (dermatology, radiology), risk prediction, documentation NLP. Regulatory: FDA for medical devices (including remote surgery), state medical boards, CMS reimbursement, HIPAA + privacy, malpractice insurance considerations. Standards: American Telemedicine Association (ATA), ACS Telehealth Steering Committee, specialty society guidelines.
reference_source: American Telemedicine Association; ACS Telehealth; Latifi R et al. World J Surg

scenario:
ระบบ EMS: surgical leadership + team dynamics

คำถาม: surgical leadership + team performance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical leadership + team-based care — modern paradigm

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Surgical leadership + team-based care — modern paradigm:

(1) Team-based care benefits:
  • Better outcomes (multiple studies — colorectal ERAS, cardiac surgery teams, trauma teams)
  • Improved safety
  • Higher patient satisfaction
  • Reduced provider burnout
  • Enhanced learning environment;

(2) OR team composition: surgeon (attending + assistants + trainees), anesthesia (attending + residents/CRNAs), nursing (circulating + scrub), technicians, perfusionist (cardiac), industry reps (selected), residents/students/observers — clear roles + communication;

(3) Crew Resource Management (CRM) — borrowed from aviation:
  • Mutual support + team awareness
  • Shared mental model
  • Closed-loop communication ('Hand me the suture' → 'Suture handing now' → 'Got it')
  • Speaking up culture (encourage all team members to raise concerns)
  • Briefing + debriefing;

(4) WHO Surgical Safety Checklist — see prior question;

(5) Briefings + debriefings:
  • Pre-procedure briefing: discuss plan, anticipated challenges, equipment, roles
  • Post-procedure debriefing: what went well, what could improve, learnings;

(6) Communication tools:
  • SBAR — situation/background/assessment/recommendation
  • I-PASS (handoff)
  • CUS — concerned/uncomfortable/stop (escalation language)
  • Closed-loop communication for orders + actions;

(7) Leadership principles in surgery:
  • Servant leadership vs traditional hierarchical
  • Psychological safety — Edmondson's work; teams that learn + innovate require team members able to take interpersonal risks
  • Inclusive leadership — value all team members' input
  • Just culture — distinguish system from individual error; encourage reporting + learning
  • Emotional intelligence — self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills
  • Adaptive leadership — respond to changing conditions;

(8) Training in non-technical skills (NOTSS):
  • Decision-making, situation awareness, communication, teamwork, leadership
  • Increasingly assessed in residency + maintenance of certification
  • Simulation training + debriefing;

(9) Diversity, equity, inclusion:
  • Diverse teams perform better (multiple evidence)
  • Representation across roles + leadership
  • Addressing bias (implicit, structural)
  • Cultural competency
  • Inclusive language + behaviors;

(10) Conflict management: respectful disagreement, principled negotiation, focus on patient care, after-action reviews;

(11) Mentorship + sponsorship: structured mentorship programs; sponsorship advocates for opportunities; impact on career trajectory + retention;

(12) Quality improvement leadership: data-driven, PDSA, lean/six-sigma methodologies; team-based QI;

(13) Resilience + wellbeing: leaders model wellbeing + advocate for team wellness
————————————————
Surgical leadership + team-based care — modern foundation of high-performing surgical care. Evidence for team-based care:
  • Better outcomes (ERAS, trauma teams, ICU rounds, OR teams)
  • Improved safety + reduced errors
  • Higher patient satisfaction
  • Reduced burnout
  • Enhanced learning Crew Resource Management (CRM) — borrowed from aviation: shifted accident reduction by addressing human factors. Applied to medicine via Operating Room Briefings, Time Out, Debriefings, Closed-Loop Communication. Key communication tools:
  • SBAR: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation
  • I-PASS: Illness severity, Patient summary, Action list, Situation awareness, Synthesis by receiver
  • CUS: Concerned, Uncomfortable, Safety issue/Stop the line
  • Closed-loop: sender → receiver acknowledges → sender confirms
  • Read-back for verbal orders, especially high-risk medications Non-technical skills (NOTSS) — surgeon-specific framework (Yule + Flin):

1. Situation awareness

2. Decision-making

3. Communication + teamwork

4. Leadership Increasingly assessed in training + practice; simulation + structured feedback. Psychological safety (Amy Edmondson, Harvard): team belief that it is safe for interpersonal risk-taking (asking questions, admitting mistakes, raising concerns). Predicts team learning + performance. Highest-performing teams have high psychological safety. Just culture (Marx, Reason):
  • Human error → console
  • At-risk behavior → coach
  • Reckless behavior → sanction
  • System problems → fix the system Encourages reporting + learning vs blame + cover-up. Leadership styles evolving from authoritative/hierarchical to:
  • Servant leadership
  • Transformational
  • Adaptive
  • Inclusive Emotional intelligence (EQ): self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills (Goleman). Correlates with leadership effectiveness + team performance. Diversity, Equity + Inclusion (DEI):
  • Diverse teams demonstrate better problem-solving + outcomes (McKinsey + others)
  • Underrepresentation in surgery (women, minorities, LGBTQ+) — ongoing efforts
  • Bias awareness + mitigation
  • Inclusive language + behavior
  • Mentorship + sponsorship for underrepresented Burnout + wellbeing intersect with leadership: leaders shape culture; model wellbeing; advocate for systems improvements; address workload + EHR + administrative burden. Quality improvement leadership: data-driven, PDSA cycles, lean/six sigma, team-based, learning health systems. Conflict management: principled negotiation (Fisher + Ury — separate people from problem, focus on interests not positions, generate options for mutual gain, use objective criteria), respectful disagreement focused on patient care, after-action reviews. Mentorship: structured programs reduce burnout, improve career trajectory, retain talent; sponsorship distinct from mentorship — sponsors advocate for opportunities.
reference_source: ACS NSQIP + Surgeon Leadership; Edmondson A. The Fearless Organization; Yule S, Flin R. NOTSS

  • Improved safety
  • Higher patient satisfaction
  • Reduced provider burnout
  • Enhanced learning environment

scenario:
Integrative: chronic post-surgical pain syndrome

คำถาม: chronic pain after surgery — integrative

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic post-surgical pain (CPSP) — integrative multimodal management

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Chronic post-surgical pain (CPSP) — integrative multimodal management:

(1) Definition: persistent pain > 3-6 months post-surgery, beyond expected healing; affects 10-50% of surgical patients depending on procedure;

(2) High-risk procedures: amputation (phantom + stump pain), thoracotomy, mastectomy, inguinal hernia, cardiac surgery, joint replacement;

(3) Risk factors: pre-op chronic pain, female, younger age, psychological factors (depression, anxiety, catastrophizing), genetic, severity of acute post-op pain, surgical factors (nerve injury, large incision, hardware);

(4) Mechanisms:
  • Neuropathic — nerve injury, neuroma, entrapment, allodynia, hyperalgesia, central sensitization
  • Nociceptive — ongoing inflammation, mechanical, scar
  • Mixed;

(5) Prevention strategies (essential — easier to prevent than treat):
  • Multimodal analgesia (see prior question)
  • Regional anesthesia (epidural, peripheral nerve blocks, local infiltration)
  • Minimally invasive surgical techniques
  • Atraumatic tissue handling, nerve preservation
  • Gabapentinoid perioperative (controversial in recent meta-analyses)
  • Identify + treat acute post-op pain aggressively
  • Psychological preparation, expectation setting, education
  • Optimize pre-op pain conditions
  • Limit opioid duration;

(6) Assessment of chronic pain:
  • Multidimensional (intensity, function, mood, sleep)
  • Validated tools (BPI, NRS, NPRS, pain catastrophizing, DN4 for neuropathic features)
  • Diagnostic workup (rule out anatomic — recurrence, mass, mesh issue, neuroma, etc.)
  • Imaging (MRI for nerve, soft tissue)
  • Selective nerve blocks (diagnostic + therapeutic);

(7) Treatment — multimodal: a. Pharmacologic:
  • Non-opioid first-line: NSAID, acetaminophen, topical (lidocaine, capsaicin, diclofenac, menthol)
  • Neuropathic-targeted: gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine (SNRI), TCA (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
  • Opioids — last-line for chronic non-cancer pain; if used, careful selection + monitoring + lowest effective dose + multimodal + functional goals + screening;
  • Cannabinoids — emerging, mixed evidence; some chronic pain conditions, legal context dependent b. Interventional:
  • Nerve blocks (diagnostic + therapeutic)
  • Pulsed radiofrequency ablation
  • Radiofrequency ablation
  • Spinal cord stimulator (SCS) — refractory neuropathic
  • Peripheral nerve stimulator
  • Intrathecal pump — selected severe c. Surgical:
  • Neurectomy / triple neurectomy (post-hernia)
  • Neuroma excision
  • Mesh removal (selected post-hernia)
  • Hardware removal d. Physical:
  • Physical therapy + tailored exercise
  • TENS, ice, heat
  • Manual therapy, massage e. Psychological:
  • CBT for chronic pain
  • Mindfulness-based stress reduction
  • Pain neuroscience education
  • Acceptance + commitment therapy (ACT) f. Lifestyle:
  • Sleep hygiene
  • Exercise (graded activity)
  • Weight management
  • Stress management;

(8) Multidisciplinary pain clinic — for complex / refractory;

(9) Opioid stewardship:
  • Avoid opioid escalation
  • Risk screening (ORT, SOAPP-R)
  • PDMP query
  • Drug monitoring
  • Naloxone co-prescription if at-risk
  • Buprenorphine transition for selected;

(10) Patient-centered: function + quality of life > pain score alone; address whole person
————————————————
Chronic Post-Surgical Pain (CPSP) — emerging recognition; common (10-50% varies by procedure); under-recognized + under-treated. Definition (IASP): pain persisting > 3 months post-surgery, beyond expected healing, not pre-existing, not explained by other cause. High-risk procedures:

1. Amputation (phantom limb + stump pain) — 50-80%

2. Thoracotomy — 30-50%

3. Mastectomy — 20-50%

4. Inguinal hernia repair — 10-15% (severe 1-3%)

5. Cardiac surgery (sternotomy) — 20-30%

6. Joint replacement — 10-20%

7. C-section — 10-15%

8. Cholecystectomy — 5-10% Risk factors:
  • Pre-op: pre-existing chronic pain, female, younger age, depression/anxiety/catastrophizing, genetic, opioid use
  • Surgical: nerve injury, type of incision, surgical experience, surgery duration
  • Post-op: severity of acute pain, opioid escalation, complications Mechanisms:
  • Peripheral sensitization (inflammation, neuroplasticity at injury site)
  • Central sensitization (CNS amplification of pain signals; descending inhibition impaired)
  • Neuropathic (nerve injury — transection, traction, compression, neuroma) Prevention (more effective than treatment):

1. Multimodal analgesia (perioperative)

2. Regional anesthesia (continuous nerve blocks, epidural) reduces CPSP in some studies

3. Minimally invasive surgery

4. Nerve preservation + identification (e.g., hernia surgery — identify all 3 nerves)

5. Gabapentinoid perioperative — controversial; recent meta-analyses + RCTs (Verret M et al — meta-analysis suggests less benefit than previously thought)

6. Aggressive acute pain control

7. Identify + address psychological factors pre-op

8. Limit opioid duration post-op

9. Patient education + expectation setting Assessment: multidimensional (intensity, function, mood, sleep, social impact). Validated tools: BPI (Brief Pain Inventory), DN4 (neuropathic features), pain catastrophizing scale, PROMIS measures. Treatment: multimodal, multidisciplinary:

1. Non-opioid first-line (NSAID, acetaminophen, topicals)

2. Neuropathic-targeted (gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, TCA) — better evidence for some conditions

3. Interventional (blocks, ablation, SCS, intrathecal)

4. Surgical (neurectomy, neuroma excision, mesh removal, hardware removal) — selected

5. Physical therapy + exercise

6. Psychological (CBT, mindfulness, ACT, pain neuroscience education)

7. Lifestyle

8. Opioids: last-line for chronic non-cancer pain; if used, careful — lowest dose, multimodal, functional goals, monitoring, PDMP, screening, naloxone co-prescription. Opioid epidemic context: judicious use post-op, limit duration + supply, educate patients, safe disposal, naloxone for at-risk, address opioid use disorder when present (MAT — methadone, buprenorphine, naltrexone). Multidisciplinary pain clinic for complex/refractory.
reference_source: IASP CPSP Definition; Schug SA et al. Br J Anaesth; Kehlet H et al. Lancet

scenario:
Integrative: post-bariatric body contouring surgery

คำถาม: integrative care of weight loss patient

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-massive weight loss body contouring — integrative

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Post-massive weight loss body contouring — integrative:

(1) Patient evaluation pre-contouring:
  • Weight stability — typically 12-18 months post-bariatric + ≥ 6 months stable weight
  • Nutritional status — albumin, prealbumin, vitamins, minerals optimized
  • Smoking cessation absolute (4 weeks pre + post)
  • Glycemic control (DM)
  • Medical optimization (HT, OSA, comorbidities)
  • Psychological readiness — realistic expectations, body image, depression, eating disorders
  • Functional assessment + activity status;

(2) Common body contouring procedures:
  • Abdominoplasty — most common; remove excess abdominal skin + fat + tighten rectus diastasis; outcomes good when patient stable
  • Brachioplasty — upper arm contouring; significant scar trade-off
  • Thigh lift (medial / lateral) — significant scar; functional + aesthetic benefit
  • Mastopexy + augmentation — breast lift ± augmentation for ptosis
  • Lower body lift (belt lipectomy / circumferential) — comprehensive lower body; major procedure
  • Buttock augmentation — autologous fat transfer (BBL) or implant
  • Facial rejuvenation — face/neck lift, brow lift;

(3) Staging strategy:
  • Often multiple procedures over 1-3 years
  • Combine compatible procedures (efficiency vs complication risk)
  • High-risk patient → staged with longer interval;

(4) Risks specific to massive weight loss:
  • Wound complications (poor healing, seroma, hematoma, dehiscence, necrosis) higher than primary cosmetic
  • DVT/PE high risk — pre-op assessment + chemoprophylaxis + early mobility
  • Long surgery + intraop hypothermia
  • Nutritional impact on healing
  • Scar burden + visibility
  • Patient expectations mismatch
  • Late recurrence with weight regain;

(5) Insurance considerations:
  • Functional indications (panniculectomy for chronic intertrigo, ulceration, hygiene; not cosmetic)
  • Documentation of conservative therapy failure
  • Pre-authorization process
  • Out-of-pocket for purely cosmetic;

(6) Multidisciplinary: bariatric surgery, plastic surgery, nutrition, psychology, primary care;

(7) Patient education + expectations:
  • Scars are permanent + visible
  • Multiple procedures may be needed
  • Recovery time substantial
  • Risk of complications higher than non-bariatric counterparts
  • Body changes ongoing — late skin laxity;

(8) Nutritional + medical optimization throughout:
  • Protein (1.5+ g/kg/day)
  • Multivitamins (bariatric-specific)
  • Lifestyle (exercise, sleep, hydration)
  • Glycemic + BP control;

(9) Psychological + body image:
  • Pre + post-op assessment
  • Realistic expectations crucial
  • Counseling support
  • Continued weight management;

(10) Long-term outcomes: body contouring contributes to weight maintenance long-term + improved quality of life + body image (multiple studies in massive weight loss population)
————————————————
Post-massive weight loss body contouring surgery — increasingly common with bariatric prevalence growth; substantial functional + aesthetic role + quality of life impact. Pre-operative readiness:

1. Weight stability ≥ 6-12 months post nadir

2. Nutritional optimization (especially protein, vitamins, minerals — post-bariatric patients often deficient)

3. Smoking cessation absolute (4+ weeks pre + post) — wound healing

4. Medical comorbidity optimization (DM, HT, OSA)

5. Psychological readiness — realistic expectations, body image work, treated depression/anxiety/eating disorder, support system

6. Reasonable functional + activity status Common procedures:

1. Abdominoplasty (panniculectomy) — most common; addresses excess skin + fat + rectus diastasis; functional (hygiene, mobility) + aesthetic

2. Brachioplasty — arm contouring

3. Thigh lift — medial/lateral

4. Lower body lift / belt lipectomy — circumferential; major

5. Mastopexy ± augmentation — breast

6. Buttock contouring — fat transfer (BBL — Brazilian Butt Lift; risks include fat embolism if injected intramuscularly), implant

7. Facial rejuvenation — face, neck, brow Specific risks in massive weight loss population:
  • Wound complications (poor healing, seroma, hematoma, dehiscence, necrosis) — 30-50% in some series vs lower in non-MWL
  • DVT/PE — major risk: longer surgery, immobility, BMI history; chemoprophylaxis + mechanical + early mobility
  • Nutritional deficiency impacts healing
  • Hypothermia in long surgeries
  • Higher reoperation rate Insurance considerations:
  • Panniculectomy can be functionally indicated (chronic skin intertrigo, ulceration, hygiene impairment) — may be covered with documentation
  • Pure cosmetic — out-of-pocket
  • Pre-authorization process important Multidisciplinary collaboration: bariatric surgery, plastic surgery, nutrition, psychology, primary care + others as needed (DM educator, sleep medicine). Staging strategy — multiple procedures over 1-3 years; balance combining for efficiency vs complication risk (longer surgery + larger trauma = higher complications); high-risk patients staged with longer intervals. Realistic expectations + scar management: scars are permanent + often extensive; realistic discussions + post-op scar care (silicone, RT for keloid-prone, time, sun protection). Long-term outcomes: body contouring contributes to maintained weight loss long-term + improved quality of life, body image, sexual function, exercise participation (multiple studies — Modarressi 2013, others). Psychological benefits substantial in appropriately selected patients.
reference_source: ASPS Body Contouring after MWL; ASMBS Position Statements; Sanger C et al. Plast Reconstr Surg

  • Nutritional status — albumin, prealbumin, vitamins, minerals optimized
  • Smoking cessation absolute (4 weeks pre + post)

scenario:
Integrative: pediatric cancer + survivorship

คำถาม: childhood cancer survivor — long-term care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Childhood cancer survivorship — multidisciplinary long-term care

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Childhood cancer survivorship — multidisciplinary long-term care:

(1) Background: - > 85% of childhood cancers cured with modern therapy - > 500,000 childhood cancer survivors in US alone, growing
  • Late effects significant — 60-80% have at least one chronic health condition by 30 years post-diagnosis (CCSS data);

(2) Late effects of cancer + treatment: a. Cardiac:
  • Anthracycline cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin, daunorubicin) — dose-dependent; cardiomyopathy, HF; echo surveillance based on cumulative dose + RT + age
  • Radiation cardiomyopathy + valve disease + coronary disease (mediastinal RT)
  • Hypertension + dyslipidemia b. Pulmonary:
  • Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity
  • Radiation pneumonitis + fibrosis
  • Restrictive disease post chest RT/surgery c. Endocrine:
  • Hypothyroidism (RT or chemo)
  • Growth hormone deficiency (cranial RT)
  • Adrenal insufficiency
  • Gonadal failure / infertility
  • DM (steroid, RT — abdomen/pancreas) d. Reproductive + fertility:
  • Premature ovarian failure
  • Azoospermia / oligospermia (alkylating agent, RT)
  • Fertility preservation pre-treatment increasingly emphasized (sperm banking, oocyte/embryo cryopreservation, ovarian/testicular tissue)
  • Increased pregnancy + neonatal complications e. Secondary malignancies:
  • Higher risk lifelong
  • RT-related (sarcoma, thyroid, breast)
  • Chemo-related (especially alkylators — AML/MDS)
  • Surveillance critical f. Neurocognitive:
  • Cranial RT, intrathecal chemo
  • Learning disabilities, attention, memory, executive function
  • School + vocational accommodations
  • Increasingly mitigated with reduced-intensity RT g. Psychosocial:
  • PTSD, anxiety, depression
  • Body image (amputation, hair loss, scars)
  • Social/relationship impact h. Other:
  • Cataract (steroid, RT)
  • Hearing loss (cisplatin, RT)
  • Osteoporosis
  • Renal dysfunction
  • GI/hepatic (HCV — older transfusion, methotrexate hepatotoxicity, RT enteritis)
  • Dental (RT)
  • Lymphedema (limb-sparing surgery, lymph node dissection);

(3) Children's Oncology Group (COG) Long-Term Follow-Up Guidelines — comprehensive surveillance recommendations by exposure:
  • Risk-based individualized
  • Annual visits at least
  • Modality-specific (anthracycline → echo; RT chest → mammogram early; cisplatin → audio; etc.);

(4) Transition to adult care:
  • Pediatric → adult survivorship clinic
  • Survivorship care plan (treatment summary + future surveillance + late effects + lifestyle)
  • Patient + family education
  • Maintain medical records lifelong;

(5) Lifestyle counseling:
  • Avoid smoking + heavy alcohol (additive late effect risk)
  • Healthy diet, exercise, weight
  • Sun protection (skin Ca risk)
  • Reproductive counseling + contraception during fertility window;

(6) Vaccinations — update; post-HCT additional series; live vaccines may be safe later (re-immunization protocols);

(7) Mental health + psychosocial support throughout life;

(8) Genetic counseling — increasing recognition of cancer predisposition syndromes in pediatric cancer; testing for selected (Li-Fraumeni, others);

(9) Multidisciplinary survivorship clinic ideal — oncology, endocrinology, cardiology, GI, neuropsych, fertility, dental, social work, child life (transition);

(10) Quality of life > survival alone — modern emphasis: late-effect reduction with reduced therapy intensity (e.g., reduced RT dose/volume + chemo de-escalation in ALL based on risk stratification)
————————————————
Childhood cancer survivorship — > 85% cure rate modern era → growing population of survivors needing long-term care. CCSS (Childhood Cancer Survivor Study) large cohort data: > 60-80% chronic health conditions, > 25% severe/life-threatening by 30 years; relative mortality 8× general population (mostly second cancers + cardiac). Treatment-related late effects organized by exposure:

1. Anthracycline cardiotoxicity — dose-dependent (cumulative > 250-300 mg/m² high risk); baseline + serial echo; risk increased with RT + younger age + female + obesity; dexrazoxane for prevention

2. Radiation effects by site + dose:
  • Chest RT → cardiomyopathy, breast Ca, lung dysfunction, thyroid
  • Cranial RT → cognitive, growth hormone deficiency, second brain tumor, cataract, stroke
  • Abdominal RT → renal, gonadal, GI, hepatic, second cancer, lymphatic
  • Pelvic RT → infertility, second cancer (bladder, colon), lymphedema

3. Alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide) → infertility, secondary AML/MDS

4. Platinum → ototoxicity (cisplatin), nephrotoxicity

5. Methotrexate intrathecal → cognitive impact

6. Steroids → osteoporosis, AVN, cataract, DM

7. Stem cell transplant → multiple systems COG Long-Term Follow-Up Guidelines — exposure-based + risk-based surveillance recommendations:
  • Anthracycline → echo q1-5 yr based on dose + RT + age
  • Chest RT → mammogram + MRI from age 25 or 8 yr post-RT
  • Cisplatin → audiogram
  • Cranial RT → cognitive + endocrine
  • Pelvic/abdominal RT → renal, gonadal, GI Updated periodically; freely available. Fertility preservation — increasingly emphasized + offered pre-treatment:
  • Sperm banking
  • Oocyte / embryo cryopreservation
  • Ovarian / testicular tissue cryopreservation (experimental in pre-pubertal — improving)
  • GnRH agonist during chemo (controversial) Transition to adult care — vulnerable period; structured transition essential. Survivorship Care Plan: treatment summary + future surveillance + late effects + lifestyle + contacts. Multidisciplinary survivorship clinic — pediatric or transition; ongoing patient + family education + advocacy. Second cancer surveillance — lifelong + risk-based; modifiable risk factors (smoking, sun, weight, alcohol). Mental health + psychosocial — chronic; PTSD, anxiety, depression elevated in survivors + caregivers + siblings (caregivers often have more PTSD than survivors themselves). Modern de-escalation — based on improved risk stratification, biomarkers + targeted therapy → reducing late effect burden while maintaining cure; e.g., modern ALL therapy reduces cranial RT in many; reduce anthracycline; sparing radiation in some Hodgkin protocols.
reference_source: COG Long-Term Follow-Up Guidelines; Childhood Cancer Survivor Study (CCSS); NCCN Survivorship
