scenario:
Clinical: pediatric tumors

คำถาม: pediatric solid tumors — surgical management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pediatric solid tumors — distinct from adult

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Pediatric solid tumors — distinct from adult:

(1) Common pediatric tumors: a. Neuroblastoma (most common extracranial solid tumor in children):
  • Arises from neural crest (sympathetic ganglia, adrenal medulla)
  • Median age 18 months
  • Catecholamines (urine VMA, HVA) elevated
  • INRGSS staging (L1, L2, M, MS)
  • Risk stratification by age + MYCN amplification + ploidy + histology
  • Treatment by risk: low — observation or surgery alone; intermediate — chemo + surgery; high — multimodal (chemo + surgery + RT + immunotherapy + transplant)
  • Anti-GD2 immunotherapy (dinutuximab) for high-risk maintenance b. Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) — most common renal in children:
  • Median age 3 yr
  • Triphasic histology
  • Associations: WAGR syndrome, Beckwith-Wiedemann, Denys-Drash, hemihypertrophy
  • Bilateral 5-10%
  • SIOP vs COG protocols (neoadjuvant chemo vs upfront nephrectomy)
  • Survival > 90% favorable histology
  • Nephron-sparing in bilateral / high-risk c. Rhabdomyosarcoma — most common pediatric soft tissue sarcoma:
  • Embryonal (most common) > alveolar > pleomorphic > spindle
  • Sites: head/neck, GU (bladder, prostate), extremity, trunk
  • Multimodal: chemo + surgery + RT
  • Outcomes by risk d. Hepatoblastoma — most common liver tumor in children:
  • Median age 1-3 yr
  • AFP marker
  • PRETEXT staging
  • Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemo + surgical resection (often hepatectomy or transplant if extensive)
  • Survival ~ 80% e. Ewing sarcoma — bone (+ extraosseous):
  • Translocation EWSR1-FLI1
  • Multimodal: chemo + local control (surgery + RT) f. Osteosarcoma — most common primary bone tumor:
  • Adolescent, distal femur > proximal tibia > proximal humerus
  • Neoadjuvant chemo + limb-sparing surgery + adjuvant chemo
  • Outcomes ~ 70% localized g. Retinoblastoma — most common pediatric intraocular:
  • RB1 gene; familial + sporadic
  • Younger age (median 1-2 yr)
  • Treatment: chemo (intra-arterial increasingly used), focal therapy, enucleation for severe
  • Second malignancy risk lifelong (especially RB1 germline — osteosarcoma, others) h. Germ cell tumors — testicular, ovarian, extragonadal:
  • AFP + β-hCG markers
  • Multimodal i. CNS tumors — most common pediatric cancer overall: medulloblastoma, ependymoma, glioma, ATRT, craniopharyngioma — pediatric neurosurgery + oncology
  • Reduced-dose RT + targeted chemo in modern era;

(2) Pediatric oncology multidisciplinary: pediatric oncology, pediatric surgery, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology, child life, social work, psychology, nutrition, school liaison;

(3) Clinical trial enrollment — high participation rate in pediatric (much higher than adult) — drives outcome improvements;

(4) COG (Children's Oncology Group) — coordinates research + trials globally;

(5) Treatment modifications for child:
  • Surgical technique
  • Chemotherapy dose by body surface area or weight
  • Radiation considerations (developing tissues — late effects)
  • Anesthesia + perioperative considerations
  • Psychological + family support
  • Long-term follow-up + late effects;

(6) Tumor biology often distinct from adult — pediatric cancers have different driver mutations, fewer mutations overall, often single translocations or copy number changes;

(7) Survival has improved dramatically — > 85% 5-yr survival modern era for childhood cancer overall (selected types — ALL ~ 90%, Wilms 90%+, retinoblastoma 95%+; brain tumors more variable; high-risk neuroblastoma + sarcomas + AML more challenging)
————————————————
Pediatric solid tumors — distinct from adult cancers in biology, behavior, treatment, outcomes. Childhood cancer overall now > 85% 5-yr survival; substantial late-effect concern. Major pediatric solid tumors:

1. Neuroblastoma — most common extracranial solid tumor in children; neural crest origin (adrenal, paraspinal sympathetic); median age 18 mo; presentation variable (mass, neuro symptoms, paraneoplastic — opsoclonus-myoclonus). INRGSS staging + risk groups (low/intermediate/high based on age + MYCN + ploidy + histology + others). High-risk treatment: induction chemo → surgery → high-dose chemo + autologous stem cell rescue (sometimes tandem) → RT to primary → maintenance with retinoic acid + anti-GD2 immunotherapy (dinutuximab) — improves survival. Stage MS (infant with skin/liver/marrow involvement) may spontaneously regress in selected.

2. Wilms tumor — most common pediatric renal; median 3 yr; SIOP (neoadjuvant chemo before nephrectomy) vs COG (upfront nephrectomy then chemo) — both with > 90% survival favorable histology. Bilateral 5-10% — nephron-sparing emphasis. Anaplastic histology worse prognosis.

3. Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) — most common pediatric soft tissue sarcoma; embryonal most common (favorable), alveolar worse (PAX3/7-FOXO1 translocation). Sites: head/neck (orbital + parameningeal + non-parameningeal), GU (bladder, prostate, paratesticular, vulva/vagina), extremity, trunk. Multimodal — chemo (VAC backbone) + surgery + RT.

4. Hepatoblastoma — most common pediatric liver tumor; AFP marker; PRETEXT staging guides resectability + neoadjuvant chemo + surgery (hepatectomy or transplant if extensive). ~ 80% cure.

5. Ewing sarcoma — bone + extraosseous; small round blue cell; EWSR1-FLI1 translocation. Multimodal.

6. Osteosarcoma — most common primary bone tumor; adolescent; distal femur most common; neoadjuvant chemo + limb-sparing surgery; ~ 70% cure if localized.

7. Retinoblastoma — most common pediatric intraocular; RB1 (Knudson 2-hit); familial (germline — bilateral, multifocal, second cancer risk lifelong) vs sporadic. Intra-arterial chemo emerging; focal therapies preserve vision.

8. Germ cell tumors — different biology from adult; sacrococcygeal teratoma classic neonatal.

9. CNS — most common pediatric cancer; medulloblastoma + ependymoma + various gliomas + ATRT + craniopharyngioma. Treatment modifications by age + risk. Pediatric oncology principles:
  • Multidisciplinary teams
  • Risk-adapted therapy
  • Clinical trial enrollment (high % in pediatric)
  • Reduce late effects (de-escalate when feasible based on better risk stratification)
  • Family + child-centered care
  • Long-term follow-up + survivorship
  • Specialized pediatric oncology centers (Children's Oncology Group sites). Tumor biology distinct: fewer mutations overall, often translocations or copy number changes driving disease; epigenetic + transcription factor-driven. Increasing precision oncology — pediatric MATCH + APEC etc. trials testing molecularly-targeted approaches.
reference_source: COG Children's Oncology Group; NCCN Pediatric Tumors; INRG Neuroblastoma; SIOP Wilms

scenario:
Clinical: gynecology — ovarian mass

คำถาม: ovarian mass workup + management
explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Ovarian mass — workup + management:

(1) Initial assessment:
  • History (menstrual, hormonal, pain, GI/GU symptoms, family history)
  • Exam (size, mobility, tenderness, ascites)
  • Imaging: transvaginal + transabdominal US first-line (size, complexity, septa, solid components, papillary, vascularity); MRI for indeterminate; CT for staging if malignancy suspected
  • Tumor markers based on age + features:
  • CA-125 — epithelial ovarian Ca surveillance + post-menopausal mass; many false positives (endometriosis, menstruation, pregnancy, peritoneal inflammation)
  • AFP, β-hCG, LDH — germ cell tumor (especially young women)
  • CEA — mucinous (could indicate GI primary metastatic)
  • Inhibin, AMH — sex cord-stromal
  • HE4 — adjunct to CA-125 (ROMA, RMI algorithms)
  • ROMA, RMI, OVA-1 — risk algorithms;

(2) Premenopausal:
  • Most common functional cysts (follicular, corpus luteum) — typically resolve
  • Endometriomas
  • Mature cystic teratoma (dermoid)
  • Mucinous + serous cystadenoma
  • Germ cell tumors (rare but ~ 5% of ovarian cancer in young)
  • Borderline + epithelial cancer less common than postmenopausal;

(3) Postmenopausal:
  • Most concerning age for malignancy
  • Any solid component, complex features → higher malignancy risk
  • Refer to gynecologic oncologist for staging + appropriate surgery;

(4) Acute complications:
  • Ovarian torsion — surgical emergency: severe pain, US Doppler flow assessment (may be normal — clinical suspicion important); detorsion + ovarian preservation when feasible (oophorectomy if non-viable); + oophoropexy if predisposing factors
  • Hemorrhagic cyst rupture — usually self-limited; observe if stable; surgery if hemodynamically unstable;

(5) Surgical approach:
  • Laparoscopic preferred for benign-appearing masses (cystectomy preserving ovary in reproductive-age, oophorectomy in postmenopausal)
  • Open surgery for large, malignant, complex
  • In suspected malignancy → gynecologic oncologist + comprehensive staging laparotomy (peritoneal washings, omentectomy, lymph node dissection, biopsies);

(6) Ovarian cancer:
  • Epithelial (most common — high-grade serous most common subtype, often with TP53; BRCA association)
  • Germ cell (young women — dysgerminoma, yolk sac, etc.)
  • Sex cord-stromal (granulosa cell, Sertoli-Leydig)
  • Borderline (low malignant potential)
  • Metastatic (Krukenberg from gastric, breast, colon) Stage I-IV; debulking surgery (cytoreduction to no visible disease — R0 cytoreduction strongest prognostic factor) + platinum-based chemo (carboplatin + paclitaxel); maintenance bevacizumab; PARP inhibitor maintenance (BRCA+, HRD+, all newly diagnosed advanced — PRIMA, PAOLA-1);

(7) Genetic counseling indicated — BRCA1/2 (10-15% ovarian Ca), Lynch (10%), other; cascade testing;

(8) Prevention:
  • Oral contraceptive use reduces ovarian Ca risk (50% if 5+ yr)
  • Tubal ligation
  • Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA+ + others (age 35-45)
  • Opportunistic salpingectomy at other gyne surgery emerging (recognize STIC — serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma — fallopian tube origin of high-grade serous ovarian Ca per modern understanding);

(9) Patient-centered: fertility preservation if reproductive age + benign or selected malignant (germ cell, borderline)
————————————————
Ovarian mass — common; differential by age + features. US characterization (IOTA criteria) + tumor markers + clinical features guide management. Algorithm:

1. TVUS — primary imaging

2. Markers age-appropriate:
  • CA-125 — post-menopausal especially
  • AFP, β-hCG, LDH — young women (germ cell)
  • HE4, ROMA, RMI — risk algorithms

3. MRI — indeterminate cases

4. CT — staging if malignancy

5. Surgical evaluation — by appropriate surgeon (gynecologist for benign-appearing, gynecologic oncologist for suspected malignancy) Common ovarian masses by age:
  • Reproductive (15-45): functional cysts (follicular, corpus luteum), endometrioma, mature cystic teratoma (dermoid), cystadenoma (serous, mucinous), borderline tumor, germ cell, less commonly epithelial cancer
  • Postmenopausal (> 45-50): any complex mass concerning for cancer; benign also possible (cystadenoma, fibroma) Ovarian cancer types:

1. Epithelial (90%):
  • High-grade serous (most common; often BRCA, TP53; thought to arise from fallopian tube — STIC lesion)
  • Low-grade serous
  • Endometrioid
  • Clear cell
  • Mucinous (often borderline; if invasive consider GI primary)
  • Brenner

2. Germ cell — young women (teens-30s); dysgerminoma, immature teratoma, yolk sac, choriocarcinoma, mixed; treatable + curable even advanced

3. Sex cord-stromal — granulosa cell (estrogen-producing), Sertoli-Leydig (androgen-producing), thecoma, fibroma

4. Metastatic (Krukenberg) — from gastric, breast, colon Surgical management:
  • Benign-appearing premenopausal: laparoscopic cystectomy (preserve ovary if reproductive-age + benign-appearing)
  • Benign-appearing postmenopausal: laparoscopic oophorectomy ± salpingectomy
  • Suspected malignancy: gynecologic oncologist; staging laparotomy (peritoneal washings + omentectomy + lymph node sampling + biopsies); cytoreductive surgery for advanced (R0 strongest prognostic factor)
  • Adnexal torsion: surgical emergency; detorsion + preservation when feasible; oophoropexy if recurrent Ovarian cancer treatment:
  • Surgery (debulking — R0 cytoreduction = no visible disease — best outcomes)
  • Adjuvant chemo (carboplatin + paclitaxel)
  • Maintenance bevacizumab (advanced)
  • PARP inhibitor maintenance (olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib) — BRCA+, HRD+, or all newly diagnosed advanced (PRIMA, PAOLA-1, SOLO-1 trials)
  • Recurrence: platinum-sensitive (> 6 mo) vs resistant Genetics: BRCA1/2 (10-15% ovarian Ca), Lynch (10%), other genes (PALB2, RAD51 family, ATM); cascade testing of family; risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy. Risk reduction: oral contraceptives (50% reduction if 5+ yr), tubal ligation, RR salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA+ at 35-40 (BRCA1) / 40-45 (BRCA2), opportunistic salpingectomy (emerging — STIC theory — fallopian tube origin of high-grade serous).
reference_source: ACOG Practice Bulletin Adnexal Mass; NCCN Ovarian Cancer; ESMO; SGO Statements

  • Imaging: transvaginal + transabdominal US first-line (size, complexity, septa, solid components, papillary, vascularity)

scenario:
Clinical: orthopedic — open fracture

คำถาม: open fracture management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Open fracture — surgical emergency requires systematic approach

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Open fracture — surgical emergency requires systematic approach:

(1) Gustilo-Anderson classification:
  • I: wound < 1 cm, clean, low-energy
  • II: wound > 1 cm without extensive soft tissue damage
  • IIIA: extensive soft tissue but coverage possible
  • IIIB: extensive soft tissue loss + bone exposure requiring coverage (flap)
  • IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair;

(2) Management: a. Emergency Department:
  • ATLS primary + secondary survey
  • IV antibiotic ASAP (within 1 hour, ideally < 3 hr of injury — strongest evidence base):
  • Type I-II: cefazolin (1st-gen cephalosporin)
  • Type III: cefazolin + aminoglycoside (gentamicin); add penicillin for farm/soil contamination (Clostridium); add fluoroquinolone or 3rd-gen cephalosporin alternative
  • Newer studies: cefazolin alone may be sufficient even for III in selected; tailored antibiogram
  • Tetanus immunization status + booster ± Tig (immunoglobulin) if high-risk
  • Photograph wound + cover with saline-soaked sterile dressing
  • Splint + immobilize
  • Pre-op labs + radiographs + appropriate imaging
  • Type & cross b. Operative management — time-sensitive:
  • Irrigation + debridement (I&D) within 24h (older 6h dogma less supported by recent evidence — FLOW trial; antibiotic timing more important than I&D timing per LEAP + others); urgent for severely contaminated, vascular compromise, compartment syndrome
  • I&D: copious irrigation (low-pressure NS preferred — FLOW trial NEJM 2015; antiseptic additive controversial), thorough debridement of devitalized tissue + foreign body
  • Fracture stabilization: external fixation initially for severe contamination → conversion to internal fixation when wound healed; primary internal fixation for less contaminated; nailing for shaft
  • Repeat I&D q24-48h until wound clean
  • Soft tissue coverage — primary closure if clean + tension-free; otherwise wound VAC + delayed primary closure OR flap (local, regional, free) within 3-7 days (Godina principle); c. Wound infection common — Pseudomonas + MRSA risk; cultures + antibiotic stewardship d. Compartment syndrome surveillance — fasciotomy if pressure elevated + symptoms; e. Limb salvage vs amputation — MESS score (Mangled Extremity Severity Score) + clinical judgment; LEAP study showed similar functional outcomes long-term salvage vs amputation in selected severe injuries; multidisciplinary;

(3) Specific concerns:
  • Vascular injury (Type IIIC) — vascular surgery + repair before bony stabilization (shunt + stabilize + then bypass);
  • Open joint — copious irrigation + early antibiotic + closure
  • Mangled extremity — multidisciplinary (vascular, ortho, plastics, trauma); patient-centered decision about salvage vs amputation;

(4) Long-term:
  • Functional rehabilitation
  • Psychological
  • Pain management
  • Nonunion + malunion management
  • Late infection
  • Hardware-related issues;

(5) Prevention of complications: early IV antibiotic (within 3h ideal), prompt I&D (within 24h), appropriate fixation, soft tissue coverage (early flap if needed), surveillance + early intervention
————————————————
Open fracture management — orthopedic surgical emergency; principles aim to prevent infection (osteomyelitis) + preserve function. Gustilo-Anderson classification — guides management + prognosis:
  • I: < 1 cm, clean, low-energy
  • II: > 1 cm, moderate soft tissue damage
  • III: extensive soft tissue or high-energy; subdivided:
  • IIIA: adequate soft tissue coverage available
  • IIIB: bone exposure + flap coverage needed
  • IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair Key principles:

1. IV antibiotic ASAP — strongest evidence (within 1 hour, ideally < 3 hr); cefazolin (I-II); cefazolin + aminoglycoside (III) — though newer studies suggest cefazolin alone may suffice in many; tailor to wound + local antibiogram. Penicillin/metronidazole for farm/soil contamination (Clostridium).

2. Tetanus: status + booster ± Tig

3. I&D timing: classic '6-hour rule' less evidence-based per modern data (FLOW + LEAP + others); within 24h acceptable + may not change outcomes; urgent if severe contamination, vascular compromise, compartment syndrome

4. Irrigation — low-pressure NS preferred (FLOW NEJM 2015 — high-pressure no advantage + may push bacteria deeper; antiseptic additives no benefit)

5. Debridement — all devitalized tissue + foreign body; assess viability; repeat as needed

6. Fracture stabilization: external fixation initially for severe; convert to internal when clean; primary internal in selected (less contaminated, fresh)

7. Soft tissue coverage: primary closure if clean + tension-free; otherwise wound VAC + delayed primary closure OR flap (Godina principle — early flap improves outcomes; within 72h ideal in tibial fractures) — local, regional, free flap by reconstructive plastic surgery

8. Infection surveillance + treatment Compartment syndrome — major concern:
  • 6 P's: pain disproportionate, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis, pulselessness (late), poikilothermia
  • Pain with passive stretch most sensitive
  • Compartment pressures > 30 mmHg or within 30 of diastolic = positive
  • Fasciotomy emergent Mangled extremity + decisions about salvage vs amputation:
  • MESS score + clinical judgment
  • LEAP study — long-term functional outcomes similar for salvage vs amputation in selected severe lower extremity injuries
  • Patient + multidisciplinary input
  • Quality of life considerations Vascular injury (IIIC) — vascular repair priority; shunt for distal perfusion while orthopedic stabilization, then definitive bypass. Long-term outcomes: pain, function, return to work, psychological all important; rehabilitation + multidisciplinary; chronic osteomyelitis significant risk despite optimal management; PTSD common in major extremity injury patients (especially combat-related).
reference_source: EAST Open Fracture Guidelines; LEAP Study; Bhandari M et al. NEJM 2015 (FLOW)

  • II: wound > 1 cm without extensive soft tissue damage
  • IIIA: extensive soft tissue but coverage possible
  • IIIB: extensive soft tissue loss + bone exposure requiring coverage (flap)
  • IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair

scenario:
Clinical: surgical infection — necrotizing pancreatitis evolution

คำถาม: walled-off pancreatic necrosis management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Walled-off pancreatic necrosis (WOPN) — step-up approach (PANTER paradigm)

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Walled-off pancreatic necrosis (WOPN) — step-up approach (PANTER paradigm):

(1) Wait for walled-off necrosis — typically 4+ weeks from onset of pancreatitis:
  • WOPN is encapsulated collection of necrotic tissue + fluid
  • Allows safer intervention than early intervention (less bleeding + sepsis);

(2) Infected vs sterile necrosis:
  • Sterile necrosis: no intervention unless symptomatic / complications (gastric outlet obstruction, biliary obstruction, persistent symptoms, expanding)
  • Infected necrosis: needs intervention; signs — clinical decline, gas in necrosis on CT, positive FNA culture, persistent organ dysfunction;

(3) Step-up approach (PANTER trial NEJM 2010 — van Santvoort):
  • Step 1: percutaneous catheter drainage (PCD) — IR-guided through retroperitoneum (left flank preferred — avoids contamination + facilitates later sinus tract for endoscopic/minimally invasive necrosectomy)
  • Step 2: minimally invasive necrosectomy if step 1 inadequate at 4-7 days:
  • Video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement (VARD)
  • Endoscopic transgastric necrosectomy (multiple sessions; cystgastrostomy + repeated debridement)
  • Laparoscopic transgastric/transmesenteric
  • Sinus tract endoscopy via PCD tract
  • Step 3: open necrosectomy — last resort for failed minimally invasive (rare in modern era);

(4) Endoscopic approach (TENSION trial Lancet 2018) — endoscopic step-up: lumen-apposing metal stent (LAMS — Hot AXIOS) cystgastrostomy → DEN (direct endoscopic necrosectomy) sessions; comparable outcomes to surgical step-up with less new-onset organ failure; growing preferred approach for WOPN amenable;

(5) Modern combined / hybrid approaches:
  • Dual modality (endo + percutaneous) for complex disease
  • Tailored by anatomy + location of necrosis
  • Multidisciplinary team essential;

(6) Indications for intervention (not all necrosis needs it):
  • Infected necrosis (clinical, gas on CT, positive FNA)
  • Persistent symptoms (failure to progress with diet, persistent pain, GOO)
  • Complications (bleeding from pseudoaneurysm — IR embolization first; mass effect on adjacent organs)
  • Sterile necrosis usually NOT intervened (improved with conservative + supportive);

(7) Adjuncts:
  • Nutritional support — early enteral (NJ or NG); TPN if not feasible
  • Pain management
  • Pancreatic enzyme replacement if EPI develops
  • Treat underlying (gallstone — cholecystectomy + ERCP, alcohol — cessation)
  • Glycemic control
  • Antibiotic if infected — broad-spectrum + culture-directed (carbapenem penetrates pancreatic tissue well);

(8) Outcomes: step-up approach reduces new organ failure + mortality (~ 16% vs 31% open in PANTER) + LOS + costs; endoscopic step-up further reduces new organ failure;

(9) Long-term: pancreatic insufficiency (exocrine + endocrine) common; PERT + DM management; survivorship care including imaging surveillance for pancreatic cancer (slightly elevated risk in chronic pancreatitis)
————————————————
Walled-off Pancreatic Necrosis (WOPN) — late complication of necrotizing pancreatitis; encapsulated collection of necrosis + fluid; typically forms by 4+ weeks. Distinction from acute necrotic collection (ANC, < 4 weeks) + pseudocyst (no solid necrotic content) + acute peripancreatic fluid collection (APFC). Atlanta 2012 classification. Management paradigm shift — from early aggressive open necrosectomy (high mortality ~ 30-50%) → delayed step-up approach (lower mortality ~ 5-20% modern series). PANTER trial (van Santvoort et al, NEJM 2010) — landmark:
  • Step-up: PCD → MIS necrosectomy as needed
  • Open: traditional approach
  • Results: step-up reduced new-onset multi-organ failure + complications (without mortality difference; subsequent updates suggest mortality benefit); set standard for management TENSION trial (van Brunschot et al, Lancet 2018) — endoscopic step-up vs surgical step-up:
  • Endoscopic step-up (LAMS cystgastrostomy + DEN) reduced new organ failure (vs surgical step-up)
  • Similar mortality
  • Less pancreatic fistulas
  • Less ICU stay
  • Endoscopic approach growing in centers with expertise Step-up approach steps:

1. Wait for walled-off (4+ weeks) unless infected + decompensating

2. Percutaneous drainage first — IR-guided; retroperitoneal route preferred (left flank — avoids peritoneal contamination + creates sinus tract for later interventions)

3. Antibiotic if infected (carbapenem — meropenem, imipenem — penetrate pancreatic tissue well; fluoroquinolone alternative)

4. Minimally invasive necrosectomy if percutaneous inadequate at 4-7 days:
  • VARD (video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement)
  • Endoscopic transgastric (LAMS cystgastrostomy + DEN)
  • Laparoscopic transgastric
  • Sinus tract endoscopy

5. Open necrosectomy — last resort; rare in modern centers Indications for intervention:

1. Infected necrosis (clinical, gas on CT, positive FNA)

2. Persistent symptoms (pain, GOO, failure to progress)

3. Complications (bleeding pseudoaneurysm — IR embolization first; mass effect) Sterile necrosis usually NOT intervened — better with supportive. Bleeding pseudoaneurysm — major mortality contributor in pancreatitis: visceral aneurysm in inflamed area; IR angio + embolization first-line; surgery if failed. Long-term sequelae:
  • Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency — PERT
  • Pancreatic endocrine (type 3c DM)
  • Pseudocyst (residual)
  • Chronic pancreatitis
  • Strictures (biliary, GI)
  • Pancreatic cancer slightly elevated risk
  • Quality of life impact substantial.
reference_source: PANTER van Santvoort HC et al. NEJM 2010; TENSION van Brunschot S et al. Lancet 2018; WSES Severe Acute Pancreatitis 2019

Walled-off pancreatic necrosis (WOPN) — step-up approach (PANTER paradigm):

(1) Wait for walled-off necrosis — typically 4+ weeks from onset of pancreatitis:
  • WOPN is encapsulated collection of necrotic tissue + fluid
  • Allows safer intervention than early intervention (less bleeding + sepsis)

  • Step-up: PCD → MIS necrosectomy as needed
  • Open: traditional approach
  • Results: step-up reduced new-onset multi-organ failure + complications (without mortality difference; subsequent updates suggest mortality benefit); set standard for management TENSION trial (van Brunschot et al, Lancet 2018) — endoscopic step-up vs surgical step-up:
  • Endoscopic step-up (LAMS cystgastrostomy + DEN) reduced new organ failure (vs surgical step-up)
  • Similar mortality
  • Less pancreatic fistulas
  • Less ICU stay
  • Endoscopic approach growing in centers with expertise Step-up approach steps:

1. Wait for walled-off (4+ weeks) unless infected + decompensating

2. Percutaneous drainage first — IR-guided; retroperitoneal route preferred (left flank — avoids peritoneal contamination + creates sinus tract for later interventions)

3. Antibiotic if infected (carbapenem — meropenem, imipenem — penetrate pancreatic tissue well; fluoroquinolone alternative)

4. Minimally invasive necrosectomy if percutaneous inadequate at 4-7 days:
  • VARD (video-assisted retroperitoneal debridement)
  • Endoscopic transgastric (LAMS cystgastrostomy + DEN)
  • Laparoscopic transgastric
  • Sinus tract endoscopy

5. Open necrosectomy — last resort; rare in modern centers Indications for intervention:

1. Infected necrosis (clinical, gas on CT, positive FNA)

2. Persistent symptoms (pain, GOO, failure to progress)

3. Complications (bleeding pseudoaneurysm — IR embolization first; mass effect) Sterile necrosis usually NOT intervened — better with supportive. Bleeding pseudoaneurysm — major mortality contributor in pancreatitis: visceral aneurysm in inflamed area; IR angio + embolization first-line; surgery if failed. Long-term sequelae:
  • Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency — PERT
  • Pancreatic endocrine (type 3c DM)
  • Pseudocyst (residual)
  • Chronic pancreatitis
  • Strictures (biliary, GI)
  • Pancreatic cancer slightly elevated risk
  • Quality of life impact substantial.

scenario:
Final integrative: complex multimorbid patient + advance care planning

คำถาม: long-term complex patient management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Long-term complex multimorbid patient — integrative care principles

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Long-term complex multimorbid patient — integrative care principles:

(1) Patient-centered approach:
  • Understand patient as person — values, goals, preferences, family, cultural, spiritual background - 'What matters to you?' alongside 'What's the matter with you?'
  • Living well with chronic conditions vs disease-by-disease focus;

(2) Care coordination:
  • Primary care medical home model
  • Care manager / patient navigator role
  • Coordinated multidisciplinary specialty input
  • Shared electronic health record
  • Transitions of care (hospital → home → other) protocols;

(3) Polypharmacy review ongoing:
  • Beers Criteria, STOPP/START
  • Deprescribe inappropriate
  • Simplify regimens
  • Adherence support;

(4) Address each chronic condition evidence-based:
  • Cardiovascular: BP, lipid, antiplatelet, statin
  • Diabetes: A1c, foot, eye, renal
  • CKD: progression, dialysis preparation if advanced
  • HF: GDMT (guideline-directed medical therapy)
  • COPD: smoking cessation, inhaler, pulm rehab, vaccinations
  • Mental health: depression, anxiety screening + treatment
  • Cancer surveillance (age + history-appropriate)
  • Bone health: DEXA, fall prevention;

(5) Functional assessment + maintenance:
  • ADL/IADL
  • Mobility, balance, fall risk
  • Cognitive screening (annual for elderly)
  • Sensory (vision, hearing)
  • Dental + nutrition;

(6) Preventive care:
  • Vaccinations (annual influenza, COVID, pneumococcal, RSV elderly, herpes zoster, Tdap, HPV catch-up)
  • Cancer screening risk-based
  • Lifestyle counseling
  • Substance use screening + treatment;

(7) Advance care planning as ongoing conversation:
  • Healthcare proxy designation
  • Living will / advance directive
  • POLST / MOLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment)
  • Goals of care discussion + documentation
  • Revisit periodically + when health status changes
  • Cultural + religious considerations
  • Family involvement appropriately;

(8) Palliative care integration — not just end-of-life:
  • Symptom management
  • Quality of life focus
  • Family support
  • May coexist with disease-modifying treatment
  • Concurrent care benefits (Temel + others);

(9) Caregiver support:
  • Burden assessment
  • Respite
  • Education
  • Mental health
  • Financial + legal counseling
  • Community resources;

(10) Equity + access:
  • Social determinants of health (housing, food, employment, transportation, social support)
  • Health literacy
  • Language access
  • Cultural humility
  • Financial support resources
  • Telemedicine access;

(11) Patient + family education:
  • Disease + medication understanding
  • Self-management skills
  • Warning signs requiring care
  • Resources + support groups;

(12) Hospital + transition care:
  • Avoid unnecessary hospitalization (e.g., emergency department, observation alternatives)
  • Care coordination at transitions
  • Medication reconciliation
  • Follow-up appointments
  • Home health services as needed;

(13) End-of-life care:
  • Comfort + dignity
  • Symptom management
  • Family support
  • Hospice when appropriate
  • Bereavement support;

(14) Population health perspective: chronic disease management programs, registry-based outreach, quality improvement, value-based care models
————————————————
Long-term complex multimorbid patient management — challenging but central to modern medicine + surgery. Most adults have multiple chronic conditions; care should be integrated + patient-centered. Key principles:

1. Patient-centered: 'What matters to you?' (Berwick + others) — alongside 'What's the matter with you?' — frames care around patient goals + values

2. Multimorbidity-focused vs disease-by-disease — recognize interactions, prioritize, balance, individualize

3. Geriatric principles: comprehensive geriatric assessment (CGA), functional + cognitive emphasis, polypharmacy deprescribing, falls prevention, social context

4. Care coordination: primary care medical home, care manager, multidisciplinary teams, shared EHR, transitions of care protocols

5. Patient + family engagement: education, self-management, shared decision-making

6. Health equity: address social determinants (housing, food security, transportation, employment, social support), language access, health literacy, cultural humility

7. Advance care planning — ongoing, not single event; revisit with health changes; healthcare proxy, advance directive, POLST/MOLST

8. Palliative care integration — concurrent with disease-modifying treatment; not just end-of-life; symptom management + quality of life + family support

9. Caregiver support — major modifiable factor; burden assessment, respite, education, mental health, community resources Specific tools + approaches:
  • 5 Ms of geriatric care: Mind, Mobility, Medications, Multicomplexity, what Matters most
  • Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment
  • Deprescribing protocols (STOPP/START, Beers)
  • POLST / MOLST — actionable medical orders, portable across settings, complement advance directive
  • Healthcare proxy designation Models of care:
  • Patient-Centered Medical Home (PCMH)
  • Accountable Care Organizations (ACO)
  • PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) — frail elderly in community
  • Hospice + palliative care
  • Chronic care management programs End-of-life care — comfort, dignity, symptom management, family support, hospice when appropriate, bereavement support extending 12+ months. Quality measurement: patient experience (HCAHPS), patient-reported outcomes (PROMs), functional outcomes, advance care planning completion, caregiver burden, healthcare utilization. Equity — recognize disparities; provide language access, health literacy-appropriate education, address SDoH, cultural humility, advocate for policy changes. Workforce: interdisciplinary training, primary care + geriatrics + palliative care; nursing, social work, pharmacy, physical/occupational therapy, mental health all critical; team-based care.
reference_source: Berwick D — Patient-Centered Care; ACS Geriatric Surgery; AAHPM; American Geriatrics Society

scenario:
หญิงไทยอายุ 45 ปี post-open cholecystectomy 5 วันก่อน มา ED ด้วยอาการ jaundice + ปวด RUQ + ไข้ + อาเจียน

VS: BP 102/64, HR 116, Temp 38.6, jaundice prominent
Lab: Total bili 8.4, Direct 7.2, ALP 480, AST 220, ALT 168, WBC 17,200, CRP 220
MRCP: complete transection CBD at hepatic hilum + biloma + dilated intrahepatic ducts (Strasberg E2 / Bismuth III injury)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Major iatrogenic bile duct injury (Strasberg E2 / Bismuth III) — emergent + definitive management

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Major iatrogenic bile duct injury (Strasberg E2 / Bismuth III) — emergent + definitive management:

(1) Initial: IV broad-spectrum antibiotic (covering biliary flora — pip-tazo OR ceftriaxone + metronidazole), resuscitation, sepsis management;

(2) Source control:
  • Percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) of dilated intrahepatic ducts — relieves obstruction + treats cholangitis; bilateral if needed
  • Drainage of biloma percutaneously (IR)
  • NPO + nutritional support;

(3) Definitive reconstruction — referral to high-volume HPB center (better outcomes per literature):
  • Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy — gold standard for major proximal bile duct injuries
  • Timing: ideally delayed 6-8 weeks for inflammation to subside + adequate drainage; immediate only in unstable / unable to drain
  • Identify proximal healthy duct + bring up Roux limb 60 cm + mucosa-to-mucosa anastomosis;

(4) End-to-end repair — only feasible for short segment defects with minimal tissue loss + no inflammation; high stricture rate; avoid in major proximal injuries;

(5) Strasberg-Bismuth classification:
  • A: cystic duct stump leak
  • B: aberrant right duct ligated
  • C: aberrant right duct leak (open)
  • D: lateral CBD injury
  • E1-E5: hilar/bifurcation involvement (E1 distal, E5 right hepatic sectoral involvement separate from main hilum)
  • Higher level = more complex repair;

(6) Outcomes: best at high-volume HPB centers; stricture + recurrent cholangitis remain common long-term; 5-yr patency after HJ ~ 75-90% in expert centers;

(7) Long-term: surveillance for stricture (LFT, MRCP), portal HTN/biliary cirrhosis if chronic obstruction, recurrent cholangitis;

(8) Legal + disclosure: open communication with patient + family per principles of open disclosure
————————————————
Major iatrogenic bile duct injury — devastating complication of cholecystectomy (laparoscopic 0.3-0.6%, open 0.1-0.2%). Strasberg-Bismuth Classification:
  • A: cystic duct/duct of Luschka leak — ERCP + stent + drain
  • B: aberrant right hepatic duct ligation — atrophy, may not need intervention
  • C: aberrant right hepatic duct leak — drain + reconstruction
  • D: lateral CBD injury — primary repair over T-tube if early + clean
  • E1: CBD > 2 cm from confluence
  • E2: CBD < 2 cm from confluence
  • E3: at confluence (right + left hepatic intact)
  • E4: at confluence with destruction (right + left separate)
  • E5: aberrant right sectoral + main CBD Recognition:
  • Intra-op (~ 30%) — best outcomes if recognized + repaired by HPB surgeon
  • Early post-op (days-weeks) — bile leak, biloma, peritonitis, cholangitis, jaundice
  • Late (months-years) — stricture, recurrent cholangitis, biliary cirrhosis Management principles:

1. Multidisciplinary — refer to high-volume HPB center; outcomes substantially better than general surgery center

2. Source control first — biliary decompression (PTBD or ERCP-stent if amenable), drain biliary collections (biloma), antibiotic for cholangitis

3. Definitive reconstruction:
  • Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy — gold standard for E1-E5;
  • End-to-end repair only for short clean defects (rare with major injuries);
  • Timing: typically delayed 6-8 weeks for inflammation to resolve + adequate drainage of biliary tree + nutritional optimization; immediate only in extenuating circumstances

4. Surgical technique: identify healthy proximal duct, transect at level of viable mucosa, 60 cm Roux limb (no reflux), mucosa-to-mucosa anastomosis with absorbable suture, transanastomotic stent (controversial — not universal), preserve duct blood supply Outcomes at high-volume HPB centers:
  • Primary patency 80-90% at 5 years
  • Secondary intervention (balloon dilation, restenting) for late strictures
  • Recurrent cholangitis 10-20%
  • Quality of life significantly improved with appropriate management vs untreated Strasberg + Critical View of Safety — best practice for cholecystectomy to prevent BDI: clear identification of cystic duct + cystic artery, only 2 structures entering gallbladder, before clipping or transection.
reference_source: Strasberg SM et al. J Am Coll Surg; SAGES Safe Cholecystectomy Program; Jablonska B + Lampe P World J Gastroenterol

scenario:
Basic science: surgical pathology + frozen section

คำถาม: intraoperative frozen section + pathology

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intraoperative pathology + frozen section — surgical decision-making tool

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Intraoperative pathology + frozen section — surgical decision-making tool:

(1) Frozen section:
  • Rapid tissue examination (~ 20-30 min) during operation
  • Cryostat freezing + H&E stain
  • Pathologist reads + reports immediately
  • Guides surgical decision in real-time;

(2) Common indications:
  • Confirm malignancy before extensive resection
  • Assess margin status (e.g., breast lumpectomy, Mohs, brain tumor, soft tissue sarcoma)
  • Identify sentinel lymph node positivity — though less common now (postpone definitive axillary decision often)
  • Identify tissue origin (parathyroid vs lymph node vs thyroid during neck exploration)
  • Assess for invasion (DCIS vs invasive, follicular lesion of thyroid)
  • Identify foreign body or unexpected findings;

(3) Limitations of frozen section:
  • Less detail than permanent (formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded — FFPE)
  • Limited tissue sampling (small piece may miss disease)
  • Some tissue types difficult on frozen (lipoma vs liposarcoma, follicular thyroid lesion — cannot distinguish benign follicular adenoma from carcinoma on frozen; requires capsular/vascular invasion assessment best on permanent)
  • Freezing artifact
  • Some immunohistochemistry not available on frozen
  • Higher discordance with permanent in difficult cases (~ 1-3%);

(4) Specific applications: a. Breast surgery:
  • Margin assessment (frozen + 'shave' margins)
  • SLN biopsy (some centers — less common now post Z0011)
  • Touch prep / imprint cytology adjunct b. Thyroid surgery:
  • Confirm malignancy if not pre-confirmed (uncommon — usually FNA Bethesda guides)
  • LN involvement c. Mohs micrographic surgery:
  • Skin cancer (BCC, SCC, melanoma in situ selected)
  • Real-time margin assessment + sequential excision until clear d. Lung surgery:
  • Wedge biopsy → frozen → decide lobectomy vs further if cancer e. Brain surgery:
  • Tumor identification + grading f. Sarcoma:
  • Margin assessment
  • Tumor identification g. Lymph node:
  • Lymphoma vs metastatic carcinoma h. Parathyroid surgery:
  • Confirm parathyroid tissue (vs lymph node, thyroid, fat)
  • Intraoperative PTH adjunct;

(5) Permanent / final pathology — gold standard:
  • Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE)
  • More accurate, comprehensive evaluation
  • Standard H&E + immunohistochemistry + special stains
  • Molecular testing (NGS, FISH, IHC for predictive markers)
  • Reported within days-weeks
  • Used for: comprehensive diagnosis, prognosis, biomarkers, treatment decisions;

(6) Communication with pathologist essential:
  • Provide clinical context
  • Specific question to answer
  • Collaborative decision-making
  • Multidisciplinary tumor board for complex;

(7) Tissue handling principles:
  • Proper labeling + orientation (sutures, ink)
  • Avoid crush + thermal injury
  • Send fresh for frozen if needed; otherwise formalin
  • Special handling: lymphoma flow cytometry (fresh tissue), microbiologic, electron microscopy;

(8) Molecular pathology + companion diagnostics — increasing role:
  • Tumor genotyping guides targeted therapy
  • Liquid biopsy emerging
  • Predictive biomarkers (ER/PR/HER2 breast, EGFR/ALK/PD-L1 lung, MSI/dMMR, BRCA, etc.)
————————————————
Intraoperative pathology + frozen section — important surgical decision-making tool. Frozen section process:
  • Surgeon submits tissue (fresh, properly oriented)
  • Cryostat freezing (~ 5 min)
  • Sectioning + H&E staining
  • Microscopic interpretation by pathologist
  • Verbal report to surgeon (typically 15-30 min total)
  • Decision made intraoperatively Common surgical applications:

1. Margin assessment — breast (lumpectomy, post-mastectomy), brain tumors, sarcoma, head/neck, GU (prostatectomy)

2. Diagnosis confirmation before extensive surgery (e.g., presumed primary lung Ca on biopsy → wedge resection → frozen confirms → lobectomy + lymphadenectomy)

3. Sentinel lymph node — historically; declining role post-ACOSOG Z0011 (axillary dissection not always needed for 1-2 positive SLN in breast Ca with BCS + RT)

4. Tissue identification — parathyroidectomy (confirm parathyroid vs LN/thyroid/fat)

5. Mohs surgery — skin cancer real-time margin assessment Limitations:
  • Less detail than permanent (frozen vs FFPE)
  • Sampling limitations
  • Difficult tissues: lipoma vs liposarcoma, follicular thyroid neoplasm (cannot reliably distinguish benign vs malignant on frozen — requires capsular/vascular invasion assessment best on permanent)
  • Freezing artifact
  • Limited IHC + molecular
  • Discordance with permanent ~ 1-3% (false-positive + false-negative both occur) Communication with pathologist key:
  • Clinical context + specific question
  • Multidisciplinary input on difficult cases
  • Defer to permanent if uncertain on frozen (often appropriate) Tissue handling:
  • Orientation (ink colors, sutures for margins)
  • Avoid crush + thermal damage
  • Fresh for frozen + microbiology + flow cytometry (lymphoma); formalin for permanent
  • Special: electron microscopy, molecular Molecular pathology + companion diagnostics — increasing role in surgical pathology:
  • NGS (next-generation sequencing) panels — tumor genotyping
  • IHC for predictive markers (ER/PR/HER2 breast; PD-L1 lung; HER2 gastric; ER+ endometrial; etc.)
  • FISH (HER2 amplification, ALK rearrangement, others)
  • Liquid biopsy (ctDNA, CTCs) — minimal residual disease, recurrence monitoring, resistance mutations
  • MSI / dMMR — colorectal + others; immunotherapy candidacy + Lynch screening
  • HRD (homologous recombination deficiency) — PARP inhibitor predictive (ovarian, breast, prostate) Reporting standards: synoptic reporting (CAP — College of American Pathologists protocols) ensures all critical information captured + standardized; improves communication + decision-making.
reference_source: CAP (College of American Pathologists) Protocols; WHO Tumor Classification; ASCO/CAP Guidelines

  • Pathologist reads + reports immediately

  • Decision made intraoperatively Common surgical applications:

1. Margin assessment — breast (lumpectomy, post-mastectomy), brain tumors, sarcoma, head/neck, GU (prostatectomy)

2. Diagnosis confirmation before extensive surgery (e.g., presumed primary lung Ca on biopsy → wedge resection → frozen confirms → lobectomy + lymphadenectomy)

3. Sentinel lymph node — historically; declining role post-ACOSOG Z0011 (axillary dissection not always needed for 1-2 positive SLN in breast Ca with BCS + RT)

4. Tissue identification — parathyroidectomy (confirm parathyroid vs LN/thyroid/fat)

5. Mohs surgery — skin cancer real-time margin assessment Limitations:
  • Less detail than permanent (frozen vs FFPE)
  • Sampling limitations
  • Difficult tissues: lipoma vs liposarcoma, follicular thyroid neoplasm (cannot reliably distinguish benign vs malignant on frozen — requires capsular/vascular invasion assessment best on permanent)
  • Freezing artifact
  • Limited IHC + molecular
  • Discordance with permanent ~ 1-3% (false-positive + false-negative both occur) Communication with pathologist key:
  • Clinical context + specific question
  • Multidisciplinary input on difficult cases
  • Defer to permanent if uncertain on frozen (often appropriate) Tissue handling:
  • Orientation (ink colors, sutures for margins)
  • Avoid crush + thermal damage
  • Fresh for frozen + microbiology + flow cytometry (lymphoma); formalin for permanent
  • Special: electron microscopy, molecular Molecular pathology + companion diagnostics — increasing role in surgical pathology:
  • NGS (next-generation sequencing) panels — tumor genotyping
  • IHC for predictive markers (ER/PR/HER2 breast; PD-L1 lung; HER2 gastric; ER+ endometrial; etc.)
  • FISH (HER2 amplification, ALK rearrangement, others)
  • Liquid biopsy (ctDNA, CTCs) — minimal residual disease, recurrence monitoring, resistance mutations
  • MSI / dMMR — colorectal + others; immunotherapy candidacy + Lynch screening
  • HRD (homologous recombination deficiency) — PARP inhibitor predictive (ovarian, breast, prostate) Reporting standards: synoptic reporting (CAP — College of American Pathologists protocols) ensures all critical information captured + standardized; improves communication + decision-making.

scenario:
Final integrative: telecare + community surgical care

คำถาม: equitable surgical care + value-based care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Equitable surgical care + value-based care — system-level approach

explanation:
ตัวเลือก B (รายละเอียด)

Equitable surgical care + value-based care — system-level approach:

(1) Equity in surgical care — disparities + interventions:
  • Racial/ethnic disparities: variable surgical access + outcomes documented in many studies
  • Socioeconomic disparities: insurance status, income affect access + outcomes
  • Geographic disparities: rural < urban access; LMIC global gap (Lancet Commission)
  • Gender disparities: women under-recruited for some surgeries (cardiac), differences in pain management
  • Age: under- + over-treatment at extremes
  • Sexual orientation/gender identity: barriers to care, discrimination
  • Disability: physical accommodation, communication barriers
  • Mental illness / substance use: stigma + barriers
  • Refugee/immigrant: language, cultural, documentation, payment barriers;

(2) Interventions for equity:
  • Cultural humility + competency training
  • Language access (medical interpreters, translated materials)
  • Health literacy-appropriate communication
  • Implicit bias training
  • Diverse workforce representing patient population
  • Community engagement + partnerships
  • Patient navigators for vulnerable populations
  • Sliding scale + charity care programs
  • Transportation + housing + food support during care
  • Telemedicine to extend reach (with digital divide consideration)
  • Policy advocacy for coverage + access;

(3) Value-based care models:
  • Shift from volume-based (fee-for-service) to value (outcomes + experience + cost)
  • Accountable Care Organizations (ACO)
  • Bundled payments for episodes of care (joint replacement, cardiac surgery, oncology)
  • Population health management
  • Pay-for-performance incentives
  • Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) integration
  • Risk-sharing arrangements
  • Total cost of care focus;

(4) Quality measurement — drives improvement:
  • Mortality + morbidity
  • Patient-reported outcomes (functional, quality of life)
  • Patient experience (HCAHPS, surgical patient experience)
  • Process measures (SCIP, WHO checklist)
  • Equity metrics (stratified outcomes by demographics)
  • Cost-effectiveness;

(5) Population health approach:
  • Address social determinants
  • Preventive care emphasis
  • Chronic disease management
  • Risk stratification + targeted interventions
  • Behavioral economics nudges;

(6) Global surgery (see prior question on Lancet Commission):
  • 5 billion people lack access to safe surgical care
  • Bellwether procedures
  • SAO workforce density
  • Surgical volume
  • Catastrophic expenditure protection
  • Bidirectional partnerships
  • Capacity building (vs medical tourism / unidirectional aid);

(7) Patient-centered care — patient as partner in care:
  • Shared decision-making
  • Care plans aligned with patient goals
  • Whole-person care
  • Family + caregiver inclusion - 'What matters to you?' approach;

(8) Quality improvement infrastructure:
  • Surgical registries (NSQIP, STS, MBSAQIP, etc.)
  • Internal quality programs
  • Audit + feedback
  • Learning health systems
  • Just culture
  • Continuous improvement methodology (lean, six sigma, PDSA);

(9) Technology to enhance equity:
  • Telemedicine (with attention to digital divide)
  • Remote monitoring
  • AI clinical decision support
  • EHR integration
  • Patient portals
  • Health information exchange;

(10) Workforce development: diverse pipeline, residency training in equity + advocacy + leadership, lifelong learning, wellness;

(11) Healthcare advocacy + policy engagement — physicians as advocates for patients + system improvement; SDoH, insurance, pharmaceutical pricing, regulation, public health;

(12) Sustainability + climate: healthcare contributes significantly to greenhouse emissions; sustainable surgery initiatives — reusable instruments where appropriate, energy efficiency, supply chain, waste reduction (Stanford GreenER OR + others)
————————————————
Equitable surgical care + value-based care — modern imperatives in surgery + medicine. Health disparities + equity: substantial documented disparities in surgical care access + outcomes by race, ethnicity, SES, geography, gender, age, disability, sexual/gender minority status. Multiple drivers — system, provider, patient factors. Interventions for equity (multi-level):

1. Workforce: diverse training, recruitment, mentorship, sponsorship for underrepresented groups

2. Cultural humility + competence: ongoing training, recognition of bias

3. Language + literacy: interpreters, plain language materials, translated resources

4. Patient navigators for vulnerable populations

5. Telemedicine with attention to digital divide

6. SDoH (social determinants of health) integration: housing, food, transportation, employment
  • screening + connection to resources

7. Community engagement + partnerships

8. Policy advocacy Value-based care — shift from fee-for-service (volume) to value (outcomes + experience ÷ cost):
  • ACO (Accountable Care Organizations)
  • Bundled payments for episodes (e.g., CJR — Comprehensive Care for Joint Replacement, BPCI — Bundled Payments for Care Improvement, OCM — Oncology Care Model)
  • Pay-for-performance
  • MIPS (Merit-based Incentive Payment System)
  • Total cost of care focus
  • Risk-sharing arrangements (upside + downside) Quality measurement — drives improvement:
  • Outcome measures: mortality, complications, readmission, PROs
  • Process measures: SCIP, antibiotic timing, VTE prophylaxis
  • Patient experience: HCAHPS, surgery-specific
  • Equity metrics: stratified analysis
  • Cost-effectiveness: ICER, quality-adjusted life years (QALY) Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) — increasingly central:
  • Quality of life
  • Functional outcomes
  • Symptom burden
  • Satisfaction
  • Standardized tools (PROMIS, ePRO platforms) Global surgery + Lancet Commission (Meara 2015):
  • 5 billion lack access to safe surgical care
  • 143 million additional procedures needed annually
  • Surgery essential to UHC
  • Bellwether procedures (cesarean, laparotomy, open fracture)
  • SAO workforce density indicator
  • Catastrophic expenditure protection Sustainability + climate — healthcare ~ 5-10% of greenhouse emissions; OR major contributor; initiatives:
  • Reusable vs single-use
  • Energy-efficient OR design
  • Supply chain optimization
  • Anesthetic gas selection (desflurane high GWP — minimize; sevoflurane lower; TIVA alternative)
  • Recycling + waste reduction
  • Telemedicine reduces travel emissions Stanford GreenER OR + other institutional initiatives demonstrating feasibility + cost savings + environmental benefit. Physician advocacy — professional responsibility to advocate for patients + system improvement; SDoH, insurance reform, pharmaceutical pricing, regulation, public health, climate.
reference_source: Lancet Commission on Global Surgery 2015; IOM Crossing the Quality Chasm; ACS Strong for Surgery; National Academy of Medicine
